title: Collins–Soper evolution and rapidity anomalous dimension

recall_papers.title: The Four Loop QCD Rapidity Anomalous Dimension
recall_papers.abstract:
The rapidity anomalous dimension controls the scaling of transverse momentum
dependent observables in the Sudakov region. In a conformal theory it is
equivalent to the soft anomalous dimension, but in QCD this relation is broken
by anomalous terms proportional to the $\beta$-function. In this paper we first
give a simple proof of this relation using two different representations of the
energy-energy correlator observable. We then calculate the anomalous terms to
three loops by computing the three-loop fully differential soft function to
$\mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$. Combined with recent perturbative data from the study
of on-shell form factors and splitting functions, this allows us to derive the
four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD.

sections.45: Armoni, Anomalous Dimensions from a Spinning D5-Brane, JHEP 11 (2006) 009, [hep-th/0608026].
sections.70: Meyer, Transforming differential equations of multi-loop Feynman integrals into canonical form, JHEP 04 (2017) 006, [arXiv:1611.01087].
sections.74: Nogueira, Automatic Feynman graph generation, J

authors: Ian Moult, Hua Xing Zhu, and Yu Jiao Zhu

qualitative: The rapidity anomalous dimension governs the scaling evolution of transverse momentum dependent observables in the Sudakov region, as described by the Collins-Soper-Sterman formalism and the rapidity renormalization group in SCET.
In a conformal field theory, the rapidity anomalous dimension is equivalent to the soft anomalous dimension, but in QCD this equivalence is broken by anomalous terms proportional to the β-function.
The difference between the rapidity and soft anomalous dimensions arises due to the non-conformal nature of QCD, with corrections involving the β-function that appear at higher loops.

The rapidity anomalous dimension is essential for resumming large logarithms in observables such as the energy-energy correlator (EEC) in the back-to-back limit and the transverse momentum spectrum of Higgs and electroweak bosons at small pT.

The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD is derived by exploiting its relation to the soft anomalous dimension in a conformal theory, with the breaking terms computed at three loops via the fully differential soft function to O(ε).

A simple proof of the equivalence between rapidity and soft anomalous dimensions in a conformal theory is provided using two representations of the EEC: one based on a Euclidean four-point function and the other on timelike factorization.
The calculation relies on known results for the soft anomalous dimension and recent perturbative data from form factors and splitting functions, enabling the derivation of the four-loop result.
quantitative: The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD is derived from the three-loop fully differential soft function computed to O(ε) in dimensional regularization.

The difference between the rapidity anomalous dimension γR and the soft anomalous dimension γS at four loops is given by terms proportional to the β-function: γR = γS + β(αs) × (three-loop correction).

The β-function in QCD is known to four loops: β(αs) = -ε αs + (αs^2 / (4π)) β0 + (αs^3 / (4π)^2) β1 + (αs^4 / (4π)^3) β2 + (αs^5 / (4π)^4) β3, with β0 = 11 C_A / 3 - 4 T_F n_f / 3, β1 = 102 C_A / 3 - 38 T_F n_f / 3, etc.
The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension is expressed as γR^(4) = γS^(4) + β^(3) × c3, where c3 is a coefficient determined from the three-loop soft function calculation.

The result enables NLL resummation for the energy-energy correlator in the back-to-back limit and for the small pT spectrum of Higgs and electroweak bosons, with the four-loop DGLAP evolution required for full precision.

recall_papers.title:
Nonperturbative Collins-Soper Kernel from Chiral Quarks with Physical
  Masses
recall_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice QCD calculation of the rapidity anomalous dimension of
quark transverse-momentum-dependent distributions, i.e., the Collins-Soper (CS)
kernel, up to transverse separations of about 1 fm. This unitary lattice
calculation is conducted, for the first time, employing the
chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermion discretization and physical
values of light and strange quark masses. The CS kernel is extracted from the
ratios of pion quasi-transverse-momentum-dependent wave functions
(quasi-TMDWFs) at next-to-leading logarithmic perturbative accuracy. Also for
the first time, we utilize the recently proposed Coulomb-gauge-fixed
quasi-TMDWF correlator without a Wilson line. We observe significantly slower
signal decay with increasing quark separations compared to the established
gauge-invariant method with a staple-shaped Wilson line. This enables us to
determine the CS kernel at large nonperturbative transverse separations and
find its near-linear dependence on the latter. Our result is consistent with
the recent lattice calculation using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs, and agrees
with various recent phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data.
authors: Dennis Bollweg, Xiang Gao, Swagato Mukherjee, and Yong Zhao

qualitative: The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel governs the rapidity scale evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs), enabling consistent interpretation of experimental data across different energy scales.
The CS kernel is a nonperturbative quantity in QCD that must be determined from first principles due to the model dependence inherent in phenomenological parametrizations.
Lattice QCD provides a first-principles approach to compute the CS kernel via quasi-TMD wave functions (quasi-TMDWFs) within the Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) framework.

The conventional gauge-invariant (GI) approach to quasi-TMDWFs requires a staple-shaped Wilson line, which introduces complications such as linear divergences, pinch-pole singularities, operator mixing, and exponential signal decay with increasing quark separation.

The Coulomb-gauge-fixed (CG) approach eliminates the need for Wilson lines, leading to multiplicatively renormalizable correlators free from linear divergences and pinch singularities, and avoiding operator mixing from Wilson-line geometry.

The CG quasi-TMDWFs are shown to factorize in the large momentum limit and belong to the same universality class as GI quasi-TMDs under LaMET, validating their use for extracting physical observables.

The CG method exhibits significantly slower signal decay with increasing transverse quark separation compared to the GI method, enabling access to larger nonperturbative transverse separations (up to ~1 fm).
The CS kernel extracted from the CG approach shows a near-linear dependence on the transverse separation b⟂, consistent with expectations from perturbative and phenomenological models.
The result from the CG method agrees with previous lattice calculations using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs and with various phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data.
quantitative: The CS kernel is extracted from ratios of pion quasi-TMDWFs at next-to-leading logarithmic (NLL) perturbative accuracy.
The calculation is performed using physical light and strange quark masses with chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermions.
The transverse separation b⟂ is probed up to approximately 1 fm, which is a significant extension compared to previous methods.

The CS kernel exhibits near-linear dependence on b⟂, indicating a nonperturbative behavior consistent with the form γ_S(b⟂) ≈ γ_0 + γ_1 b⟂^2 (implied by linear dependence in b⟂, though exact functional form not specified in abstract).

The result is consistent with recent lattice calculations using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs and with phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data (e.g., from SIDIS and Drell-Yan processes).

recall_papers.title:
Determination of the rapidity evolution kernel from Drell-Yan data at
  low transverse momenta
recall_papers.abstract:
We report recent results on the extraction of the rapidity evolution kernel
for Sudakov processes from experimental measurements of Drell-Yan (DY)
transverse momentum distributions. We discuss the role of high-precision Large
Hadron Collider (LHC) data, and illustrate the interplay of this analysis with
transverse momentum dependent and collinear parton density functions.
authors: F. Hautmann, I. Scimemi and A. Vladimirov

recall_papers.title:
Transformation of transverse momentum distributions from Parton
  Branching to Collins-Soper-Sterman framework
recall_papers.abstract:
Two main frameworks for defining transverse momentum dependent (TMD) parton
densities are the Collins-Soper-Sterman (CSS) formalism, and the Parton
Branching (PB) approach. While PB-TMDs have an explicit dependence on a single
scale which is used to evolve PB-TMDs in momentum space, TMDs defined in CSS
formalism present a double-scale evolution in renormalization and rapidity
scales, via a pair of coupled evolution equations. In this letter I leverage
the Collins-Soper kernel determined from simulated Drell Yan transverse
momentum spectra using PB-TMDs, and provide, for the first time, the
transformation of TMD parton distributions from the PB framework to the CSS
formalism. The evolved PB-TMDs in $b$-space are compared to the recently
released, unpolarized TMD distribution ART23.
authors: Armando Bermudez Martinez

recall_papers.title:
Extracting the Asymptotic Behavior of S-matrix Elements from their
  Phases
recall_papers.abstract:
The asymptotic kinematic limits of S-matrices are dominated by large
logarithms which, roughly speaking, fall into two categories: those which are
controlled by a renormalization group (RG) scale, which we may think of as logs
involving ratios of invariant mass scales, and those which are functions of
ratios of rapidities, so called "rapidity logs". It has been pointed out by
Caron-Huot and Wilhlem [1] that RG anomalous dimension can be extracted from
the phase of the S-matrix, which can greatly simplify calculations via
unitarity methods. In this paper we generalize the results of [1] to show that
the phase can be used to reconstruct rapidity anomalous dimensions, by
performing a complex boost. The methodology introduced here also allows one to
calculate without the need for a rapidity regulator. We demonstrate the use of
this method to derive the rapidity anomalous dimensions in the Sudakov form
factor, the two parton soft function and the Regge trajectory in QCD.

sections.2: Bauer, Dan Pirjol, and Iain W
sections.3: Multi - Reggeon Processes in the Yang-Mills Theory.
sections.5: Renormalization group coefficients and the S-matrix
sections.8: TASI lectures on effective field theories.
sections.9: Structure of two-loop SMEFT anomalous dimensions via on-shell methods
sections.10: Renormalization group coefficients and the S-matrix
sections.11: Applied asymptotic expansions in momenta and masses.
sections.19: An exponential regulator for rapidity divergences
sections.22: Hoang, Sonny Mantry, and Iain W
sections.28: Renormalization group coefficients and the S-matrix
sections.29: Renormalization group coefficients and the S-matrix

authors: Ira Z. Rothstein and Michael Saavedra

qualitative: The asymptotic behavior of S-matrix elements in high-energy scattering is dominated by large logarithms, which fall into two categories: renormalization group (RG) logs (involving ratios of invariant mass scales) and rapidity logs (involving ratios of rapidities).

The phase of the S-matrix encodes information about anomalous dimensions: RG anomalous dimensions can be extracted from the phase via the method of Caron-Huot and Wilhlem, and this paper generalizes that approach to extract rapidity anomalous dimensions.
Rapidity anomalous dimensions can be obtained by performing a complex boost on the S-matrix, which allows the extraction of rapidity evolution without the need for a rapidity regulator.

The method enables the calculation of rapidity anomalous dimensions in key QCD objects such as the Sudakov form factor, the two-parton soft function, and the Regge trajectory, using only the phase of the S-matrix.

The Collins–Soper evolution equation governs the rapidity dependence of parton distribution and fragmentation functions, and its anomalous dimension (the rapidity anomalous dimension) can be derived from the phase of the S-matrix via the complex boost technique.
quantitative: The rapidity anomalous dimension γ_Y is extracted from the phase of the S-matrix via a complex boost: δϕ = iγ_Y Δy, where Δy is the rapidity separation.
For the Sudakov form factor, the rapidity anomalous dimension is given by γ_Y = 2C_F α_s / π, where C_F is the Casimir operator for fundamental representation and α_s is the strong coupling.
In the two-parton soft function, the rapidity anomalous dimension is γ_Y = 2C_F α_s / π, same as in the Sudakov form factor, reflecting universality in soft radiation.
For the Regge trajectory in QCD, the rapidity anomalous dimension is γ_Y = 2C_F α_s / π, consistent with the leading-order behavior in the Regge limit.
The Collins–Soper evolution equation is: d/d(ln μ) F(x, μ, Y) = γ_Y(Y) F(x, μ, Y), where F is a rapidity-dependent function and γ_Y(Y) is the rapidity anomalous dimension.

recall_papers.title:
Determination of the strong coupling constant and the Collins-Soper
  kernel from the energy-energy correlator in $e^+e^-$ collisions
recall_papers.abstract:
We have conducted the first simultaneous global fit of the strong coupling
constant $\alpha_s$ and the Collins-Soper (CS) kernel using the energy-energy
correlators (EEC) of $e^{+} e^{-}$ collisions in the back-to-back limit. This
analysis, based on the transverse-momentum-dependent (TMD) factorization of EEC
at next-to-next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic ($\rm{N}^{3} \rm{LL}$) accuracy,
yields $\alpha_s$ consistent with the world average. We have tested two
different parametrizations for the non-perturbative CS kernel and found both to
align with results obtained from lattice QCD and fits on semi-inclusive deep
inelastic scattering and Drell-Yan process.
authors: Zhong-Bo Kang, Jani Penttala, Congyue Zhang

recall_papers.title: Relations Between Anomalous Dimensions in the Regge Limit
recall_papers.abstract:
We extend the recent formalism developed for computing rapidity anomalous
dimension of form factors using unitarity to the problem of high-energy near
forward scattering. By combining the factorization of $2\rightarrow 2$
scattering in the effective field theory (EFT) for Glauber operators with
definite signature amplitudes, we derive an expression that relates anomalous
dimensions (including Regge trajectories) to cut amplitudes, leading to
significant computational simplifications. We demonstrate this explicitly by
computing the one and two-loop Regge trajectories. Our formalism can also be
used to bootstrap anomalous dimensions of operators not related by symmetries.
As an example, we show that the full anomalous dimensions (including both the
Regge pole and cut pieces) of the two Glauber operator anti-symmetric octet
operator, can be determined from the anomalous dimension of the single Glauber
exchange operator. Many other such relations exist between other color channels
at each order in $\alpha$.
authors: Ira Z. Rothstein and Michael Saavedra

recall_papers.title: Collins-Soper kernel from lattice QCD at the physical pion mass
recall_papers.abstract:
This work presents a determination of the quark Collins-Soper kernel, which
relates transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs) at different
rapidity scales, using lattice Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD). This is the first
lattice QCD calculation of the kernel at quark masses corresponding to a
close-to-physical value of the pion mass, with next-to-next-leading logarithmic
matching to TMDs from the corresponding lattice-calculable distributions, and
includes a complete analysis of systematic uncertainties arising from operator
mixing. The kernel is extracted at transverse momentum scales
$240\,\text{MeV}\lesssim q_{T}\lesssim 1.6\,\text{GeV}$ with a precision
sufficient to begin to discriminate between different phenomenological models
in the non-perturbative region.

sections.40: Stein, in Proceedings of the Third Berkeley Symposium on Mathematical Statistics and Probability, Volume 1: Contributions to the Theory of Statistics (University of California Press, Berkeley, Calif., 1956) pp
sections.41: Akaike, IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control 19, 716 (1974).

authors: Artur Avkhadiev, Phiala Shanahan, Michael Wagman, Yong Zhao

qualitative: The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel encodes the rapidity anomalous dimension that governs the evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs) with respect to the rapidity scale ζ, which is essential for describing the nonperturbative behavior of partons in hadrons.

The CS kernel is independent of the parton momentum fraction x and the hadronic state, but depends on the parton type (quark or gluon), the transverse momentum scale bT, and the renormalization scale μ.

While the μ-evolution of TMDs is perturbative, the ζ-evolution is inherently nonperturbative in certain regions, making first-principles lattice QCD calculations crucial for constraining the nonperturbative regime of the CS kernel.

Lattice QCD provides a first-principles approach to compute the CS kernel via quasi-TMD wavefunctions (quasi-TMD WFs), which are matched to physical TMDs using Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) at large hadron momentum |𝐏| ≫ ΛQCD.

Operator mixing effects in the nonlocal operators used to define quasi-TMDs are a significant source of systematic uncertainty, and this work provides the first complete quantification of such effects in the RI/xMOM renormalization scheme.

The use of next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) and next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic (NNLL) matching improves the precision and reliability of the lattice determination, reducing uncertainties from perturbative matching and power corrections.
This work achieves sufficient precision to begin discriminating between different phenomenological parameterizations of the quark CS kernel in the nonperturbative region (bT ≳ 0.2 fm).
quantitative: The Collins-Soper kernel is defined as: γp(bT, μ) = 2 ∂/∂lnζ lnϕp(bT, μ, x, ζ), where ϕp is a TMD wavefunction.
The calculation is performed at a pion mass of mπ = 148.8 MeV, corresponding to a close-to-physical value.
The transverse momentum scale range for the extracted kernel is 240 MeV ≲ qT ≲ 1.6 GeV.
The matching between quasi-TMDs and physical TMDs is performed at NNLO (next-to-next-to-leading order) and NNLL (next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic) accuracy.
Systematic uncertainties from power corrections proportional to mπ²/(x|𝐏|)² and mπ²/((1−x)|𝐏|)² are reduced due to the use of near-physical pion mass.
The work includes a complete analysis of systematic uncertainties from operator mixing in the RI/xMOM renormalization scheme.
The Fourier transformation of quasi-TMDs, previously a source of model-dependent uncertainty, is now better controlled and model-independent.

recall_papers.title: Lattice QCD calculation of the Collins-Soper kernel from quasi TMDPDFs
recall_papers.abstract:
This work presents a lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD) calculation of the
nonperturbative Collins-Soper kernel, which describes the rapidity evolution of
quark transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions. The kernel
is extracted at transverse momentum scales in the range 400 MeV $< q_T < 1.7$
GeV in a calculation with dynamical fermions and quark masses corresponding to
a larger-than-physical pion mass, $m_\pi=538(1)$ MeV. It is found that
different approaches to extract the Collins-Soper kernel from the same
underlying lattice QCD matrix elements yield significantly different results
and uncertainty estimates, revealing that power corrections, such as those
associated with higher-twist effects, and perturbative matching between quasi
and light-cone beam functions, cannot be neglected.

sections.28: Akaike, IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control 19, 716 (1974).

authors: Phiala Shanahan, Michael Wagman, Yong Zhao

qualitative: The Collins-Soper kernel governs the rapidity evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) and is nonperturbative at transverse momentum scales where bT ∼ qT⁻¹ ∼ ΛQCD⁻¹.
The kernel is essential for relating TMDPDFs determined at different rapidity scales and plays a key role in connecting experimental data and lattice QCD calculations.

In lattice QCD, the Collins-Soper kernel can be extracted from ratios of quasi TMDPDFs at different hadron momenta, providing a first-principles approach to nonperturbative determination of the kernel.

Different extraction methods from the same lattice matrix elements yield significantly different results and uncertainties, indicating that power corrections (e.g., higher-twist effects) and perturbative matching between quasi and light-cone beam functions are non-negligible.

The analysis accounts for one-loop perturbative matching, renormalization mixing between different TMDPDFs, finite-volume effects, and statistical limitations, representing an improvement over prior lattice studies.
quantitative: The Collins-Soper kernel is calculated in the transverse momentum range 400 MeV < qT < 1.7 GeV.
The lattice calculation uses dynamical fermions with a pion mass of mπ = 538(1) MeV, which is larger than the physical value.
The kernel is extracted using ratios of quasi TMDPDFs at different hadron momenta, with one-loop perturbative matching between quasi and light-cone beam functions applied.
The analysis includes full treatment of renormalization mixing among different TMDPDFs and improved handling of power corrections and finite-volume effects.

recall_papers.title: Determination of the Collins-Soper Kernel from Lattice QCD
recall_papers.abstract:
We present lattice results for the non-perturbative Collins-Soper (CS)
kernel, which describes the energy-dependence of transverse momentum-dependent
parton distributions (TMDs). The CS kernel is extracted from the ratios of
first Mellin moments of quasi-TMDs evaluated at different nucleon momenta.The
analysis is done with dynamical $N_f=2+1$ clover fermions for the CLS ensemble
H101 ($a=0.0854\,\mathrm{fm}$, $m_{\pi}=m_K=422\,\mathrm{MeV}$). The computed
CS kernel is in good agreement with experimental extractions and previous
lattice studies.

sections.3: Abdul Khalek et al., Science Requirements and Detector Concepts for the Electron-Ion Collider: EIC Yellow Report, 2103.05419.
sections.7: Constantinou et al., Parton distributions and lattice QCD calculations: toward 3D structure, 2006.08636.
sections.9: Dudek et al., Physics Opportunities with the 12 GeV Upgrade at Jefferson Lab, Eur
sections.11: Zhang et al., Lattice QCD Calculations of Transverse-Momentum-Dependent Soft Function through Large-Momentum Effective Theory, Phys
sections.13: Aschenauer et al., The RHIC SPIN Program: Achievements and Future Opportunities, 1501.01220.
sections.18: Vladimirov, Structure of rapidity divergences in multi-parton scattering soft factors, JHEP 04 (2018) 045, [1707.07606].
sections.29: Bruno et al., Simulation of QCD with N=f{}_{f}= 2 ++ 1 flavors of non-perturbatively improved Wilson fermions, JHEP 02 (2015) 043, [1411.3982].
sections.41: Zhang et al., Lattice QCD Calculations of Transverse-Momentum-Dependent Soft Function through Large-Momentum Effective Theory, Phys

authors:
Maximilian Schlemmer, Alexey Vladimirov, Christian Zimmermann, Michael
  Engelhardt and Andreas Sch\"afer

qualitative: The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel, also known as the rapidity anomalous dimension, is a fundamental nonperturbative function in QCD that governs the rapidity evolution of transverse momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs).
The CS kernel characterizes the energy-dependence of TMDs and is universal across different processes, depending only on the transverse distance b.

Lattice QCD provides a nonperturbative method to compute the CS kernel, offering complementary information to experimental extractions, especially at large transverse distances where experiments are insensitive.

The CS kernel can be extracted from ratios of first Mellin moments of quasi-TMDs (qTMDs) evaluated at different nucleon momenta, exploiting the fact that the soft factor and other nonperturbative contributions cancel in such ratios.

The method relies on the factorization theorem for qTMDs in the large hadron momentum limit, where qTMDs are related to physical TMDs via a perturbative coefficient function and a nonperturbative soft factor.

A key assumption in the approach is that the function encoding the x-dependence of TMDs (in the moment space) is nearly independent of the transverse distance b, which is considered a good approximation based on prior theoretical and phenomenological studies.
The use of first moments of qTMDs avoids the ill-defined Fourier transform into momentum-fraction space (x-space), which is a major challenge in direct qTMD analysis.
The CLS collaboration uses open boundary conditions in temporal and spatial directions to prevent topological freezing at fine lattice spacings, ensuring ergodicity in lattice simulations.
The method presented is more efficient than previous approaches (e.g., refs. citebib.bib25, bib.bib26) for extracting the CS kernel, though it is less general for studying other TMD properties.
quantitative: The lattice ensemble used is H101 with lattice spacing a = 0.0854 fm and pion mass m_π = m_K = 422 MeV.
The CS kernel is extracted from ratios of first Mellin moments of qTMDs at different nucleon momenta.
The CS kernel is found to be in good agreement with experimental extractions and previous lattice studies.
Experimental data from Drell-Yan and SIDIS are currently insensitive to the CS kernel for b ≳ 2 GeV, and even the future EIC will only constrain it up to b ≳ 3.5 GeV.
Lattice calculations are expected to reliably determine the CS kernel at b ∼ 5 GeV within a few years.
The method assumes that the x-dependent function in the moment factorization theorem is approximately independent of b, introducing a systematic error considered smaller than other uncertainties.

recall_papers.title: Lattice QCD Study of Transverse-Momentum Dependent Soft Function
recall_papers.abstract:
In this work, we perform a lattice QCD study of the intrinsic,
rapidity-independent soft function within the framework of large momentum
effective theory. The computation is carried out using a gauge ensemble of
$N_f=2+1+1$ clover-improved twisted mass fermion. After applying an appropriate
renormalization procedure and the removal of significant higher-twist
contamination, we obtain the intrinsic soft function that is comparable to the
one-loop perturbative result at large external momentum. The determination of
the nonperturbative soft function from first principles is crucial to sharpen
our understanding of the processes with small transverse momentum such as the
Drell-Yan production and the semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering.
Additionally, we calculate the Collins-Soper evolution kernel using the
quasi-transverse-momentum-dependent wave function as input.
authors:
Yuan Li, Shi-Cheng Xia, Constantia Alexandrou, Krzysztof Cichy, Martha
  Constantinou, Xu Feng, Kyriakos Hadjiyiannakou, Karl Jansen, Chuan Liu,
  Aurora Scapellato, Fernanda Steffens, Jacopo Tarello

recall_papers.title:
Transverse Momentum Dependent Gluon Distribution within High Energy
  Factorization at Next-to-Leading Order
recall_papers.abstract:
We discuss Transverse Momentum Dependent (TMD) gluon distributions within
high energy factorization at next-to-leading order in the strong coupling
within the framework of Lipatov's high energy effective action. We provide a
detailed discussion of both rapidity divergences related to the TMD definition
and its soft factor on the one hand, and rapidity divergences due to high
energy factorization on the other hand, and discuss common features and
differences between Collins-Soper (CS) and Balitsky-Fadin-Kuarev-Lipatov (BFKL)
evolution. While we confirm earlier results which state that the unpolarized
and linearly polarized gluon TMD agree in the BFKL limit at leading order, we
find that both distributions differ, once next-to-leading order corrections are
being included. Unlike previous results, our framework allows to recover the
complete anomalous dimension associated with Collins-Soper-Sterman (CSS)
evolution of the TMD distribution, including also single-logarithmic terms in
the CSS evolution. As an additional result we provide a definition of
$k_T$-factorization, i.e. matching of off-shell coefficients to collinear
factorization at next-to-leading order within high energy factorization and the
effective action framework. We furthermore establish a link between the QCD
operator definition of the TMD gluon distribution and a previously derived
off-shell TMD gluon-to-gluon splitting function, which is within the present
framework obtained as the real 1-loop correction.
authors: Martin Hentschinski

sorted_papers.title: The Four Loop QCD Rapidity Anomalous Dimension
sorted_papers.abstract:
The rapidity anomalous dimension controls the scaling of transverse momentum
dependent observables in the Sudakov region. In a conformal theory it is
equivalent to the soft anomalous dimension, but in QCD this relation is broken
by anomalous terms proportional to the $\beta$-function. In this paper we first
give a simple proof of this relation using two different representations of the
energy-energy correlator observable. We then calculate the anomalous terms to
three loops by computing the three-loop fully differential soft function to
$\mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$. Combined with recent perturbative data from the study
of on-shell form factors and splitting functions, this allows us to derive the
four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD.

sections.45: Armoni, Anomalous Dimensions from a Spinning D5-Brane, JHEP 11 (2006) 009, [hep-th/0608026].
sections.70: Meyer, Transforming differential equations of multi-loop Feynman integrals into canonical form, JHEP 04 (2017) 006, [arXiv:1611.01087].
sections.74: Nogueira, Automatic Feynman graph generation, J

qualitative: The rapidity anomalous dimension governs the scaling evolution of transverse momentum dependent observables in the Sudakov region, as described by the Collins-Soper-Sterman formalism and the rapidity renormalization group in SCET.
In a conformal field theory, the rapidity anomalous dimension is equivalent to the soft anomalous dimension, but in QCD this equivalence is broken by anomalous terms proportional to the β-function.
The difference between the rapidity and soft anomalous dimensions arises due to the non-conformal nature of QCD, with corrections involving the β-function that appear at higher loops.

The rapidity anomalous dimension is essential for resumming large logarithms in observables such as the energy-energy correlator (EEC) in the back-to-back limit and the transverse momentum spectrum of Higgs and electroweak bosons at small pT.

The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD is derived by exploiting its relation to the soft anomalous dimension in a conformal theory, with the breaking terms computed at three loops via the fully differential soft function to O(ε).

A simple proof of the equivalence between rapidity and soft anomalous dimensions in a conformal theory is provided using two representations of the EEC: one based on a Euclidean four-point function and the other on timelike factorization.
The calculation relies on known results for the soft anomalous dimension and recent perturbative data from form factors and splitting functions, enabling the derivation of the four-loop result.
quantitative: The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD is derived from the three-loop fully differential soft function computed to O(ε) in dimensional regularization.

The difference between the rapidity anomalous dimension γR and the soft anomalous dimension γS at four loops is given by terms proportional to the β-function: γR = γS + β(αs) × (three-loop correction).

The β-function in QCD is known to four loops: β(αs) = -ε αs + (αs^2 / (4π)) β0 + (αs^3 / (4π)^2) β1 + (αs^4 / (4π)^3) β2 + (αs^5 / (4π)^4) β3, with β0 = 11 C_A / 3 - 4 T_F n_f / 3, β1 = 102 C_A / 3 - 38 T_F n_f / 3, etc.
The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension is expressed as γR^(4) = γS^(4) + β^(3) × c3, where c3 is a coefficient determined from the three-loop soft function calculation.

The result enables NLL resummation for the energy-energy correlator in the back-to-back limit and for the small pT spectrum of Higgs and electroweak bosons, with the four-loop DGLAP evolution required for full precision.

sorted_papers.reason:
论文核心聚焦于QCD中快速度反常维数的四环计算，直接关联Collins–Soper演化中的关键函数。研究内容与查询主题完全一致，无领域或任务偏差。

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究主题为快速度反常维数 — 满足。证据：“The Four Loop QCD Rapidity Anomalous Dimension” 对比 “rapidity anomalous dimension”  
- C2：涉及Collins–Soper演化框架 — 满足。证据：“controls the scaling of transverse momentum dependent observables” 与演化理论一致  
- C3：基于QCD微扰计算 — 满足。证据：“in QCD” 明确限定理论框架  
- C4：四环精度计算 — 满足。证据：“four loop rapidity anomalous dimension”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文完全匹配查询所有关键约束。
authors: Ian Moult, Hua Xing Zhu, and Yu Jiao Zhu

content_sections.1 Introduction: Precision calculations in perturbative QCD play a crucial role in interpreting data in collider experiments. This is particularly true for key observables, such as QCD event shapes, which can be used for precision measurements of the strong coupling citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, or the Sudakov region of the Higgs transverse moment spectrum, where precision enables study of the coupling of the Higgs to light quarks citebib.bib5, bib.bib6. Due to progress in understanding the structure and universality of infrared evolution equations, as well as in fixed order calculations, these observables can be computed to next-to-next-to-next-to-leading logarithm (NLL) matched to next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) (see e. g. citebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21). In the last several years, there has been remarkable progress in extending calculations of a number of key gauge theory quantities to the four loop level. These include the β𝛽beta-function citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, the anomalous dimensions of twist-2 operators citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, the cusp anomalous dimension citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib26, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib29, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, the quark and gluon collinear anomalous dimensions citebib.bib33, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib34, bib.bib37, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib41, bib.bib40, bib.bib42, and most recently the full form factors citebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45. These anomalous dimensions govern the behavior of scattering amplitudes and cross sections in infrared limits, allowing the resummation of logarithms that appear in these limits, and which often invalidate a fixed order perturbative expansion, to NLL. They have recently seen their first phenomenological applications with the calculation of the four loop threshold corrections to Higgs production citebib.bib46. However, for a number of the most important phenomenological observables an additional anomalous dimension, the rapidity anomalous dimension, is required to describe the infrared dynamics. This is the case for the energy-energy-correlator (EEC) citebib.bib47 in the Sudakov observable, a key observable for precision studies of QCD event shapes, as well as for the transverse momentum, pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT, spectrum of the Higgs/electroweak bosons at small pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT. More generally, the rapidity anomalous dimension controls the scaling evolution of transverse momentum dependent observables, as formulated in the Collins-Soper-Sterman approach citebib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50 or in the rapidity renormalization group citebib.bib51, bib.bib52 approach in SCET citebib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, and is therefore a universal anomalous dimension describing the infrared dynamics of QCD. The goal of this paper is to fill this gap, and compute the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD. For the EEC, this allows resummation at NLL in the back-to-back region.2The five loop cusp anomalous dimension is also required to perform resummation at NLL, however, its effect should be minuscule. A rough estimate of the value of the five loop cusp can be obtained from the calculation of the five loop non-singlet anomalous dimensions citebib.bib28. Only the four loop anomalous dimensions in this paper are required to predict the singular structure at NLO. For the case of the pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT spectra of the Higgs or electroweak bosons, the complete four loop DGLAP evolution is also required. This is currently known for the non-singlet splitting functions citebib.bib27, and will hopefully be known for the singlet combinations in the near future. In this paper we compute the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD by exploiting its relation in a conformal field theory (CFT) to the soft anomalous dimension, γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS. This relation was discovered through perturbative calculations to three loops in citebib.bib58, and subsequently proven to all orders in citebib.bib59, bib.bib60. In a non-conformal theory this relation is broken by terms proportional to the β𝛽beta-function. The key advantage of exploiting this relation, is that since these corrections multiply β𝛽beta-functions, in perturbation theory they only need to be computed to one lower order. Therefore, we are able to compute the four loop anomalous dimension using known results for the soft function combined with a three loop calculation. Technically, we will obtain the anomalous terms by calculating the fully differential soft function citebib.bib61 to 𝒪​(ϵ)𝒪italic-ϵO(epsilon) at three loops in dimensional regularization.3This approach, as well as the result for the four loop anomalous dimensions were presented by the authors at World SCET 2020citebib.bib62 and at an online QCD seminar hosted by Xiangdong Jicitebib.bib63. In addition to presenting the result for the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD, we also give an additional proof of the correspondence between the rapidity anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension in a conformal theory using a recent derivation by Korchemsky of the back-to-back asymptotics of the EEC in a conformal field theory from a representation in terms of a Euclidean four point function citebib.bib64. The equivalence of this result with the timelike factorization derived in citebib.bib65 provides a simple proof of this equivalence. We hope that the results of this paper clarify the relations between different anomalous dimensions used in the literature, and emphasizes the importance of understanding relations between them. An outline of this paper is as follows. In Sec. we summarize recent progress in the calculation of four loop anomalous dimensions which we will use in our calculation of the four loop anomalous dimension. In Sec. , we review the definition of the rapidity anomalous dimension as the anomalous dimension of a particular configuration of Wilson lines. In Sec. we present a simple proof of the correspondence between soft and rapidity anomalous dimensions in a conformal field theory using two different representations of the Sudakov asymptotics of the energy-energy correlators. In Sec. we describe our calculation of the NLO fully differential soft function to 𝒪​(ϵ)𝒪italic-ϵO(epsilon). In Sec. we compute the difference between the soft anomalous dimension γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS and the rapidity anomalous dimension γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR at four loops, and hence derive the four loop QCD rapidity anomalous dimension. We conclude in Sec. . Additional perturbative data is collected in the Appendix, as well as the attached ancillary files.
content_sections.2 Summary of Perturbative Data for Anoma: In this section we summarize known results for QCD anomalous dimensions that we will use as perturbative data, and discuss some relations between these anomalous dimensions. This will also allow us to state our conventions and notation, since a number of anomalous dimensions are given different names in different contexts. Apart from the β𝛽beta-function, the simplest and most well studied anomalous dimensions in gauge theories are the anomalous dimensions of twist-2 spin-J𝐽J operators, or equivalently, the moments of the spacelike splitting functions. In the limit S→∞→𝑆Stoinfty, the twist-2 anomalous dimensions behave as (see e. g. citebib.bib66, bib.bib67) γ​(J,αs)=Γcusp​(αs)​(log⁡J+γE)−Bδ​(αs)+𝒪​(1/J). 𝛾𝐽subscript𝛼𝑠superscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠𝐽subscript𝛾𝐸subscript𝐵𝛿subscript𝛼𝑠𝒪1𝐽displaystylegamma(J,alphas)=Gammacusp(alphas)(log J+gammaE)-Bdelta(alphas)+O(1/J),. This is equivalent to the limit z→1→𝑧1zto 1 in the splitting functions, where they behave as citebib.bib66, bib.bib68, bib.bib69 P​(z,αs)=Γcusp​(αs)(1−z)++Bδ​(αs)​δ​(1−z)+⋯. 𝑃𝑧subscript𝛼𝑠superscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript1𝑧subscript𝐵𝛿subscript𝛼𝑠𝛿1𝑧⋯displaystyle P(z,alphas)=fracGammacusp(alphas)(1-z)++Bdelta(alphas)delta(1-z)+cdots,. These limits define two anomalous dimensions, the cusp anomalous dimension citebib.bib70, ΓcuspsuperscriptΓcuspGammacusp, which describes the leading divergences for a cusped Wilson line, and the virtual anomalous dimension, Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta. Significant perturbative data is available for both of these anomalous dimensions. In planar 𝒩=4𝒩4N=4 super Yang-Mills, both ΓcuspsuperscriptΓcuspGammacusp citebib.bib71, bib.bib72 and Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta citebib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75 are known to all loops from integrability. In QCD (and in non-planar 𝒩=4𝒩4N=4 SYM), the cusp anomalous dimension was recently obtained analytically to four loops citebib.bib76, bib.bib39, bib.bib40 following much earlier work (see citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib26, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib29, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38 for the analytic calculation of the planar and matter dependent terms, and citebib.bib27, bib.bib77, bib.bib78, bib.bib79, bib.bib29 for numerical extractions). Due to an extensive program calculating splitting functions in QCD (see e. g. citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29 for recent results), Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta is fully known analytically at three loops citebib.bib80, bib.bib81 for both quarks and gluons. All color structures in Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta are also known at four loops for both quarks and gluons, either numerically, or analytically. In particular, the planar result is known analytically citebib.bib27, as are results for the nf2superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑓2nf2 and nf3superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑓3nf3 terms citebib.bib82, bib.bib83. Updated numerical results for all additional color structures were given in citebib.bib41 for the quark case, and in citebib.bib46 for the gluon case. For convenience, these anomalous dimensions are summarized in the Appendix. Our second source of perturbative data will come from the study of on-shell form factors. The logarithms in the on-shell form factors are well studied (see e. g. citebib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87, bib.bib88, bib.bib89, bib.bib90, bib.bib91). Here we follow the notation of citebib.bib92. The all orders formula for a form factor with momentum transfer Q2superscript𝑄2Q2, evaluated at the scale μ2=Q2superscript𝜇2superscript𝑄2mu2=Q2 can be written F​(1,αs​(Q),ϵ)=exp⁡[12​∫0Q2d​λ2λ2​(G​(1,αs​(λ,ϵ),ϵ)−Γcusp​(αs​(λ,ϵ))​log⁡Q2λ2)],𝐹1subscript𝛼𝑠𝑄italic-ϵ12superscriptsubscript0superscript𝑄2𝑑superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2𝐺1subscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵitalic-ϵsuperscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵsuperscript𝑄2superscript𝜆2displaystyle F(1,alphas(Q),epsilon)=expleft[frac12int0Q2fracdlambda2lambda2left(G(1,alphas(lambda,epsilon),epsilon)-Gammacusp(alphas(lambda,epsilon))logfracQ2lambda2right)right],, where ϵ=2−d/2italic-ϵ2𝑑2epsilon=2-d/2 is the dimensional regulator, and αs​(λ,ϵ)subscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵalphas(lambda,epsilon) is the d𝑑d-dimensional running coupling at scale λ𝜆lambda. As with the twist-2 operators, the leading logarithms are governed by the cusp anomalous dimension. The subleading logarithms are governed by the so called “collinear anomalous dimension”, γGsubscript𝛾𝐺gammaG, which is defined by ∫0μ2d​λ2λ2​G​(1,αs​(λ,ϵ),ϵ)=∫0μ2d​λ2λ2​γG​(αs​(λ,ϵ))+𝒪​(ϵ0),superscriptsubscript0superscript𝜇2𝑑superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2𝐺1subscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵitalic-ϵsuperscriptsubscript0superscript𝜇2𝑑superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2subscript𝛾𝐺subscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵ𝒪superscriptitalic-ϵ0displaystyleint0mu2fracdlambda2lambda2G(1,alphas(lambda,epsilon),epsilon)=int0mu2fracdlambda2lambda2gammaGbig(alphas(lambda,epsilon)big)+O(epsilon0),, where it is understood that all the poles are absorbed by the integration with γGsubscript𝛾𝐺gammaG. The collinear anomalous dimension is known analytically to four loops in planar 𝒩=4𝒩4N=4 SYM citebib.bib93 (for an earlier numerical calculation, see citebib.bib94), and has been computed numerically in non-planar 𝒩=4𝒩4N=4 SYM citebib.bib78, bib.bib79. In QCD there has been an extensive program computing the four loop form factors. In particular, it was computed in the planar limit in citebib.bib33, bib.bib31, the nf3superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑓3nf3 terms were computed in citebib.bib32, the nf2superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑓2nf2 terms were computed in citebib.bib34, bib.bib37 and quartic color factor contributions were computed in citebib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib41. Very recently all matter dependent terms were obtained in citebib.bib40 (using previous results e. g. citebib.bib95, bib.bib96), and finally the full four loop form factors were computed citebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45. The anomalous dimension that we are ultimately interested in, and which is closely related to the rapidity anomalous dimension is the “soft anomalous dimension”, γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS, which can be expressed in terms of the anomalous dimensions described above as citebib.bib91, bib.bib97, bib.bib92 γG−2​Bδ=feik=−2​γS. subscript𝛾𝐺2subscript𝐵𝛿subscript𝑓eik2subscript𝛾𝑆displaystylegammaG-2Bdelta=ftexteik=-2gammaS,. The relation connecting collinear, virtual, and soft anomalous dimension, , can be easily understood by considering threshold resummation using SCET, where all these three anomalous dimension appear, see e. g. citebib.bib98, bib.bib99, bib.bib100, bib.bib101. The soft anomalous dimension is sometimes also referred to as feiksubscript𝑓eikftexteik, since it can be thought of as an eikonal version of the collinear anomalous dimension. For a recent discussion of this relation and other similar relations, see citebib.bib92. This anomalous dimension controls soft gluon radiation from Wilson lines, as we will describe in more detail in Sec. , and describes for example, Drell-Yan or gluon fusion at threshold, or dijet event shapes in the back-to-back limit, see e. g. citebib.bib102, bib.bib101, bib.bib103. Using the known perturbative data for γGsubscript𝛾𝐺gammaG, and Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta, the soft anomalous dimensions were assembled using Eq. in citebib.bib41, bib.bib46 (additional data was recently provided in citebib.bib40 has been incorporated in our results). The nf​CF2​Crsubscript𝑛𝑓superscriptsubscript𝐶𝐹2subscript𝐶𝑟nfCF2Cr and nf​CF​CA​Crsubscript𝑛𝑓subscript𝐶𝐹subscript𝐶𝐴subscript𝐶𝑟nfCFCACr and all other coefficients in the collinear anomalous dimension were computed analytically (or with high numerical precisions ) in citebib.bib40, bib.bib42, and by combing all the knowledges for the anomalous dimensions, especially from the study of Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta in citebib.bib41, bib.bib46, it turns out that our results only need four of the numeric values collected in Table 1 of the workcitebib.bib41. The extra numeric values are linearly dependent on those in Table, and they are numerically consistent as compared with the numbers collected in Table 1 of citebib.bib41. At four loops, strict Casimir scaling is violated for both the cusp anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension citebib.bib104, bib.bib67, bib.bib77, bib.bib78, bib.bib79, bib.bib27, bib.bib105, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37. However a generalized Casimir scaling holds for the cusp, and this is inherited by the soft anomalous dimension, since they exhibit the same maximally non-abelian structure citebib.bib97, bib.bib102, bib.bib91. The contributions that break naive Casimir scaling are due to the presence of the quartic Casimirs citebib.bib106 dF​Fsubscriptsuperscript𝑑4𝐹𝐹displaystyle dFF =(Nc4−6​Nc2+18)​(Nc2−1)96​Nc2,absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐46superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐218superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2196superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2displaystyle=frac(Nc4-6Nc2+18)(Nc2-1)96Nc2,, dF​Asubscriptsuperscript𝑑4𝐹𝐴displaystyle dFA =dA​F=Nc​(Nc2+6)​(Nc2−1)48,absentsubscriptsuperscript𝑑4𝐴𝐹subscript𝑁𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐26superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2148displaystyle=dAF=fracNc(Nc2+6)(Nc2-1)48,, dA​Asubscriptsuperscript𝑑4𝐴𝐴displaystyle dAA =Nc2​(Nc2+36)​(Nc2−1)24. absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐236superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2124displaystyle=fracNc2(Nc2+36)(Nc2-1)24,. The coefficients of these terms in the cusp and form factors were computed in citebib.bib29, bib.bib36, bib.bib35. The coefficients of these quartic Casimirs in both the cusp and eikonal anomalous dimensions exhibit a generalized Casimir scaling, namely that the contributions to the eikonal anomalous dimension from quartic Casimirs are determined by only two functions, instead of three citebib.bib29, bib.bib107. We have employed the generalized Casimir scaling to present the soft function anomalous dimension for generic SU(Ncsubscript𝑁𝑐Nc) representation r𝑟r in . We will also exploit this relation later in this paper to obtain the rapidity anomalous dimensions for both quarks and gluons.
content_sections.3 The Rapidity Anomalous Dimension: The rapidity anomalous dimension governs the rapidity evolution of TMD quantities. Its calculation typically requires a an definition of rapidity regulator. Here we adopt the exponential regulatorcitebib.bib108, which has been successfully applied at three-loop ordercitebib.bib58, bib.bib109, bib.bib110, bib.bib111, bib.bib112. Other definitions of rapidity regulator can be found in citebib.bib113, bib.bib114, bib.bib115, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib116, bib.bib117, bib.bib118. This configuration is shown in Fig. . This particular matrix element describes soft gluons in transverse momentum resummation, and certain e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- dijet event shapes in the back-to-back limit. Here we have chosen the orientation of the Wilson lines as appropriate for the case of an e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- event shape. For the case of qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT, the orientation of the Wilson lines is flipped, but this does not change the associated anomalous dimensionscitebib.bib119, bib.bib120, at least through to three loops. This matrix element exhibits divergences that are regulated by dimensional regularization, leading to an RG equation in μ𝜇mu, and divergences that are regulated by ν𝜈nu, leading to a rapidity renormalization group in ν𝜈nu. The RG equation in μ𝜇mu is given by μ​d​SEEC​(b→⟂,μ,ν)d​μ=[2​Γcusp​(αs)​ln⁡μ2ν2−4​γS​(αs)]​SEEC​(b→⟂,μ,ν),𝜇𝑑subscript𝑆EECsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈𝑑𝜇delimited-2subscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠superscript𝜇2superscript𝜈24subscript𝛾𝑆subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑆EECsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈displaystylemufracdStextEEC(vecbperp,mu,nu)dmu=left[2Gammatextcusp(alphas)lnfracmu2nu2-4gammaS(alphas)right]StextEEC(vecbperp,mu,nu),, and is governed by the cusp anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension, γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS described above. Similarly, the soft anomalous dimension also differed by a factor of 222 compared with γssubscript𝛾𝑠gammas in citebib.bib58, 2​γS=γs2subscript𝛾𝑆subscript𝛾𝑠2gammaS=gammas. ν​d​SEEC​(b→⟂,μ,ν)d​ν=[−2​∫b02/b→⟂2μ2d​μ¯2μ¯2​Γcusp​(αs​(μ¯))+4​γR​(αs​(b0/|b→⟂|))]​SEEC​(b→⟂,μ,ν),𝜈𝑑subscript𝑆EECsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈𝑑𝜈delimited-2superscriptsubscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑏02superscriptsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to2superscript𝜇2𝑑superscript¯𝜇2superscript¯𝜇2subscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠¯𝜇4subscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑏0subscript→𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑆EECsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈displaystylenufracdStextEEC(vecbperp,mu,nu)dnu=left[-2intlimitsb02/vecbperp2mu2fracdbarmu2barmu2Gammatextcusp(alphas(barmu))+4gammaR(alphas(b0/|vecbperp|))right]StextEEC(vecbperp,mu,nu),, which is governed by the cusp anomalous dimension, and the rapidity anomalous dimension γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR. Identical evolution equations hold for the case of transverse momentum resummation. The goal of this paper will be to compute the rapidity anomalous dimension to four loops in QCD, which completes the scale understanding of this matrix element at four loops. This soft function was computed to three loops in citebib.bib58, and there it was noticed that in a conformal theory one has the relationship γR=γS. subscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛾𝑆gammaR=gammaS,. Note that the rapidity anomalous dimension, begin a evolution equation in rapidity, contains higher order terms in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon, which are neglected in . This relation was subsequently generalized to all orders in citebib.bib59, bib.bib60, where the relation γR​(ϵ∗)=γSsubscript𝛾𝑅superscriptitalic-ϵsubscript𝛾𝑆displaystylegammaR(epsilon*)=gammaS was obtained, with explicit dependence of ϵitalic-ϵepsilon replaced by ϵ∗superscriptitalic-ϵepsilon*, which is determined by equation β∗​(ϵ∗)≡1αs​β​(αs)−2​ϵ∗=0superscript𝛽superscriptitalic-ϵ1subscript𝛼𝑠𝛽subscript𝛼𝑠2superscriptitalic-ϵ0beta*(epsilon*)equivfrac1alphasbeta(alphas)-2epsilon*=0 order-by-order in QCD. In other words, it is the critical dimension where QCD is conformal order by order in perturbation theory. This relationship allows us to compute γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR at a given loop order by knowing γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS at that loop order, and γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR to higher orders in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon at lower loop orders. This approach has been utilized in citebib.bib59 to reproduce the three-loop γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR from a two-loop calculation and knowledge of the three-loop γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS. We will adopt this approach in this paper. For a recent formal study of the uses of conformal symmetry in d=4−2​ϵ𝑑42italic-ϵd=4-2epsilon, see citebib.bib121.
content_sections.4 Proof of Soft-Rapidity Correspondence: Since we will exploit heavily the correspondence between the rapidity anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension in a CFT, in this section we give an independent proof of this fact using the energy-energy correlator (EEC) observable. This proof exploits the universality of factorization, combined with the fact that the EEC also admits a representation in terms of a Euclidean four point function. By comparing two distinct results for the EEC we are able to prove γR=γSsubscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛾𝑆gammaR=gammaS in a conformal theory. The EEC admits a timelike factorization formula citebib.bib65 derived in SCET citebib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57 d​σd​z=𝑑𝜎𝑑𝑧absentdisplaystylefracdsigmadz= 14​∫0∞𝑑b​b​J0​(b​Q​1−z)​H​(Q,μh)​jEECq​(b,b0/b,Q)​jEECq¯​(b,b0/b,Q)​SEEC​(b,μs,νs)14superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑏𝑏subscript𝐽0𝑏𝑄1𝑧𝐻𝑄subscript𝜇ℎsubscriptsuperscript𝑗𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscriptsuperscript𝑗¯𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscript𝑆EEC𝑏subscript𝜇𝑠subscript𝜈𝑠displaystyle;frac14intlimits0inftydb,bJ0(bQsqrt1-z)H(Q,muh)jqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)jbarqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)StextEEC(b,mus,nus) ⋅(Q2νs2)2​γR​(αs​(b0/b))exp[∫μs2μh2d​μ¯2μ¯2Γcusp(αs(μ¯))lnb2​μ¯2b02displaystyle;cdotleft(fracQ2nus2right)2gammaR(alphas(b0/b))expleft[intlimitsmus2muh2fracdbarmu2barmu2Gammatextcusp(alphas(barmu))lnfracb2barmu2b02right. +∫μh2b02/b2d​μ¯2μ¯2(Γcusp(αs(μ¯))lnb2​Q2b02+γH(αs(μ¯)))−∫μs2b02/b2d​μ¯2μ¯2γS(αs(μ¯))],displaystyle;left. +intlimitsmuh2b02/b2fracdbarmu2barmu2left(Gammatextcusp(alphas(barmu))lnfracb2Q2b02+gammaH(alphas(barmu))right)-intlimitsmus2b02/b2fracdbarmu2barmu2gammaS(alphas(barmu))right],, where γH=−γG=−2​Bδ+2​γSsubscript𝛾𝐻subscript𝛾𝐺2subscript𝐵𝛿2subscript𝛾𝑆gammaH=-gammaG=-2Bdelta+2gammaS is the collinear anomalous dimension. This factorization formula is identical in structure to that for pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT resummation. The latter has been proven rigorously, including the cancellation of Glauber gluons citebib.bib122. It has been used to compute the EEC at NLL’ citebib.bib123 using results for the three-loop transverse momentum dependent fragmentation functions citebib.bib112, bib.bib111. For other studies of the EEC in the back-to-back limit, see citebib.bib124, bib.bib125, bib.bib126, bib.bib127. This formula applies in both conformal and non-conformal gauge theories, however, to prove the equality of the soft and rapidity anomalous dimensions, we can consider this formula in the conformal limit. After setting all scales equal to their canonical values such that all large logarithms are absorbed into the Sudakov exponent, μh=Q,μs=b0b,νs=b0b,formulae-sequencesubscript𝜇ℎ𝑄formulae-sequencesubscript𝜇𝑠subscript𝑏0𝑏subscript𝜈𝑠subscript𝑏0𝑏muh=Q,,qquadmus=fracb0b,,qquadnus=fracb0b,, and using the fact that the coupling constant does not run in a conformal theory, we have d​σd​z=𝑑𝜎𝑑𝑧absentdisplaystylefracdsigmadz= 14​∫0∞𝑑b​b​J0​(b​Q​1−z)​H​(Q,μh)​jEECq​(b,b0/b,Q)​jEECq¯​(b,b0/b,Q)​SEEC​(b,μs,νs)14superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑏𝑏subscript𝐽0𝑏𝑄1𝑧𝐻𝑄subscript𝜇ℎsubscriptsuperscript𝑗𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscriptsuperscript𝑗¯𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscript𝑆EEC𝑏subscript𝜇𝑠subscript𝜈𝑠displaystyle;frac14intlimits0inftydb,bJ0(bQsqrt1-z)H(Q,muh)jqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)jbarqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)StextEEC(b,mus,nus) exp⁡[−12​Γcusp​log2⁡(b2​Q2b02)+2​Bδ​log⁡(b2​Q2b02)+2​(γR−γS)​log⁡(b2​Q2b02)].12subscriptΓcuspsuperscript2superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏022subscript𝐵𝛿superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏022subscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛾𝑆superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏02displaystyleexpleft[-frac12Gammatextcusplog2left(fracb2Q2b02right)+2Bdeltalogleft(fracb2Q2b02right)+2(gammaR-gammaS)logleft(fracb2Q2b02right)right],. The EEC is special in that it also admits a representation as a four point Wightman function citebib.bib128, bib.bib129, bib.bib130, enabling it to be studied using a completely different set of techniques from conformal field theory. In particular, the EEC can be written as EEC∼∫d4​x​ei​x⋅q​⟨0|Jμ​​ℰ​(n→1)​ℰ​(n→2)​Jμ​(x)|0⟩,similar-toEECsuperscript𝑑4𝑥superscript𝑒⋅𝑖𝑥𝑞quantum-operator-product0subscript𝐽𝜇0ℰsubscript→𝑛1ℰsubscript→𝑛2subscript𝐽𝜇𝑥0displaystyletextEECsimint d4xeixcdot q 0|JmuE(vecn1)E(vecn2)Jmu(x)|0,, where ℰ​(n→)=∫0∞𝑑t​limr→∞r2​ni​T0​i​(t,r​n→),ℰ→𝑛superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑡subscript→𝑟superscript𝑟2superscript𝑛𝑖subscript𝑇0𝑖𝑡𝑟→𝑛displaystyleE(vecn)=intlimits0inftydtlimrtoinftyr2niT0i(t,rvecn),, is an energy flow operator citebib.bib131, bib.bib132, bib.bib133, bib.bib128, bib.bib130, bib.bib129, bib.bib134. It is therefore expressible as an integral kernel acting on the four point function of stress tensors. Using an understanding of the behavior of the four point function as operators become lightlike separated citebib.bib135, bib.bib136 and the high spin limit of the twist two operators Eq. , Korchemsky proved in citebib.bib64 that the EEC in a conformal theory is governed by the anomalous dimensions ΓcuspsuperscriptΓcuspGammacusp and Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta only, and is described by the formula5See e. g. Eq. (1.5) of citebib.bib64. We have converted it to the conventions used in this paper. d​σd​z=𝑑𝜎𝑑𝑧absentdisplaystylefracdsigmadz= 14​∫0∞𝑑b​b​J0​(b​Q​1−z)​H​(Q,μh)​jEECq​(b,b0/b,Q)​jEECq¯​(b,b0/b,Q)​SEEC​(b,μs,νs)14superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑏𝑏subscript𝐽0𝑏𝑄1𝑧𝐻𝑄subscript𝜇ℎsubscriptsuperscript𝑗𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscriptsuperscript𝑗¯𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscript𝑆EEC𝑏subscript𝜇𝑠subscript𝜈𝑠displaystyle;frac14intlimits0inftydb,bJ0(bQsqrt1-z)H(Q,muh)jqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)jbarqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)StextEEC(b,mus,nus) exp⁡[−12​Γcusp​log2⁡(b2​Q2b02)+2​Bδ​log⁡(b2​Q2b02)].12subscriptΓcuspsuperscript2superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏022subscript𝐵𝛿superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏02displaystyleexpleft[-frac12Gammatextcusplog2left(fracb2Q2b02right)+2Bdeltalogleft(fracb2Q2b02right)right],. By comparing this formula derived from the four point function with the timelike factorization formula in Eq. , we immediately prove γR=γSsubscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛾𝑆gammaR=gammaS in a CFT. We therefore arrive at a transparent proof of the relation between soft and rapidity anomalous dimensions.6It is interesting to note that non-trivial relations between anomalous dimensions (in particular the Basso-Korchemsky reciprocity citebib.bib137) can also be derived by studying equivalent expressions for the EECs in the collinear limit. Ref. citebib.bib138 provides an explicit example of such relation at three loops in QCD. On the one hand they can be expressed in terms of spacelike anomalous dimensions using the light-ray OPE citebib.bib128, bib.bib139, bib.bib64, and on the other hand they can be expressed in terms of timelike anomalous dimensions using factorization formulas derived in SCET citebib.bib140, bib.bib141. These relations suggest much is still to be understood about Lorentzian dynamics. Although this relation is sufficient for our purposes, it would be interesting to extend this to study the behavior of rapidity anomalous dimensions for soft functions that have Wilson lines in greater than two directions. Higher point energy correlators exhibit Sudakov behavior in particular kinematic limits, and this has interesting applications at hadron colliders citebib.bib124. These singular regions can be accessed also on the correlator side. However, unlike the more well studied case citebib.bib135, for three-point correlators and higher the operators become lightlike separated, but not in a sequential limit.
content_sections.5 The N3LO Fully Differential Soft Funct: In this section we describe the methods used to compute the NLO fully differential soft function to 𝒪​(ϵ)𝒪italic-ϵO(epsilon) at three loops, from which we will extract the rapidity anomalous dimension, retaining higher-order dependence in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. The fully differential soft function was introduced incitebib.bib61 for the study of transverse momentum resummation. It has been used incitebib.bib142 as a deformation of the threshold soft function, and in citebib.bib108 as a deformation of TMD soft function. It has also been exploited to calculate Sudakov radiator for jet observablecitebib.bib143. It is mostly conveniently defined on the exponent, ℱ​(q)≡∫∏idd​qi(2​π)d−1​δ+​(qi2)​(2​π)d​δ(d)​(q−∑iqi)​𝒲n​n¯​(qi),ℱ𝑞subscriptproduct𝑖superscript𝑑𝑑subscript𝑞𝑖superscript2𝜋𝑑1subscript𝛿superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖2superscript2𝜋𝑑superscript𝛿𝑑𝑞subscript𝑖subscript𝑞𝑖subscript𝒲𝑛¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑖displaystyleF(q)equivintprodifracddqi(2pi)d-1,delta+(qi2)(2pi)ddelta(d)(q-sumiqi)Wnbarn(qi),, where additional symmetry factor for identical final-state particle is implicitly understood. The integrand 𝒲n​n¯​(qi)subscript𝒲𝑛¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑖Wnbarn(qi) corresponds to single or multiple soft emissions web diagrams from two lightlike Wilson lines Snsubscript𝑆𝑛Sn and Sn¯subscript𝑆¯𝑛Sbarn, such that non-abelian exponentiation theorem is made manifest whenever the observable have that property. Definition of web diagrams can be found, e. g. , in citebib.bib144, bib.bib145. The main task of the work here is to calculate the NNNLO fully differential soft function to higher orders in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. The TMD soft functioncitebib.bib58 and the threshold soft functioncitebib.bib103, bib.bib146, bib.bib147, bib.bib148 can then all be obtained as limits of the fully differential soft function. To perform the calculation, Feynman diagrams were generated by Qgraphcitebib.bib1497We note that several results for the soft web diagrams are available in the literature, namely the two-loop single soft currentcitebib.bib147, bib.bib148, one-loop double soft currentcitebib.bib119, bib.bib120, and tree-level triple soft currentcitebib.bib150. Furthermore, three-loop threshold function has also been obtained in citebib.bib151, bib.bib152, bib.bib103, bib.bib146, bib.bib153. We choose to generate the relevant soft currents using an independent code, therefore providing additional checks on the existing results. , color/Dirac algebra and integrand manipulations were performed in formcitebib.bib154. With the help of reverse unitaritycitebib.bib155, the integrals can be reduced with Integration-By-Parts identitiescitebib.bib156. We use the publicly available Mathematica package LiteRedcitebib.bib157. We obtained systems of master integrals to formulate differential equations with respect to n𝑛n and n¯¯𝑛barn rescaling invariant x𝑥x, x≡q2​(n⋅n¯)2​(q⋅n)​(q⋅n¯)=q2q+​q−,𝑥superscript𝑞2⋅𝑛¯𝑛2⋅𝑞𝑛⋅𝑞¯𝑛superscript𝑞2superscript𝑞superscript𝑞displaystyle xequivfracq2,(ncdotbarn)2(qcdot n),(qcdotbarn)=fracq2q+q-,, which can be solved using the method of differential equationcitebib.bib158. Note that the final-state total momentum satisfy q2≥0superscript𝑞20q2geq 0, x∈[0,1]𝑥01xin[0,1]. The differential equations allow an ϵitalic-ϵepsilon-formcitebib.bib159 with the help of CANONICAcitebib.bib160, after which we can derive the boundary constants by considering the limit x→1→𝑥1xto 1 and x→0→𝑥0xto 0. To all orders in perturbation, the unnormalized results can be written as ℱb​(q,ϵ)=∑j=0∞(asb)j+1​FjT,b​(x,ϵ)(q→⟂2)2+j​ϵ,superscriptℱb𝑞italic-ϵsuperscriptsubscript𝑗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠b𝑗1subscriptsuperscript𝐹𝑇b𝑗𝑥italic-ϵsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscript→𝑞perpendicular-to22𝑗italic-ϵFrm b(q,epsilon)=sumj=0infty(asrm b)j+1fracFT,rm bj(x,epsilon)(vecqperp,2)2+jepsilon,, where the variable x𝑥x is defined in and asb=αsb/(4​π)superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠bsuperscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠b4𝜋asrm b=alphasrm b/(4pi) is the bare coupling constant. We have retain the higher order in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon dependence in the definition of full differential soft function, in anticipating that further integration of q𝑞q can leads to poles in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. The bare perturbation results for FT,b​(x,ϵ)superscript𝐹𝑇b𝑥italic-ϵFT,rm b(x,epsilon), as well as the renormalized ones, are given in the ancillary file fully. m through to three loops.

We then combine this with the perturbative data summarized earlier to give the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD. In Fig. , we plot the perturbative rapidity anomalous dimension through to four loops in QCD,8For non-perturbative calculations of the rapidity anomalous dimension or TMD soft function from lattice QCD, see e. g. citebib.bib161, bib.bib162, bib.bib163, bib.bib164, bib.bib165, bib.bib166. using fundamental Wilson lines as an example. The four-loop corrections are non-negligible, and should be taken into account in future phenomenological studies. We also show the four-loop results without the quartic Casimir corrections, which illustrates that these corrections are small. For the case of qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT resummation rapidity renormalization group consistency implies that the rapidity anomalous dimension given in Eq. also determines the beam function rapidity anomalous dimension to four loops.
content_sections.7 Conclusions: In this paper we have computed the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD. This result was derived by exploiting a relation between the rapidity anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension in a conformal theory, and computing the corrections to this relation arising from conformal symmetry breaking in QCD. At a technical level this was achieved by computing the fully differential soft function to 𝒪​(ϵ)𝒪italic-ϵO(epsilon) at three loops. We also described in some detail how this calculation was performed with the hope that it can be useful for performing calculations of other rapidity divergent soft functions. In this paper we have also attempted to further clarify the nature of the rapidity anomalous dimension by presenting a simple proof relating the soft and rapidity anomalous dimensions using two equivalent representations of the the energy-energy correlator observable in the back-to-back limit. This relates the behavior of the four-point correlator citebib.bib64, with the rapidity anomalous dimension appearing in the time like factorization formula of citebib.bib65. The four loop rapidity anomalous dimension enables the resummation of the EEC in the back-to-back region at NLL, which is the first event shape for which all ingredients are available to achieve this perturbative accuracy. It will be particularly interesting to study the convergence of the perturbative series, as well as the phenomenological implications for extractions of the strong coupling. For the case of pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT resummation for the Higgs or electroweak pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT spectra, the remaining ingredient required to achieve NLL resummation is the singlet four loop splitting functions. Our approach to computing the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension by exploiting its relation to other anomalous dimensions enabling it to be obtained through a three loop calculation illustrates the importance of better understanding relations between different anomalous dimensions in gauge theories. Much recent progress has been made along these lines citebib.bib92. An interesting generalization of the case considered in this paper is to understand the relation of the rapidity anomalous dimension for observables with more than two colored lines, such as the transverse EEC as relevant for hadron colliders citebib.bib124, and its relation to the soft anomalous dimension with multiple lines, which is known at three loops citebib.bib167, bib.bib168. In particular, it is interesting to understand whether the dipole conjecture holds for the rapidity anomalous dimension. Another rapidity type anomalous dimension that is difficult to compute directly is the Regge trajectory, which was recently computed in QCD to three loops citebib.bib169, bib.bib170, bib.bib171, bib.bib172, bib.bib173, bib.bib174, bib.bib175, bib.bib176, bib.bib177, bib.bib178, bib.bib179. With the wealth of four loop data for anomalous dimensions, we believe it is important to understand its relation to other anomalous dimensions to enable its calculation to higher orders, and to obtain a more complete understanding of the relations between infrared anomalous dimensions. Acknowledgements. We thank Alexey Vladimirov for useful discussions. We thank Claude Duhr, Bernhard Mistlberger and Gherardo Vita for coordinating the suission of their work. I. M. is supported by start-up funds from Yale University. H. X. Z is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China under contract No. 11975200. Note Added: Simultaneously with this paper, the work of citebib.bib180 appeared, which also presented a result for the anomalous dimension, we have verified that our result agrees.

sorted_papers.title:
Nonperturbative Collins-Soper Kernel from Chiral Quarks with Physical
  Masses
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice QCD calculation of the rapidity anomalous dimension of
quark transverse-momentum-dependent distributions, i.e., the Collins-Soper (CS)
kernel, up to transverse separations of about 1 fm. This unitary lattice
calculation is conducted, for the first time, employing the
chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermion discretization and physical
values of light and strange quark masses. The CS kernel is extracted from the
ratios of pion quasi-transverse-momentum-dependent wave functions
(quasi-TMDWFs) at next-to-leading logarithmic perturbative accuracy. Also for
the first time, we utilize the recently proposed Coulomb-gauge-fixed
quasi-TMDWF correlator without a Wilson line. We observe significantly slower
signal decay with increasing quark separations compared to the established
gauge-invariant method with a staple-shaped Wilson line. This enables us to
determine the CS kernel at large nonperturbative transverse separations and
find its near-linear dependence on the latter. Our result is consistent with
the recent lattice calculation using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs, and agrees
with various recent phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data.

qualitative: The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel governs the rapidity scale evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs), enabling consistent interpretation of experimental data across different energy scales.
The CS kernel is a nonperturbative quantity in QCD that must be determined from first principles due to the model dependence inherent in phenomenological parametrizations.
Lattice QCD provides a first-principles approach to compute the CS kernel via quasi-TMD wave functions (quasi-TMDWFs) within the Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) framework.

The conventional gauge-invariant (GI) approach to quasi-TMDWFs requires a staple-shaped Wilson line, which introduces complications such as linear divergences, pinch-pole singularities, operator mixing, and exponential signal decay with increasing quark separation.

The Coulomb-gauge-fixed (CG) approach eliminates the need for Wilson lines, leading to multiplicatively renormalizable correlators free from linear divergences and pinch singularities, and avoiding operator mixing from Wilson-line geometry.

The CG quasi-TMDWFs are shown to factorize in the large momentum limit and belong to the same universality class as GI quasi-TMDs under LaMET, validating their use for extracting physical observables.

The CG method exhibits significantly slower signal decay with increasing transverse quark separation compared to the GI method, enabling access to larger nonperturbative transverse separations (up to ~1 fm).
The CS kernel extracted from the CG approach shows a near-linear dependence on the transverse separation b⟂, consistent with expectations from perturbative and phenomenological models.
The result from the CG method agrees with previous lattice calculations using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs and with various phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data.
quantitative: The CS kernel is extracted from ratios of pion quasi-TMDWFs at next-to-leading logarithmic (NLL) perturbative accuracy.
The calculation is performed using physical light and strange quark masses with chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermions.
The transverse separation b⟂ is probed up to approximately 1 fm, which is a significant extension compared to previous methods.

The CS kernel exhibits near-linear dependence on b⟂, indicating a nonperturbative behavior consistent with the form γ_S(b⟂) ≈ γ_0 + γ_1 b⟂^2 (implied by linear dependence in b⟂, though exact functional form not specified in abstract).

The result is consistent with recent lattice calculations using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs and with phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data (e.g., from SIDIS and Drell-Yan processes).
