content_sections.I Introduction: Since the 1970s it has been understood that the intrinsic motion of partons inside hadrons in the direction transverse to the hadron’s momentum plays an important role in experimentally observed processes, beginning historically with Drell-Yan scattering (DY)citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3. The effect of this motion on the DY cross section has been rigorously derived in QCD in the form of a factorization theoremcitebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6 and thereby described in terms of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDs). TMDs are universal, appearing in the factorization of cross-sections for processes including also semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering (SIDIS) and di-hadron production in e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collisions. Constraints on TMDs, particularly for the nucleon, have thus been the target of experimental programs since the 2000s (see Refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib8 for a review) and remain key targets of current and future experiments at facilities including the Thomas Jefferson National Accelerator Facilitycitebib.bib9, bib.bib10, the Large Hadron Collidercitebib.bib11, bib.bib12, and the Electron-Ion Collidercitebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19. Simultaneously, significant efforts are being made from the theoretical perspective to constrain TMDs, including through lattice QCD calculationscitebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37. TMDs have a functional dependence on two scales: a virtuality scale μ𝜇mu and a rapidity scale ζ𝜁zeta, which is related to the hadron momentum in a scattering process. While the renormalization group (RG) evolution of TMDs with μ𝜇mu is perturbative for perturbative scales μ𝜇mu and ζ𝜁zeta, the evolution with ζ𝜁zeta is inherently nonperturbative in certain regions of parameter space, even for perturbative μ𝜇mu. The ζ𝜁zeta-evolution of TMDs is encoded in the Collins-Soper (CS) kernelcitebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, which can be defined as the rapidity anomalous dimension entering the relevant RG evolution equations (up to a conventional factor): γp​(bT,μ)=2​ddln⁡ζ⁡ln⁡ϕp​(bT,μ,x,ζ),subscript𝛾𝑝subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇2derivative𝜁subscriptitalic-ϕ𝑝subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇𝑥𝜁gammap(bT,mu)=2derivativelnzetalnphip(bT,mu,x,zeta), where ϕp​(bT,x,μ,ζ)subscriptitalic-ϕ𝑝subscript𝑏𝑇𝑥𝜇𝜁phip(bT,x,mu,zeta) is a TMD, chosen here as a TMD wavefunction (TMD WF) encoding the transverse motion of a parton p∈q,g𝑝𝑞𝑔pinq,g in a meson statecitebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41. The TMD WF is defined in a factorization formula valid in the limit of ultra-relativistic hadron momentum 𝐏𝐏P and depends on the fraction x𝑥x of the parton’s momentum collinear with 𝐏𝐏P, as well as the parton’s momentum transverse to 𝐏𝐏P as given by its Fourier conjugate bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, the transverse displacement. The CS kernel depends on μ𝜇mu, bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT and parton type p𝑝p, but is independent of x𝑥x and the hadronic state. Experimental DY and SIDIS data has been used to constrain phenomenological parameterizations of the quark CS kernelcitebib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56. A number of parameterizations are in some tension in the region bT≳0.2fmgreater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑏𝑇times0.2femtometerbTgtrsim0.2text,fm (at μ=2GeV𝜇times2gigaelectronvoltmu=2text,GeV), which may be partially understood to arise from different approaches to modelling nonpertubative effects. In the more recent analysescitebib.bib55, bib.bib56, the tensions have been reduced as larger sets of experimental data sensitive to the CS kernel in the nonperturbative regimecitebib.bib57, bib.bib53 were included. Further improvements are expected with future data from the LHCcitebib.bib12 and the Electron-Ion Collidercitebib.bib17, bib.bib18. A direct way of constraining the kernel from cross-section ratios has also been proposed and demonstrated on synthetic datacitebib.bib58 and could be applied to experimental data in the future. A more precise determination of the nonperturbative CS kernel is important in particular for measurements of electroweak observables such as the W±superscript𝑊plus-or-minusWpm-boson masscitebib.bib59 and especially for studies of nucleon and nuclear structure via deep inelastic scatteringcitebib.bib17. Complementing phenomenological approaches, lattice QCD offers a pathway towards first-principles constraints of the CS kernel in the nonperturbative regime. One approach to such calculations is provided by Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET)citebib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, in which physical TMDs, defined by matrix elements of lightlike-separated operators, and quasi-distributions, defined by the matrix elements of the corresponding spacelike-separated operators which are computable in lattice QCD, are perturbatively matched at large hadron momentum |𝐏|≫ΛQCDmuch-greater-than𝐏subscriptΛQCDlvertPrvertggLambdaQCDcitebib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib41, bib.bib68, bib.bib69, bib.bib70, bib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74. For example, a TMD WF ϕp​(bT,μ,x,ζ)subscriptitalic-ϕ𝑝subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇𝑥𝜁phip(bT,mu,x,zeta) is matched to a quasi-TMD WF ϕp​(bT,μ,x,ζ)subscriptitalic-ϕ𝑝subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇𝑥𝜁tildephip(bT,mu,x,zeta) with matching coefficients computed perturbatively in LaMETcitebib.bib71, bib.bib75 up to a nonperturbative soft factor independent of x𝑥x and ζ𝜁zeta and power corrections that vanish in the limit of infinite boost. To date, several lattice QCD calculations have been carried out using quasi-TMD WFs and other quasi-distributions to extract the quark CS kernelcitebib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib29, bib.bib28, bib.bib32, bib.bib34, bib.bib37 and the soft functioncitebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib37, as well as the full kinematic dependence of TMDscitebib.bib33, bib.bib35. Using quasi-TMD WFs and LaMET, this work presents the first lattice QCD calculation of the quark CS kernel at valence quark masses corresponding to a close-to-physical value of the pion mass, mπ=148.8​MeVsubscript𝑚𝜋timesuncertain148.81megaelectronvoltmpi=148.8text,MeV, thereby addressing the systematic uncertainty arising from the sensitivity of the kernel to the QCD vacuum structurecitebib.bib76 and reducing those arising from perturbative LaMET matching and proportional to mπ2/(x​|𝐏|)2subscriptsuperscript𝑚2𝜋superscript𝑥𝐏2m2pi/(xlvertPrvert)2 and mπ2/((1−x)​|𝐏|)2subscriptsuperscript𝑚2𝜋superscript1𝑥𝐏2m2pi/((1-x)lvertPrvert)2. Other bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT-dependent systematic uncertainties associated with matching are better quantified relative to previous calculations. The matching is performed at next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) and next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic (NNLL) accuracies for the first time in a calculation of the CS kernel, using recent results of Refs. citebib.bib77, bib.bib78. Moreover, previously dominantcitebib.bib28 systematic uncertainties from the Fourier transformation of quasi-TMDs are reduced in this work, and the associated model dependence is eliminated. Finally, renormalization-induced mixing effects for the nonlocal operators associated with quasi-TMDs are fully quantified for the first time in the RI/xMOMRIxMOMrm RI/xMOM renormalization schemecitebib.bib79, bib.bib80, bib.bib81. Taken together, this work achieves sufficient control and precision to begin to discriminate in the nonperturbative region between phenomenological parameterizationscitebib.bib44, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib55, bib.bib56 of the quark CS kernel and provides a better understanding of perturbative convergence in LaMET matching and the associated power corrections.

content_sections.III Numerical Investigation: The quark CS kernel is computed numerically using an ensemble of lattice gauge-field configurations produced by the MILC collaborationcitebib.bib83 with 2+1+12112+1+1 dynamical quark flavors and four-volume V=L3×T=(48​a)3×64​a𝑉superscript𝐿3𝑇superscript48𝑎364𝑎V=L3times T=(48a)3times 64a with a=0.12fm𝑎times0.12femtometera=0.12text,fm. The one-loop Symanzik improved gauge actioncitebib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87 and the highly improved staggered quark action with sea quark masses tuned to produce a close-to-physical pion masscitebib.bib88, bib.bib89, bib.bib90 are used for gauge field generation. Gauge field configurations are subjected to Wilson flow with flow-time 𝔱=1.0𝔱1.0mathfrakt=1.0citebib.bib91 to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio in numerical results, and are gauge fixed to Landau gauge. Calculations are performed in a mixed-action setup with the tree-level 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a)-improved Wilson clover fermion actioncitebib.bib92, bib.bib93, bib.bib94 used for propagator computation, with hopping parameter κ=0.125 47𝜅0.12547kappa=0.125,47 and clover term coefficient csw=1.0subscript𝑐sw1.0csw=1.0, resulting in a pion mass of mπ=148.8​MeVsubscript𝑚𝜋timesuncertain148.81megaelectronvoltmpi=148.8text,MeV. The following subsections detail the steps of the calculation of the quark CS kernel, including calculations of the bare quasi-TMD WF ratios and renormalization matrices, the Fourier transform to bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT-space, and finally the extraction of the CS kernel from ratios of quasi-TMD WF ratios with perturbative matching corrections.

At the level of renormalization constants as defined by , mixing effects for collinear configurations of pRμsubscriptsuperscript𝑝𝜇RpmuR and ξRμsubscriptsuperscript𝜉𝜇RximuR are consistent with constraints on staple-shaped operator mixing from 𝒞𝒞C, 𝒫𝒫P and 𝒯𝒯T transformationscitebib.bib107, bib.bib36, and the dominant contributions are as expected from lattice perturbation theory at one-loop ordercitebib.bib106. While noncollinear momentum configurations are not used in the determination of the kernel, an investigation of mixing effects using such a definition of the associated renormalization scales, summarized in Appendix, reveals contributions to mixing in addition to those expected in lattice perturbation theory at one-loop order. The additional contributions may be understood as artifacts of an off-shell renormalization scheme. This indicates different rates of perturbative convergence in real and imaginary parts of matching corrections. The same qualitative picture is observed for fixed-order corrections in of . The contribution of a​Pz𝑎superscript𝑃𝑧aPz-dependent discretization artifacts to γΓMS¯subscriptsuperscript𝛾¯MSΓhatgammaoverlinerm MSGamma can be expected to be comparable to that of x​Pz𝑥superscript𝑃𝑧xPz-dependent power corrections in the intermediate x𝑥x region. Since both effects are Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz-dependent, they can not be disentangled and it is left to future work to quantify their separate contributions to systematic uncertainty in the CS kernel determination. Weighted averages of Re​[γΓMS¯​(bT,x,P1z,P2z,μ)]Redelimited-superscriptsubscript𝛾Γ¯MSsubscript𝑏𝑇𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑃1𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑧𝜇Rebig[hatgammaGammaoverlinerm MS(bT,x,P1z,P2z,mu)big] are computed at the bootstrap level with weights taken to be proportional to the inverse variance of Re​[γΓMS¯​(bT,x,P1z,P2z,μ)]Redelimited-superscriptsubscript𝛾Γ¯MSsubscript𝑏𝑇𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑃1𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑧𝜇Rebig[hatgammaGammaoverlinerm MS(bT,x,P1z,P2z,mu)big]. The estimator Re​[γq,ΓMS¯]Redelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝛾¯MS𝑞ΓRebig[hatgammaoverlinerm MSq,Gammabig] is computed for a uniform grid of points in x𝑥x with spacing Δ​x=0.05Δ𝑥0.05Delta x=0.05; a wide range of different choices of Δ​xΔ𝑥Delta x lead to indistinguishable results as long as correlations between Re​[γΓMS¯​(bT,x,P1z,P2z,μ)]Redelimited-superscriptsubscript𝛾Γ¯MSsubscript𝑏𝑇𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑃1𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑧𝜇Rebig[hatgammaGammaoverlinerm MS(bT,x,P1z,P2z,mu)big] with different x𝑥x are accounted for. Comparisons of these averaged estimators with different choices of ΓΓGamma, perturbative matching accuracy, and momentum pairs, are shown in and. The fitting procedure for Im​[γΓMS¯]Imdelimited-superscriptsubscript𝛾Γ¯MSImbig[hatgammaGammaoverlinerm MSbig] is identical. Whereas the CS kernel is a real quantity, averages of Im​[γq,ΓMS¯]Imdelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝛾¯MS𝑞ΓImbig[hatgammaoverlinerm MSq,Gammabig] at different perturbative accuracies indicate a nonzero imaginary part as illustrated in . By comparing to the LO estimate, where the matching correction vanishes, it is clear that matching is a dominant source of the imaginary part. As discussed in , the imaginary part from the matching is attributed to bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT-dependent power corrections enhanced at small bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT and mitigated by uNLO and uNNLL corrections. Consistent with this explanation, for small bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, Im​[γq,ΓMS¯]Imdelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝛾¯MS𝑞ΓImbig[hatgammaoverlinerm MSq,Gammabig] at uNLO and uNNLL are reduced relative to all other orders of matching; as bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT increases and power corrections are suppressed, they approach NLO and NNLL results, respectively. However, uNLO and uNNLL accuracies still do not lead to values of Im​[γq,ΓMS¯]Imdelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝛾¯MS𝑞ΓImbig[hatgammaoverlinerm MSq,Gammabig] that are consistent with zero within the accessible range of bT​Pzsubscript𝑏𝑇superscript𝑃𝑧bTPz. This suggests that power corrections beyond those that have been accounted for by the unexpanded matching are relevant at the level of precision of this calculation. Since matching corrections with smallest expected power corrections are given by uNNLL, this accuracy is used for the final estimate of the CS kernel. Furthermore, considering both the larger qualitative difference between Im​[γq,ΓMS¯]Imdelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝛾¯MS𝑞ΓImbig[hatgammaoverlinerm MSq,Gammabig] for different accuracies and momenta, as well as the parametrically larger estimates of bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT-dependent power corrections compared to Re​[γq,ΓMS¯]Redelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝛾¯MS𝑞ΓRebig[hatgammaoverlinerm MSq,Gammabig], the central value of the CS kernel is determined from fits to Re​[γq,ΓMS¯,uNNLL]Redelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝛾¯MSuNNLL𝑞ΓRebig[hatgammaoverlinerm MS,,uNNLLq,Gammabig] while Im​[γq,ΓMS¯]Imdelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝛾¯MS𝑞ΓImbig[hatgammaoverlinerm MSq,Gammabig] is not treated as a direct source of systematic uncertainty. Finally, scale variation in resummed corrections around μ0=2​pzsubscript𝜇02superscript𝑝𝑧mu0=2pz, with pz∈x​Pz,(1−x)​Pzsuperscript𝑝𝑧𝑥superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑥superscript𝑃𝑧pzinxPz,(1-x)Pz, is not used to estimate the associated perturbative uncertainties. This choice is motivated by the range of pzsuperscript𝑝𝑧pz used to determine the CS kernel, and in particular because results at scales μ0/2subscript𝜇02mu0/2 are sensitive to nonperturbative effects. The significance of higher-order perturbative effects may instead be judged by comparing the final uNNLL CS kernel determination to those obtained with other accuracies, as shown in . The final CS kernel results of this work are summarized in Table. These results are shown as a function of bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT and compared with phenomenological determinations of the CS kernel in Fig. .
content_sections.IV Outlook: This work presents a numerical determination of the quark Collins-Soper kernel γqMS¯​(bT,μ=2GeV)superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑞¯MSsubscript𝑏𝑇𝜇times2gigaelectronvoltgammaqoverlinerm MS(bT,mu=2text,GeV) in the nonperturbative range of bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT corresponding to transverse momentum scales 240MeV≲qT≲1.6GeVless-than-or-similar-totimes240megaelectronvoltsubscript𝑞Tless-than-or-similar-totimes1.6gigaelectronvolt240text,MeVlesssim qTlesssim1.6text,GeV, through a lattice QCD calculation at a fixed lattice spacing and volume, quark masses corresponding to an approximately physical value of the pion mass mπ=148.8​MeVsubscript𝑚𝜋timesuncertain148.81megaelectronvoltmpi=148.8text,MeV, and uNNLL perturbative matching power corrections in LaMET. Additionally, this work presents improved estimates of systematic uncertainties associated with perturbative matching from LaMET, the associated power corrections, and mixing effects in staple-shaped operators using the RI/xMOMRIxMOMrm RI/xMOM renormalization scheme. While a complete quantification of systematic uncertainties would require performing lattice QCD calculations at multiple lattice spacings and at larger boosts or higher-order perturbative matching, the precision and control over systematic uncertainties achieved in this work is sufficient to preliminarily compare the CS kernel determination with phenomenological parameterizations of the kernel fit to experimental data. In Fig. the final determination is compared with the following parameterizations: Scimemi and Vladimirov (SV19)citebib.bib51, Bachetta et al. (Pavia19)citebib.bib52, the MAP Collaboration (MAPTMD22)citebib.bib55, Moos et al. (ART23)citebib.bib56, as well as an older parameterization based on the work of Brock, Landry, Nadolsky and Yuan (BLNY)citebib.bib44 and employed in recent code packages for resummation calculations relevant to precision electroweak measurementscitebib.bib111, bib.bib112. Within quantified uncertainties, the data agrees with all models in the range 0.12fm≲bT≲0.24fmless-than-or-similar-totimes0.12femtometersubscript𝑏𝑇less-than-or-similar-totimes0.24femtometer0.12text,fmlesssim bTlesssim0.24text,fm, with all but BLNY for 0.24fm≲bT≲0.6fmless-than-or-similar-totimes0.24femtometersubscript𝑏𝑇less-than-or-similar-totimes0.6femtometer0.24text,fmlesssim bTlesssim0.6text,fm, and with SV19, MAPTMD22 and ART23 for bT≳0.6fmgreater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑏𝑇times0.6femtometerbTgtrsim0.6text,fm. Finally, for bT≥0.6fmsubscript𝑏𝑇times0.6femtometerbTgeq0.6text,fm, the results are consistent with a constant, as suggested for the large-bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT behavior in Ref. citebib.bib108. Discretization artifacts and power corrections, both enhanced at small bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, will be studied in more detail in future work. More refined comparisons would also take into account the differences in the number of quark flavors and their masses between the lattice QCD determination and the global analyses, which lead to perturbative corrections described in Ref. citebib.bib113. The first-principles QCD calculations achieved in this work provide new constraints on the quark CS kernel, with better control of the associated systematic uncertainties. The results are complementary to those achieved experimentally and, once the continuum limit is taken, can be rigorously compared to phenomenological parameterizations of the CS kernel from current global analyses. Moreover, in future analyses, lattice QCD constraints could be used to constrain the parameterizations in joint fits with experimental data. Acknowledgements. sorted_papers.title: Lattice QCD calculation of the Collins-Soper kernel from quasi TMDPDFs
sorted_papers.abstract:
This work presents a lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD) calculation of the
nonperturbative Collins-Soper kernel, which describes the rapidity evolution of
quark transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions. The kernel
is extracted at transverse momentum scales in the range 400 MeV $< q_T < 1.7$
GeV in a calculation with dynamical fermions and quark masses corresponding to
a larger-than-physical pion mass, $m_\pi=538(1)$ MeV. It is found that
different approaches to extract the Collins-Soper kernel from the same
underlying lattice QCD matrix elements yield significantly different results
and uncertainty estimates, revealing that power corrections, such as those
associated with higher-twist effects, and perturbative matching between quasi
and light-cone beam functions, cannot be neglected.

sections.28: Akaike, IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control 19, 716 (1974).

qualitative: The Collins-Soper kernel governs the rapidity evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) and is nonperturbative at transverse momentum scales where bT ∼ qT⁻¹ ∼ ΛQCD⁻¹.
The kernel is essential for relating TMDPDFs determined at different rapidity scales and plays a key role in connecting experimental data and lattice QCD calculations.

In lattice QCD, the Collins-Soper kernel can be extracted from ratios of quasi TMDPDFs at different hadron momenta, providing a first-principles approach to nonperturbative determination of the kernel.

Different extraction methods from the same lattice matrix elements yield significantly different results and uncertainties, indicating that power corrections (e.g., higher-twist effects) and perturbative matching between quasi and light-cone beam functions are non-negligible.

The analysis accounts for one-loop perturbative matching, renormalization mixing between different TMDPDFs, finite-volume effects, and statistical limitations, representing an improvement over prior lattice studies.
quantitative: The Collins-Soper kernel is calculated in the transverse momentum range 400 MeV < qT < 1.7 GeV.
The lattice calculation uses dynamical fermions with a pion mass of mπ = 538(1) MeV, which is larger than the physical value.
The kernel is extracted using ratios of quasi TMDPDFs at different hadron momenta, with one-loop perturbative matching between quasi and light-cone beam functions applied.
The analysis includes full treatment of renormalization mixing among different TMDPDFs and improved handling of power corrections and finite-volume effects.

authors: Phiala Shanahan, Michael Wagman, Yong Zhao

content_sections.I Introduction: Transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) describe the intrinsic transverse momentum qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT of the partonic constituents of hadronscitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3. These non-perturbative functions can be accessed directly in high-energy scattering processes such as Drell-Yan production and semi-inclusive deep-inelastic scattering with small transverse hadron momentum qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qTcitebib.bib4, bib.bib5, and indirectly through other processes such as studies of hadrons in jetscitebib.bib6, bib.bib7. Significant efforts are underway to improve constraints on TMDPDFs both from current and planned experimentscitebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12 and through theory calculations in the framework of lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD)citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22 using approaches such as large-momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25 or the Lorentz-invariant method based on ratios of TMDPDFscitebib.bib13. TMDPDFs fiTMD​(x,bT,μ,ζ)superscriptsubscript𝑓𝑖TMD𝑥subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇𝜁fitextTMD(x,bT,mu,zeta), defined for a parton of flavor i𝑖i in a given hadron state, are functions of the longitudinal momentum fraction x𝑥x of the parton, the Fourier conjugate bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT of qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT, the virtuality scale μ𝜇mu, and the rapidity scale ζ𝜁zeta which is related to the hadron momentum. While the μ𝜇mu-evolution of TMDPDFs is perturbative for perturbative scales μ𝜇mu and ζ𝜁zeta, the ζ𝜁zeta-evolution is governed by the Collins-Soper evolution kernel (or anomalous dimension) γζi​(μ,bT)superscriptsubscript𝛾𝜁𝑖𝜇subscript𝑏𝑇gammazetai(mu,bT), which is nonperturbative for scales bT∼qT−1∼ΛQCD−1similar-tosubscript𝑏𝑇superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑇1similar-tosubscriptsuperscriptΛ1QCDbTsim qT-1simLambda-1QCD, even if both μ𝜇mu and ζ𝜁zeta are perturbative. Constraints on the kernel γζi​(μ,bT)superscriptsubscript𝛾𝜁𝑖𝜇subscript𝑏𝑇gammazetai(mu,bT) in the nonperturbative region are necessary in order to relate TMDPDFs determined from experiment or lattice QCD at different scales. Recently, it was shown in Refs. citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28 that the Collins-Soper kernel can be calculated from ratios of quasi TMDPDFs at different hadron momenta, quantities which are both calculable in lattice QCD and which can be related to TMDPDFscitebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib27, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. This provides a pathway to first-principles QCD calculations of the kernel in the nonperturbative region, which will provide valuable complementary information to constraints from global analyses of experimental data. This prospect has motivated a series of proof-of-principle lattice QCD investigations of the Collins-Soper kernel both directlycitebib.bib19, bib.bib18, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22 through the approach of Refs. citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28 and via related prescriptionscitebib.bib33. In this work, a direct calculation of the Collins-Soper kernel is presented, based on a lattice QCD study with dynamical fermions and quark masses corresponding to a larger-than-physical pion mass mπ=538​subscript𝑚𝜋5381mpi=538MeV, and a single value of the lattice spacing and volume. The kernel is extracted at transverse momentum scales in the range 400MeV<qT<1.7absentsubscript𝑞𝑇1.7<qT<1.7GeV and compared with phenomenological parametrizations and existing lattice QCD calculations. This analysis includes several advances over previous lattice QCD studies of the Collins-Soper kernel via the same approach. In particular, matching of quasi TMDPDFs and TMDPDFs is performed to one-loop order, the mixing of different TMDPDFs under renormalization is fully accounted for, and the analysis includes improved treatments of power corrections and systematic effects arising from the finite lattice volume and various statistical limitations of the calculation. It is found that different approaches to extract the Collins-Soper kernel from the same underlying lattice QCD matrix elements yield significantly different results and uncertainty estimates, revealing that power corrections, such as those associated with higher-twist effects, and perturbative matching between quasi and light-cone beam functions, cannot be neglected. The method by which the Collins-Soper kernel can be computed following Refs. citebib.bib26, bib.bib27 is detailed in Section. The lattice QCD calculation is reported in Section, while a summary and outlook is provided in Section.

To ensure that the matching factor ℛ𝒪γ4​ΓMS¯subscriptsuperscriptℛ¯MSsubscript𝒪superscript𝛾4ΓRoverlineMSOgamma4Gamma is in the perturbative region, both Z𝒪γ4​ΓRI′/MOMsubscriptsuperscript𝑍superscriptRI′MOMsubscript𝒪superscript𝛾4ΓZRIprime/MOMOgamma4Gamma and ℛ𝒪γ4​ΓMS¯subscriptsuperscriptℛ¯MSsubscript𝒪superscript𝛾4ΓRoverlineMSOgamma4Gamma should be computed at a scale bTR≪ΛQCD−1much-less-thansuperscriptsubscript𝑏𝑇𝑅superscriptsubscriptΛQCD1bTRllLambdarm QCD-1. The factor R𝑅tildeR is included to cancel such divergences. The specific choice of R𝑅tildeR (including the choices of pRsubscript𝑝𝑅tildepR and b→Tsubscript→𝑏𝑇vecbT and b→TRsubscriptsuperscript→𝑏𝑅𝑇vecbRT orientations) does not affect the value of γζqsubscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑞𝜁gammaqzeta.
content_sections.III Numerical study: The Collins-Soper kernel is computed via Eq. in a lattice QCD calculation with four dynamical quark flavors, using an ensemble of gauge field configurations generated by the MILC collaboration with the one-loop Symanzik improved gauge action and the highly improved staggered quark action. A single ensemble is studied, with a lattice volume of L3×T=483×64superscript𝐿3𝑇superscript48364L3times T=483times 64, a lattice spacing corresponding to a=0.12𝑎0.12a=0.12 fm, and sea quark masses tuned to approximately match the physical quark masses in nature; see Ref. citebib.bib37 for further details of the ensemble generation. Calculations are performed in a partially-quenched mixed-action setup, with the tree-level 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a)-improved Wilson clover fermion action used for the valence quarks, with κ=0.1241𝜅0.1241kappa=0.1241 tuned such that the pion mass is mπ=538​subscript𝑚𝜋5381mpi=538MeV. The gauge fields used in the calculation have been subjected to Wilson flow to flow-time 𝔱=1.0𝔱1.0mathfrakt=1.0citebib.bib38, to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio in the numerical results1Note that the flowed gauge fields were also used for constructing D̸italic-D̸notD. . The following sections detail the computation of each element of γζqsubscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑞𝜁gammaqzeta.

For those operator structures where the RI′/MOMsuperscriptRI′MOMRIprime/MOM renormalization factors have been computed at other choices of pRsubscript𝑝𝑅pR, this yields indistinguishable results for the Collins-Soper kernel compared with results obtained using the weighted averaging procedure over momenta which is employed in Ref. citebib.bib18. The quasi beam functions with both Dirac structures are thus treated on equal footing in this work.
content_sections.III Numerical study III.3 Renormalized q: Modified MS¯¯MSoverlineMS-renormalized quasi beam functions are computed via Eq. from the bare quasi beam functions and MS¯¯MSoverlineMS renormalization factors calculated as described in Secs. and ; the uncertainties of the two components are combined in quadrature. While for clarity all equations in this section are expressed for renormalized quasi beam functions defined with the Dirac structure γ4superscript𝛾4gamma4, both Bγ4MS¯subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾4BoverlineMSgamma4 and Bγ3MS¯subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾3BoverlineMSgamma3 are computed and analyzed independently. The real and imaginary parts of the renormalized quasi beam functions should be symmetric and antisymmetric functions of bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz respectively in the η→∞→𝜂etarightarrowinfty limit. The numerical results obtained in this work, however, show significant departures from these expectations at finite η𝜂eta as was also observed in the quenched calculation of Ref. citebib.bib18. The bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz-dependence of the asymmetry in the absolute value of the renormalized quasi beam functions is illustrated in Fig. . This asymmetry can be understood as an incomplete cancellation of the Wilson-line self-energy correction proportional to e−V​(bT)​bzsuperscript𝑒𝑉subscript𝑏𝑇superscript𝑏𝑧e-V(bT),bz between the MS¯¯MSoverlineMS renormalization factor and the bare quasi beam function, where V​(bT)𝑉subscript𝑏𝑇V(bT) is the static quark-antiquark potential at distance bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT. Such an effect yields a bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz-dependent asymmetry proportional to e−Δ​bzsuperscript𝑒Δsuperscript𝑏𝑧e-Delta bz, depending on an asymmetry parameter Δ=−V​(bT)+V​(bTR)Δ𝑉subscript𝑏𝑇𝑉superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑇𝑅Delta=-V(bT)+V(bTR). This is in fact a good model of the asymmetry observed in the numerical calculations of this work; fits of |Bγ4MS¯​(μ,−bz,b→T,a,η,Pz)|/|Bγ4MS¯​(μ,bz,b→T,a,η,Pz)|subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾4𝜇superscript𝑏𝑧subscript→𝑏𝑇𝑎𝜂superscript𝑃𝑧subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾4𝜇superscript𝑏𝑧subscript→𝑏𝑇𝑎𝜂superscript𝑃𝑧|BoverlineMSgamma4(mu,-bz,vecbT,a,eta,Pz)|/|BoverlineMSgamma4(mu,bz,vecbT,a,eta,Pz)| (and the analogous expression constructed from Bγ3MS¯subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾3BoverlineMSgamma3) to this functional form, fit separately for fixed values of bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, η𝜂eta, and Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz, achieve acceptable goodness-of-fit with an average χ2/d. o. f. =0.56superscript𝜒2d. o. f.0.56chi2/textd. o. f. =0.56. These fits, and the resulting values of the asymmetry parameter ΔΔDelta, are illustrated in Fig. and in Appendix. Here, the uncertainties in the factor eΔ​|bz|superscript𝑒Δsuperscript𝑏𝑧eDelta|bz| and the quasi beam functions are added in quadrature and, after asymmetry correction, the more precise results for beam functions with bz>0superscript𝑏𝑧0bz>0 (which involve shorter Wilson lines) are mirrored to bz<0superscript𝑏𝑧0bz<0. This expectation is satisfied within uncertainties for the numerical investigations of this work as illustrated in Fig. ; for use in the calculation of the Collins-Soper kernel, a weighted averagecitebib.bib40 over possible choices of bTRsuperscriptsubscript𝑏𝑇𝑅bTR in the window a≪bTR≪ΛQCD−1much-less-than𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑇𝑅much-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptΛQCD1all bTRllLambdatextQCD-1 is thus implemented, performed precisely as detailed in Appendix C of Ref. citebib.bib19. Figures showing B¯γ4MS¯subscriptsuperscript¯𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾4overlineBoverlineMSgamma4 and B¯γ3MS¯subscriptsuperscript¯𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾3overlineBoverlineMSgamma3 are provided in Appendix. The contributions to these quantities from mixing between operators with different Dirac structures is generally found to be less than 10%percent1010%; see Fig. for an illustration. content_sections.III Numerical study III.4 Collins-Soper: To determine the Collins-Soper kernel via Eq. from the averaged asymmetry-corrected modified MS¯¯MSoverlineMS-renormalized quasi beam functions B¯γ4MS¯superscriptsubscript¯𝐵superscript𝛾4¯MSoverlineBgamma4overlineMS and B¯γ3MS¯superscriptsubscript¯𝐵superscript𝛾3¯MSoverlineBgamma3overlineMS, defined in the previous section, requires a Fourier transform of the quasi beam functions in bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz. As is clear from Fig. , however, the bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz-range of the data is not sufficient for the tails of the quasi beam functions at large |bz|superscript𝑏𝑧|bz| to decay to plateaus consistent with zero, particularly at the largest bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT and smallest Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz values used in this numerical study. As such, it is to be expected that a discrete Fourier transform (DFT) will have significant systematic uncertainties from the truncation of the data in Pz​bzsuperscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝑏𝑧Pzbz; details of a DFT analysis of the quasi beam functions are presented in Appendix. Instead, Fourier transforms are taken after fitting the quasi beam functions to functional forms that allow extrapolation in bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz. This approach trades the systematic uncertainties of a DFT for model-dependence in the fit form used to extrapolate the quasi beam functions. While this model-dependence is difficult to quantify rigorously, this approach allows the physical expectation that the quasi beam functions should decay smoothly at large |bz|superscript𝑏𝑧|bz| to be incorporated (a DFT effectively models the beam functions as zero outside the bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz-range in which lattice QCD results are computed). In particular, the quasi beam functions are modeled as a sum of polynomials in bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz times Gaussian functions, which provide an appropriate basis, since it is expected that the quasi beam functions should be analytic functions of bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz (the bz→0→superscript𝑏𝑧0bzto 0 limit at fixed bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT does not introduce additional divergences), and yield high-quality fits with few free parameters. Specifically, for each choice of bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT and Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz, the real and imaginary parts of the quasi beam function B¯γ4MS¯subscriptsuperscript¯𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾4overlineBoverlineMSgamma4 (and independently B¯γ3MS¯subscriptsuperscript¯𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾3overlineBoverlineMSgamma3) are fit with even and odd functions of bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz respectively, defined as fRe​(σ,rn;bz)subscript𝑓Re𝜎subscript𝑟𝑛superscript𝑏𝑧displaystyle ftextRe(sigma,rn;bz) =exp​[−(bz)2/(2​σ2)]​∑n=0nmaxrn​(bz)2​nabsentexpdelimited-superscriptsuperscript𝑏𝑧22superscript𝜎2superscriptsubscript𝑛0subscript𝑛maxsubscript𝑟𝑛superscriptsuperscript𝑏𝑧2𝑛displaystyle=textexp[-(bz)2/(2sigma2)]sumn=0ntextmaxrn(bz)2n fIm​(σ,rn;bz)subscript𝑓Im𝜎subscript𝑟𝑛superscript𝑏𝑧displaystyle ftextIm(sigma,rn;bz) =exp​[−(bz)2/(2​σ2)]​∑n=0nmaxin​(bz)2​n+1. absentexpdelimited-superscriptsuperscript𝑏𝑧22superscript𝜎2superscriptsubscript𝑛0subscript𝑛maxsubscript𝑖𝑛superscriptsuperscript𝑏𝑧2𝑛1displaystyle=textexp[-(bz)2/(2sigma2)]sumn=0ntextmaxin(bz)2n+1. The value of nmaxsubscript𝑛maxntextmax is chosen to minimize the Akaike information criterion (AIC)citebib.bib41 and corresponds to nmax∈2,3,4subscript𝑛max234ntextmaxin2,3,4 for all cases. The fits with these optimal values of nmaxsubscript𝑛maxntextmax are of high quality in all cases, with an average χ2/d. o. f. =0.41superscript𝜒2d. o. f.0.41chi2/textd. o. f. =0.41. The resulting models of the quasi beam functions are denoted Bγ4MS¯subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾4hatBoverlineMSgamma4 (and, correspondingly Bγ3MS¯subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾3hatBoverlineMSgamma3), and are illustrated in Fig. (with further examples provided in Appendix). Finally, in terms of the models Bγ4MS¯subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSsuperscript𝛾4hatBoverlineMSgamma4, the relation defining the Collins-Soper kernel in Eq. is realized as γζq​(μ,bT;P1z,P2z,x)subscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑞𝜁𝜇subscript𝑏𝑇superscriptsubscript𝑃1𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑧𝑥displaystylehatgammaqzeta(mu,bT;P1z,P2z,x) ≡1ln⁡(P1z/P2z)ln[CnsTMD​(μ,x​P2z)CnsTMD​(μ,x​P1z)displaystyleequivfrac1ln(Pz1/Pz2)lnBiggr[fracCTMDtextns(mu,xP2z)CTMDtextns(mu,xP1z) ×∫dbz​e−i​bz​x​P1z​P1z​Bγ4MS¯​(μ,bz,bT,P1z)∫dbz​e−i​bz​x​P2z​P2z​Bγ4MS¯​(μ,bz,bT,P2z)],displaystyle!times!fracint!dbze-ibz!xP1zP1zhatBoverlineMSgamma4(mu,bz,bT,P1z)int!dbze-ibz!xP2z!P2zhatBoverlineMSgamma4(mu,bz,bT,P2z)Biggr], which coincides with γζq​(μ,bT)subscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑞𝜁𝜇subscript𝑏𝑇gammaqzeta(mu,bT) up to power corrections such as higher-twist corrections in the factorization formula for the quasi TMDPDF, and discretization artifacts, which introduce the dependence on P1zsuperscriptsubscript𝑃1𝑧P1z, P2zsuperscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑧P2z, and x𝑥x. One approach to determine γζq​(μ,bT)subscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑞𝜁𝜇subscript𝑏𝑇gammaqzeta(mu,bT) from γζq​(μ,bT;P1z,P2z,x)subscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑞𝜁𝜇subscript𝑏𝑇superscriptsubscript𝑃1𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑧𝑥hatgammaqzeta(mu,bT;P1z,P2z,x) is to model, fit, and subtract, these various artifacts. However, the most straightforward models of these effects do not provide good fits to the numerical data of this study, as detailed in Appendix. Instead, since the contamination in γζqsubscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑞𝜁hatgammaqzeta will be different at each choice of P1zsuperscriptsubscript𝑃1𝑧P1z, P2zsuperscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑧P2z, and x𝑥x, and the effects can be expected to be larger2The matching coefficient includes large logarithms of x​Piz𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝑃𝑧𝑖xPzi at small x𝑥x, while the quasi beam functions at x→0→𝑥0xto 0 and x→1→𝑥1xto 1 are sensitive to the long-range correlations in bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz and are thus affected by the truncation of the data in Pz​bzsuperscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝑏𝑧Pzbz. In addition, the power corrections are expected to be enhanced at small x𝑥x. at large and small values of x𝑥x and at small values of Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz, the variation of γζqsubscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑞𝜁hatgammaqzeta over these choices is used to define an estimate of the systematic uncertainty. Precisely, a best value for the Collins-Soper kernel is determined from γζqsubscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑞𝜁hatgammaqzeta via a multi-step procedure. First, the largest window of x𝑥x is determined for which the data for all choices of the pair P1z,P2zsuperscriptsubscript𝑃1𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑧P1z,P2z are consistent with a common constant value. In practice this region is defined as the largest window in which a constant fit to the data at a set of discrete x𝑥x points has a χ2/d. o. f. ≤1superscript𝜒2d. o. f.1chi2/textd. o. f. leq 1. The central value and uncertainty are defined as the median and the 68% confidence interval of the union of the bootstrap data in that x𝑥x window, including all P1z,P2zsuperscriptsubscript𝑃1𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑧P1z,P2z pairs. The result of this procedure is robust to changes in the discretization of x𝑥x, for sufficiently fine discretization scales (100 points spanning 02superscript𝜒2d. o. f.2chi2/textd. o. f. >2. In the case of the “bz=0/bT=0superscript𝑏𝑧0subscript𝑏𝑇0bz=0/bT=0, bare” approach, bare quasi beam functions with η/a=14𝜂𝑎14eta/a=14, which is the largest extent studied in this work for all Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz, are used in the analysis. Clearly, although the same quasi beam functions are used, the Collins-Soper kernel determined via each of these approaches is very different, and many of the results are inconsistent with the best results of this study at several standard deviations, with uncertainties as much as an order of magnitude smaller. This is to be expected if the effects of higher-order matching, renormalization and mixing, and power corrections, are significant, as each of the approaches listed above treats one or more of these systematic effects differently than in the primary analysis presented here.
content_sections.IV Outlook: This work presents a determination of the Collins-Soper kernel from a dynamical lattice QCD calculation following the approach of Refs. citebib.bib26, bib.bib27. Several systematic uncertainties remain to be addressed; in particular, the quark masses used correspond to an unphysically-large pion mass of mπ=538​subscript𝑚𝜋5381mpi=538MeV, and the results are obtained using a single ensemble of gauge field configurations such that effects from the discretization and finite lattice volume cannot be fully quantified. A fully model-independent calculation will require these systematics to be addressed, lattice QCD calculations to be performed over a larger range of Pz​bzsuperscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝑏𝑧Pzbz to eliminate the need to extrapolate the quasi beam functions to large |bz|superscript𝑏𝑧|bz| and enable the DFT approach to be used, and larger values of Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz to be included to reduce the contributions from power corrections and higher-twist effects which dominate the uncertainties of this calculation. With these caveats in mind, the results of this work may be compared with phenomenological extractions of the Collins-Soper kernel, as shown in Fig. . The lattice QCD and phenomenological determinations are broadly consistent at large bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, with clear deviations at the smallest bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT values studied; discretization effects are expected to be largest at small bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT and might be relevant for understanding this effect. It is clear that, while challenging to achieve computationally, future fully-controlled calculations by this approach have the potential to differentiate different models of the Collins-Soper kernel and will provide important input for the analysis of low-energy SIDIS data and the determinations of the TMDPDFs. In considering the prospects for such future controlled determinations of the Collins-Soper kernel from lattice QCD, it is informative to contrast the results of this study with those of other lattice QCD investigations; a comparison of existing calculationscitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22 is provided in Fig. . All dynamical calculations use quark masses resulting in similar values of the pion mass to that of the calculation presented here (ranging from the lightest ensemble with mπ=350subscript𝑚𝜋350mpi=350MeV in Ref. citebib.bib22 to mπ=547subscript𝑚𝜋547mpi=547 MeV in Ref. citebib.bib20), while the quenched calculation of Ref. citebib.bib19, in which the kernel should not depend on the valence quark masses since it is independent of the external state, is performed at mπ=1.207subscript𝑚𝜋1.207mpi=1.207 GeV. Each calculation uses a slightly different approach to constrain the Collins-Soper kernel from quasi beam functions. In particular, the “Hermite/Bernstein” approach is followed in Ref. citebib.bib19 (“SWZ”), the calculation of Ref. citebib.bib20 (“LPC”) uses the “bz=0superscript𝑏𝑧0bz=0, bare” approach, that of Ref. citebib.bib21 (“Regensburg/NMSU”) uses an approach similar to the “bz=0superscript𝑏𝑧0bz=0, bare” approach but with NLO matching, and Ref. citebib.bib22 (“ETMC/PKU) applies the “bz=0/bT=0superscript𝑏𝑧0subscript𝑏𝑇0bz=0/bT=0, bare” approach. While the various calculations exhibit similar dependence on bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, there are some significant discrepancies between the numerical results, and a wide range of uncertainty estimates. Given the analysis of Sec. , this is to be expected; even when the same quasi beam function data is used, following the various “bz=0superscript𝑏𝑧0bz=0” approaches and the approach presented here result in significant systematic differences, and significantly different uncertainty estimates. Since Refs. citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22 all use somewhat larger maximum Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz values than that of the present study, the effects of power corrections and higher-twist contamination can be expected to be smaller than those found in Sec. , but these effects, together with the difference between NLO and LO matching illustrated in Appendix, could nevertheless be responsible for the discrepancies visible in Fig. . Clearly, a fully-controlled determination of the Collins-Soper kernel from lattice QCD will require NLO or even higher-order matching or resummation and a treatment of power corrections that is more robust than that achieved in any of the studies performed to date. The analysis presented here constitutes an important step towards that goal, revealing clearly that these important sources of systematic uncertainty cannot be neglected. Acknowledgements. sorted_papers.title: Determination of the Collins-Soper Kernel from Lattice QCD
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present lattice results for the non-perturbative Collins-Soper (CS)
kernel, which describes the energy-dependence of transverse momentum-dependent
parton distributions (TMDs). The CS kernel is extracted from the ratios of
first Mellin moments of quasi-TMDs evaluated at different nucleon momenta.The
analysis is done with dynamical $N_f=2+1$ clover fermions for the CLS ensemble
H101 ($a=0.0854\,\mathrm{fm}$, $m_{\pi}=m_K=422\,\mathrm{MeV}$). The computed
CS kernel is in good agreement with experimental extractions and previous
lattice studies.

sections.3: Abdul Khalek et al., Science Requirements and Detector Concepts for the Electron-Ion Collider: EIC Yellow Report, 2103.05419.
sections.7: Constantinou et al., Parton distributions and lattice QCD calculations: toward 3D structure, 2006.08636.
sections.9: Dudek et al., Physics Opportunities with the 12 GeV Upgrade at Jefferson Lab, Eur
sections.11: Zhang et al., Lattice QCD Calculations of Transverse-Momentum-Dependent Soft Function through Large-Momentum Effective Theory, Phys
sections.13: Aschenauer et al., The RHIC SPIN Program: Achievements and Future Opportunities, 1501.01220.
sections.18: Vladimirov, Structure of rapidity divergences in multi-parton scattering soft factors, JHEP 04 (2018) 045, [1707.07606].
sections.29: Bruno et al., Simulation of QCD with N=f{}_{f}= 2 ++ 1 flavors of non-perturbatively improved Wilson fermions, JHEP 02 (2015) 043, [1411.3982].
sections.41: Zhang et al., Lattice QCD Calculations of Transverse-Momentum-Dependent Soft Function through Large-Momentum Effective Theory, Phys

qualitative: The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel, also known as the rapidity anomalous dimension, is a fundamental nonperturbative function in QCD that governs the rapidity evolution of transverse momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs).
The CS kernel characterizes the energy-dependence of TMDs and is universal across different processes, depending only on the transverse distance b.

Lattice QCD provides a nonperturbative method to compute the CS kernel, offering complementary information to experimental extractions, especially at large transverse distances where experiments are insensitive.

The CS kernel can be extracted from ratios of first Mellin moments of quasi-TMDs (qTMDs) evaluated at different nucleon momenta, exploiting the fact that the soft factor and other nonperturbative contributions cancel in such ratios.

The method relies on the factorization theorem for qTMDs in the large hadron momentum limit, where qTMDs are related to physical TMDs via a perturbative coefficient function and a nonperturbative soft factor.

A key assumption in the approach is that the function encoding the x-dependence of TMDs (in the moment space) is nearly independent of the transverse distance b, which is considered a good approximation based on prior theoretical and phenomenological studies.
The use of first moments of qTMDs avoids the ill-defined Fourier transform into momentum-fraction space (x-space), which is a major challenge in direct qTMD analysis.
The CLS collaboration uses open boundary conditions in temporal and spatial directions to prevent topological freezing at fine lattice spacings, ensuring ergodicity in lattice simulations.
The method presented is more efficient than previous approaches (e.g., refs. citebib.bib25, bib.bib26) for extracting the CS kernel, though it is less general for studying other TMD properties.
quantitative: The lattice ensemble used is H101 with lattice spacing a = 0.0854 fm and pion mass m_π = m_K = 422 MeV.
The CS kernel is extracted from ratios of first Mellin moments of qTMDs at different nucleon momenta.
The CS kernel is found to be in good agreement with experimental extractions and previous lattice studies.
Experimental data from Drell-Yan and SIDIS are currently insensitive to the CS kernel for b ≳ 2 GeV, and even the future EIC will only constrain it up to b ≳ 3.5 GeV.
Lattice calculations are expected to reliably determine the CS kernel at b ∼ 5 GeV within a few years.
The method assumes that the x-dependent function in the moment factorization theorem is approximately independent of b, introducing a systematic error considered smaller than other uncertainties.

authors:
Maximilian Schlemmer, Alexey Vladimirov, Christian Zimmermann, Michael
  Engelhardt and Andreas Sch\"afer

content_sections.1 Introduction: The last decades witnessed a rapid increase in our understanding of hadron structure, leading to ever more ambitious goals, such as the investigation of multi-dimensional elements of nucleon structure, which are parameterized by functions like generalized parton distributions (GPDs) and transverse momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs). These functions contain significantly more information than collinear parton distributions. Determining the multi-dimensional structure is the goal for many modern (such as COMPASS at CERN citebib.bib1, RHIC at BNL citebib.bib2, and JLab12 citebib.bib3) and future (such as the Electron-Ion Collider (EIC) citebib.bib4) experimental facilities. Even so, the precise determination of TMDs and GPDs is exceedingly difficult from experimental data alone. Fortunately, lattice QCD has reached a level of maturity that allows supplementing the experimental data in many ways. This progress is made possible not only by Exaflop computing becoming a reality but also by the theoretical progress exploring new avenues to extract parton distributions from lattice simulations. As this field is very much in a state of flux and opinions differ strongly on which approach is the most successful, we cite here only a few relevant papers citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8. In many cases, lattice QCD and experimental measurements give access to complementary information, making a combined strategy very promising. In this paper, we discuss one such case, namely the Collins-Soper (CS) kernel citebib.bib9, also known as the rapidity anomalous dimension citebib.bib10. The CS kernel is a fundamental nonperturbative function that characterizes the QCD vacuum citebib.bib11. It appears as the universal rapidity evolution kernel for TMDs and can be extracted by comparing experimental data at different scales. The CS kernel depends on transverse distance b𝑏b, characterizing the relevant nonlocal parton correlator. The comparison of the most recent extractions made in refs. citebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14 demonstrates that presently available data (from Drell-Yan and Semi-Inclusive Deep-Inelastic scattering (SIDIS) reactions) are not sensitive to the value of the CS kernel at b≳2greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝑏2bgtrsim 2 GeV. The situation will certainly improve with inclusion in the fit of on-going and future measurements, but even the EIC will hardly restrict the CS kernel for b≳3.5greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝑏3.5bgtrsim 3.5 GeV. By contrast, lattice calculations for b∼5similar-to𝑏5bsim 5 GeV with reliable uncertainty could be possible within a few years. Several ways to extract the CS kernel were suggested within the last years citebib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18. All these suggestions are based on the measurement of quasi-transverse momentum-dependent parton distributions (qTMDs) – the correlation of two quark fields in a hadron wave function connected by a staple-shaped gauge link positioned in a space-like plane. It has been shown that in the regime of large hadron momentum and large staple length, a qTMD is a convolution of a physical TMD, a perturbative coefficient function, and a soft factor. The latter is an unknown nonperturbative function, depending only on b𝑏b, that cancels (together with other multiplicative factors, particularly the Wilson line renormalization factors) in the ratio of qTMDs. The ratios of qTMDs give access to several different pieces of information. In particular, ratios of qTMDs at distinct hadron momenta (with the other parameters being identical) are exclusively sensitive to the CS kernel citebib.bib15, bib.bib18. In this work, we evaluate such ratios and extract the CS kernel using the approach suggested in ref. citebib.bib18. One of the fundamental difficulties in the evaluation of quasi-distributions is the necessity to perform a Fourier transformation into momentum-fraction space (introducing the variable x𝑥x). This transformation is notoriously ill-defined, already for one-dimensional observables (for a review of recent developments, see citebib.bib19, bib.bib20), and even more so for qTMDs, where one should respect an entangled hierarchy of several scales. To by-pass this severe complication, we study the first moment of qTMDs (i. e. , the integral over x𝑥x). These are much simpler observables that were already studied on the lattice before the formulation of factorization theorems for qTMDs citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24. Thus, the methodology of the determination of the first moments of qTMDs is well established. On the theory side, qTMDs are related to TMDs by the factorization theorem, which is valid in the regime of large hadron momentum. The integral over the factorized expression gives us the factorization theorem of the moments, which contains an unknown function that accumulates the information about the x𝑥x-dependence of TMDs. The central aspect of the present approach is the assumption that this function is almost independent of b𝑏b. This is a model assumption. However, all previous studies (theoretical and phenomenological) show that it is a very good approximation. We expect that a systematic error introduced by such an assumption is much smaller than our approach’s other uncertainties. However, let us stress that our method has a limited scope of application and is less valuable for determining other properties of TMDs. In contrast, the approaches used in citebib.bib25, bib.bib26 are more general. As members of the CLS (Coordinated Lattice Simulations) collaboration, we subscribe to its general strategy of focusing on controlling the continuum limit. As the lattice spacing is reduced, standard QCD simulation algorithms suffer from critical slowing down, meaning that an increasing Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) simulation time is required to sample the configuration space fully. Suppose gauge (and fermion) fields fulfill (anti)periodic boundary conditions in all directions. In that case, the simulation will eventually become stuck in a fixed topological sector, and ergodicity is lost, typically for lattice spacing a<0.05𝑎0.05a<0.05 fm. This effect is known as “topological freezing” citebib.bib27. To avoid the “topological freezing”, the CLS collaboration uses open boundary conditions for lattices with fine spacing. So far, the CLS collaboration has generated about 50 different ensembles, which together allow to reliably control all systematic errors of results extrapolated to the physical point. In this paper, we present results for just one of these ensembles labeled H101. More ensembles and the extrapolation to the physical point will be analyzed in future work. This paper focuses on analyzing the applicability of the method proposed in citebib.bib18 and demonstrates that it is more efficient than the methods used in the refs. citebib.bib25 and citebib.bib26. The paper is partitioned into three main sections. In sec. we review the factorization theorem for qTMDs and explain the theoretical setup for our study. The theoretical peculiarities and assumptions of the CS kernel determination from the first moment of qTMDs are described in subsec. . Sec. is devoted to the details of our lattice analysis. In sec. the extraction of the CS kernel is presented. Subsec. presents technical details of the extraction, while we discuss systematic uncertainties in subsec. . The results are given in subsec. . The paper concludes with section.
content_sections.2 Formalism: In this section, we introduce the notation and provide theory details used to determine the CS kernel. For the derivation of the presented expressions cf. ref. citebib.bib18.

Essentially, one needs large hadron momentum, large longitudinal size of the contour L𝐿L, and fixed transverse size b𝑏b. The parameter ℓℓell must not be too large to guarantee the ℓℓell-independence of the function ΨΨPsi. The presence of unknown nonperturbative factors prevents the direct determination of TMDs from the lattice matrix element . To eliminate the unknown and singular factor ΨΨPsi we consider a ratio of W𝑊W’s evaluated at the same value of b𝑏b but for different momenta. In this case, the functions ΨΨPsi cancel, and the result is expressed entirely in terms of the physical TMDs. Note that the ratio is independent of μ𝜇mu. The factorization theorems derived in refs. citebib.bib16, bib.bib17 are equivalent to . The only difference is that the authors of refs. citebib.bib16, bib.bib17 consider the qTMD together with a certain soft factor Sbentsubscript𝑆bentStextbent, which is equivalent to the division of by Sbentsubscript𝑆bentStextbent. The factor Sbentsubscript𝑆bentStextbent is constructed such that it cancels ΨΨPsi in the perturbative regime. Performing the inverse Fourier transformation, one determines the physical TMD distribution from the combination of qTMD and Sbentsubscript𝑆bentStextbent. This approach gives access to the full TMD. However, it contains several fundamental complications. The two main complications are – the absence of proof that Ψ/Sbent=1Ψsubscript𝑆bent1Psi/Stextbent=1 nonperturbatively and the fact that the inverse Fourier integration incorporates values of ℓℓell that violate the requirements of the factorization theorem . We expect these complications to be resolved in the future. For this work, the dependence on x𝑥x is irrelevant since we are only interested in determining the CS kernel. We extract the CS-kernel from the ratios evaluated at fixed ℓℓell.

Let us mention that in the case of the Sivers function, the integrals are not sign-definite, and thus the ratios are singular at certain points (see fig. (right)). This results in the enormous error band for 𝐫Siverssubscript𝐫SiversrtextSivers, and indicates possible issues in an application of the suggested method in this case.
