To determine the constant 𝐌f←h[Γ]subscriptsuperscript𝐌delimited-Γ←𝑓ℎM[Gamma]fleftarrow h we normalize K𝐾K computed on the lattice to the perturbation theory values with b𝑏b in the regime where perturbation theory and the factorization formula are applicable. That requires simultaneously b≫1/P+much-greater-than𝑏1superscript𝑃bgg 1/P+ and b≪1/ΛQCDmuch-less-than𝑏1subscriptΛQCDbll 1/LambdatextQCD. We select b∼1​GeV−1similar-to𝑏1superscriptGeV1bsim 1,textnormalGeV-1 for which the perturbative series converges well citebib.bib15, bib.bib33. For normalization we use the NLO value in the resummed form citebib.bib32, bib.bib12. The normalization procedure introduces a correlated systematic uncertainty. Assuming that 𝐫𝐫r (𝐌𝐌M) has variance δ​𝐫𝛿𝐫deltar (δ​𝐌𝛿𝐌deltaM) we compute the variance for K𝐾K as δ​K=δ​𝐫𝐫​1ln⁡(P1+/P2+)=8​CF​αs4​π​δ​𝐌𝐫. 𝛿𝐾𝛿𝐫𝐫1superscriptsubscript𝑃1superscriptsubscript𝑃28subscript𝐶𝐹subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋𝛿𝐌𝐫displaystyledelta K=fracdeltarrfrac1ln(P1+/P2+)=8CFfracalphas4pifracdeltaMr. Considering the phenomenological extractions shown in figs. , we obtain δ​K∼0.15similar-to𝛿𝐾0.15delta Ksim 0.15 for the unpolarized proton case, and δ​K∼0.06similar-to𝛿𝐾0.06delta Ksim 0.06 for the unpolarized pion case. In the case of the Sivers function, this uncertainty is larger, δ​K∼0.25similar-to𝛿𝐾0.25delta Ksim 0.25. Therefore, using this approach, we can estimate the CS kernel from ℓ=0ℓ0ell=0 matrix elements with a systematic uncertainty of about ±0.1plus-or-minus0.1pm 0.1. Let us emphasize that the method described here does not depend on the error in the determination of absolute values of 𝐫𝐫r, but only on the error of the deviation of 𝐫​(b)𝐫𝑏r(b) from the constant. The analysis presented in this section is valid only for ΓΓGamma-matrices corresponding to leading twist TMDs, for which Γ′=ΓsuperscriptΓ′ΓGammaprime=Gamma . For leading twist TMDs, the CS kernel is independent of ΓΓGamma. This fact can be exploited for a cross-check of our computation. There are three ΓΓGamma-matrices of leading twist TMDs Γ=γ+,γ+​γ5,i​σα+​γ5,Γsuperscript𝛾superscript𝛾superscript𝛾5𝑖superscript𝜎limit-from𝛼superscript𝛾5displaystyleGamma=gamma+,gamma+gamma5,isigmaalpha+gamma5, where the index α𝛼alpha is transverse. For these cases, the matrix elements Φ[Γ]superscriptΦdelimited-ΓPhi[Gamma] are parameterized in terms of the leading twist TMDs according to citebib.bib37, bib.bib38 Φf←h[γ+]​(x,b)subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-superscript𝛾←𝑓ℎ𝑥𝑏displaystylePhi[gamma+]fleftarrow h(x,b) =displaystyle= f1​(x,b)+i​ϵTμ​ν​bμ​sT​ν​M​f1​T⟂​(x,b),subscript𝑓1𝑥𝑏𝑖subscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝜈𝑇subscript𝑏𝜇subscript𝑠𝑇𝜈𝑀superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑇perpendicular-to𝑥𝑏displaystyle f1(x,b)+iepsilonmunuTbmusTnuMf1Tperp(x,b), Φf←h[γ+​γ5]​(x,b)subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-superscript𝛾superscript𝛾5←𝑓ℎ𝑥𝑏displaystylePhi[gamma+gamma5]fleftarrow h(x,b) =displaystyle= λ​g1​L​(x,b)+i​bμ​sTμ​M​g1​T​(x,b),𝜆subscript𝑔1𝐿𝑥𝑏𝑖subscript𝑏𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑠𝑇𝜇𝑀subscript𝑔1𝑇𝑥𝑏displaystylelambda g1L(x,b)+ibmusTmuMg1T(x,b), Φf←h[i​σα+​γ5]​(x,b)subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-𝑖superscript𝜎limit-from𝛼superscript𝛾5←𝑓ℎ𝑥𝑏displaystylePhi[isigmaalpha+gamma5]fleftarrow h(x,b) =displaystyle= sTα​h1​(x,b)−i​λ​bα​M​h1​L⟂​(x,b)superscriptsubscript𝑠𝑇𝛼subscriptℎ1𝑥𝑏𝑖𝜆superscript𝑏𝛼𝑀superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝐿perpendicular-to𝑥𝑏displaystyle sTalphah1(x,b)-ilambda balphaMh1Lperp(x,b) where ϵTμ​νsubscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝜈𝑇epsilonmunuT and gTμ​νsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝑇𝜇𝜈gTmunu are the transverse parts of the Levi-Civita and the metric tensor (ϵT23=−ϵT32=1subscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵ23𝑇subscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵ32𝑇1epsilon23T=-epsilon32T=1, if the transverse plane is the (y,z)𝑦𝑧(y,z) plane), sTμsuperscriptsubscript𝑠𝑇𝜇sTmu and λ𝜆lambda are the transverse and longitudinal components of the nucleon’s spin vector, and M𝑀M is the mass of the nucleon. Considering different polarization states of the hadron, we can access different TMDs independently. The adopted method’s main advantage is that lattice computations of R​(ℓ=0)𝑅ℓ0R(ell=0) are essentially simpler than the evaluation of qTMDs with x𝑥x-dependence. In our case, we do not need to account for ℓℓell-dependent renormalization factors and power corrections induced by ℓℓell. We see this as an advantage of our analysis. In any case, the CS kernel extraction might be strongly affected by power corrections, which (as we expect) is a common property of all determinations of the CS kernel from the lattice. Better control of power corrections is vital for continued progress. At present, too little is known about these to predict which precision can be ultimately reached. The only point that seems inevitable is that further progress will require substantial work on all sides – perturbative QCD, lattice QCD, and experiment.
content_sections.3 Evaluation of TMD matrix elements on t: In this section we provide details of the computation of the matrix elements W[Γ]superscript𝑊delimited-ΓW[Gamma] on the lattice.

In the following, we consider Euclidean spacetime, and utilize the following notation for any four-vector, a=(a→,a4)𝑎→𝑎superscript𝑎4a=(veca,a4), i. e. a→→𝑎veca represents the spatial vector components, whereas a4superscript𝑎4a4 is the Euclidean time component. The TMD matrix element can be accessed on an Euclidean lattice by evaluating an extended three point function C3​p​tsubscript𝐶3ptC3pt. It is defined as: C3​p​tΓ​(P→,S,𝒞,t,τ):=⟨tr​ΓS​𝒫​(P→,t)​JΓ​(𝒞,τ)​𝒫¯​(P→,0)⟩,assignsubscriptsuperscript𝐶Γ3pt→𝑃𝑆𝒞𝑡𝜏delimited-⟨⟩trsubscriptΓ𝑆𝒫→𝑃𝑡superscript𝐽Γ𝒞𝜏¯𝒫→𝑃0displaystyle CGamma3pt(vecP,S,C,t,tau):=lefttrleftGammaSP(vecP,t) JGamma(C,tau) overlineP(vecP,0)rightright,, where the angle brackets indicate the sum over all gauge configurations of the employed gauge ensemble and the trace is taken with respect to spinor indices. The operators 𝒫𝒫P and 𝒫¯¯𝒫overlineP are the proton interpolators 𝒫​(k→,t)𝒫→𝑘𝑡displaystyleP(veck,t) :=a3​∑x→e−i​x→​k→​ϵa​b​c​ua​(x)​[ubT​(x)​C​γ5​1+γ42​dc​(x)]|x4=t,assignabsentevaluated-atsuperscript𝑎3subscript→𝑥superscript𝑒𝑖→𝑥→𝑘subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑎𝑏𝑐subscript𝑢𝑎𝑥delimited-superscriptsubscript𝑢𝑏𝑇𝑥𝐶subscript𝛾51subscript𝛾42subscript𝑑𝑐𝑥superscript𝑥4𝑡displaystyle:=a3sumvecxe-ivecxveckleft. epsilonabcua(x)left[ubT(x)Cgamma5frac1+gamma42dc(x)right]right|x4=t,, 𝒫¯​(k→,t)¯𝒫→𝑘𝑡displaystyleoverlineP(veck,t) :=a3​∑x→ei​x→​k→​ϵa​b​c​[u¯aT​(x)​C​γ5​1+γ42​d¯b​(x)]​u¯c​(x)|x4=t. assignabsentevaluated-atsuperscript𝑎3subscript→𝑥superscript𝑒𝑖→𝑥→𝑘subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑎𝑏𝑐delimited-superscriptsubscript¯𝑢𝑎𝑇𝑥𝐶subscript𝛾51subscript𝛾42subscript¯𝑑𝑏𝑥subscript¯𝑢𝑐𝑥superscript𝑥4𝑡displaystyle:=a3sumvecxeivecxveckleft. epsilonabcleft[baruaT(x)Cgamma5frac1+gamma42bardb(x)right]baruc(x)right|x4=t,. These annihilate or create a tri-quark state sharing the same quantum numbers as the proton, which is implemented in the Chroma software systemcitebib.bib39. The spinor matrix ΓSsubscriptΓ𝑆GammaS projects out the state with positive parity (at rest) and the desired proton spin S𝑆S, see . The expression is invariant under shifts in time, assuming infinite or periodic time extensions. In our simulations, we use a lattice with open boundaries in the time direction. For that reason we shall use the source time slice tsrc≫0much-greater-thansubscript𝑡src0tsrcgg 0. In the current case, the operator JΓsuperscript𝐽ΓJGamma is represented by a non-local gauge-invariant quark bilinear , JΓ​(𝒞,τ):=q¯​(b+ℓ​v,τ)​Γ​𝒰​[𝒞​(ℓ,v,b,L)]​q​(0,τ). assignsuperscript𝐽Γ𝒞𝜏¯𝑞𝑏ℓ𝑣𝜏Γ𝒰delimited-𝒞ℓ𝑣𝑏𝐿𝑞0𝜏displaystyle JGamma(C,tau):=barq(b+ell v,tau)GammaU[C(ell,v,b,L)]q(0,tau),. The vectors b𝑏b and v𝑣v have vanishing time components and can be directly translated to Minkowski spacetime. Hence, the quark fields are positioned at the same Euclidean time slice τ𝜏tau. The operator JΓsuperscript𝐽ΓJGamma contains the staple-shaped gauge link 𝒰​[𝒞]𝒰delimited-𝒞U[C] defined in . The lattice analog of 𝒰𝒰U can be constructed with the elementary gauge links Uμ​(x)subscript𝑈𝜇𝑥Umu(x). In our simulations, we use Wilson lines [x,x+y]𝑥𝑥𝑦[x,x+y] with two possible orientations, y=μ​N𝑦𝜇𝑁y=hatmuN and y=(μ+ν)​N𝑦𝜇𝜈𝑁y=(hatmu+hatnu)N. Here, the variables μ𝜇hatmu and ν𝜈hatnu are the lattice unit vectors pointing in different directions and N𝑁N is an integer number. The explicit construction of [x,x+y]𝑥𝑥𝑦[x,x+y] in terms of elementary links is [x,x+y]𝑥𝑥𝑦displaystyle[x,x+y] =∏n=0N−1Uμ​(x+n​μ)absentsuperscriptsubscriptproduct𝑛0𝑁1subscript𝑈𝜇𝑥𝑛𝜇displaystyle=prodn=0N-1Umu(x+nhatmu) for y𝑦displaystyle y =μ​N,absent𝜇𝑁displaystyle=hatmuN,, [x,x+y]𝑥𝑥𝑦displaystyle[x,x+y] =∏n=0N−1Uμ​(x+n​(μ+ν))​Uν​(x+(n+1)​μ+n​ν)absentsuperscriptsubscriptproduct𝑛0𝑁1subscript𝑈𝜇𝑥𝑛𝜇𝜈subscript𝑈𝜈𝑥𝑛1𝜇𝑛𝜈displaystyle=prodn=0N-1Umu(x+n(hatmu+hatnu)) Unu(x+(n+1)hatmu+nhatnu) for y𝑦displaystyle y =(μ+ν)​N. absent𝜇𝜈𝑁displaystyle=(hatmu+hatnu)N,. Notice that in the latter case, there are two nonequivalent possibilities to implement the link path. The second is obtained by interchanging μ𝜇hatmu and ν𝜈hatnu. We perform our calculations using both versions. In order to relate the three-point function C3​p​tsubscript𝐶3ptC3pt to the TMD matrix element Wf←h[Γ]superscriptsubscript𝑊←𝑓ℎdelimited-ΓWfleftarrow h[Gamma] we calculate the following quantity: 2​m2+P→2​C3​p​t​(P,S,𝒞,t,τ)C2​p​t​(P,S,t)|0≪τ≪t=∑r​su¯​(r,P)​ΓS​u​(s,P)​⟨h​(P,s)|​JΓ​(𝒞,τ)​|h​(P,r)⟩∑su¯​(s,P)​ΓS​u​(s,P),evaluated-at2superscript𝑚2superscript→𝑃2subscript𝐶3pt𝑃𝑆𝒞𝑡𝜏subscript𝐶2pt𝑃𝑆𝑡much-less-than0𝜏much-less-than𝑡subscript𝑟𝑠¯𝑢𝑟𝑃subscriptΓ𝑆𝑢𝑠𝑃braℎ𝑃𝑠superscript𝐽Γ𝒞𝜏ketℎ𝑃𝑟subscript𝑠¯𝑢𝑠𝑃subscriptΓ𝑆𝑢𝑠𝑃displaystyleleft.2sqrtm2+vecP2fracC3pt(P,S,C,t,tau)C2pt(P,S,t)right|0lltaull t=fracsumrsbaru(r,P)GammaSu(s,P)brah(P,s)JGamma(C,tau)keth(P,r)sumsbaru(s,P)GammaSu(s,P),, where the r. h. s. can be identified with Wf←h[Γ]superscriptsubscript𝑊←𝑓ℎdelimited-ΓWfleftarrow h[Gamma] if ΓSsubscriptΓ𝑆GammaS is chosen accordingly. In equation the Euclidean time separations between source, insertion, and sink should be large. In this limit, the excited proton states, which also overlap with the proton interpolators, are exponentially suppressed. The ratio with the two-point function is constructed using C2​p​t​(P→,S,t):=⟨tr​ΓS​𝒫​(P→,t)​𝒫¯​(P→,0)⟩. assignsubscript𝐶2pt→𝑃𝑆𝑡delimited-⟨⟩trsubscriptΓ𝑆𝒫→𝑃𝑡¯𝒫→𝑃0displaystyle C2pt(vecP,S,t):=lefttrleftGammaSP(vecP,t) overlineP(vecP,0)rightright,. This weighting is required for the correct normalization. The evaluation of the fermionic integral implicit in leads to several Wick contractions, of which we distinguish two kinds. These are referred to as "connected" and "disconnected" graphs, where the latter involves a fermion loop, including the operator JΓsuperscript𝐽ΓJGamma. In this study, we restrict ourselves to the non-singlet quark contributions (particularly, u−d𝑢𝑑u-d). In this combination, the disconnected graphs vanish exactly. Hence, we have to consider only one Wick contraction, which is schematically drawn in figure . It is called C3​p​tΓ,connsuperscriptsubscript𝐶3ptΓconnC3ptGamma,conn in the following. The connected Wick contraction is evaluated using smeared quark sources at random spatial position z→→𝑧vecz. The propagator obtained by an inversion of this source is denoted by MzΦ,P→superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑧Φ→𝑃MzPhi,vecP. The superscript Φ,P→Φ→𝑃Phi,vecP indicates that the source has been treated with momentum smearing citebib.bib40 in combination with HYP smeared gauge links citebib.bib41. Momentum smearing is crucial to realize large hadron momenta on the lattice, as it increases the overlap with the proton ground state dramatically. The propagator connecting the proton sink and the insertion operator is evaluated by the sequential source method citebib.bib42. The proton sink and the sequential source, which is placed at time slice t𝑡t, are again improved by momentum smearing. The corresponding sequential propagator is denoted by Xt,3​p​tΦ,P→subscriptsuperscript𝑋Φ→𝑃𝑡3ptXPhi,vecPt,3pt. Notice that it should be Hermitian conjugated and followed by multiplication with γ5subscript𝛾5gamma5, since an inversion on the sequential source returns a backward propagator. In total, we can write the connected contraction as: C3​p​tΓ,conn​(P→,S,𝒞,t,τ)=⟨ei​P→​z→​∑x→[Xt,3​p​t†,Φ,P→​(x→,τ)​γ5​Γ​𝒰latt​[𝒞]​MzΦ,P→​(x→,τ)]⟩,superscriptsubscript𝐶3ptΓconn→𝑃𝑆𝒞𝑡𝜏delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝑒𝑖→𝑃→𝑧subscript→𝑥delimited-subscriptsuperscript𝑋†Φ→𝑃𝑡3pt→𝑥𝜏subscript𝛾5Γsuperscript𝒰lattdelimited-𝒞superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑧Φ→𝑃→𝑥𝜏displaystyle C3ptGamma,conn(vecP,S,C,t,tau)=left eivecPveczsumvecxleft[Xdagger,Phi,vecPt,3pt(vecx,tau)gamma5GammaUlatt[C]MzPhi,vecP(vecx,tau)right]right,, where 𝒰latt​[𝒞]superscript𝒰lattdelimited-𝒞Ulatt[C] denotes the staple-shaped gauge link, which is constructed using the lattice gauge links or , respectively. Notice that we are able to calculate C3​p​tΓ,connsuperscriptsubscript𝐶3ptΓconnC3ptGamma,conn for all insertion time slices τ𝜏tau by performing only one sequential inversion, whereas the sink time slice t𝑡t is fixed by the location of the sequential source.

The two-point function, as well as the connected three-point graph C3​p​tΓ,connsuperscriptsubscript𝐶3ptΓconnC3ptGamma,conn are evaluated on 200020002000 configurations of the H101 CLS ensemble citebib.bib43. It employs the tree-level improved Lüscher-Weisz gauge action and includes Nf=2+1subscript𝑁𝑓21Nf=2+1 Sheikholeslami-Wohlert fermions. The extension is 323×96superscript32396323times 96 with lattice spacing a=0.0854𝑎0.0854a=0.0854fm. Additional ensemble parameters are given in table. For each configuration, we perform calculations for four different quark sources located at random spatial position with the source time slice tsrc=10​asubscript𝑡src10𝑎tsrc=10a, and the source-sink separation tsnk−tsrc=11​asubscript𝑡snksubscript𝑡src11𝑎ttextsnk-ttextsrc=11a. The quantity that enters the l. h. s. of is obtained by a fit to a constant regarding the insertion time τ𝜏tau. The fit includes an interval of insertion time slices assuming that excited states are sufficiently suppressed. In the present work we consider τ∈[4​a,7​a]𝜏4𝑎7𝑎tauin[4a,7a]. In our simulation the proton momentum P→=(P1,0,0)→𝑃subscript𝑃100vecP=(P1,0,0) has values P1∈0,1,2,3​2​πa​Lsubscript𝑃101232𝜋𝑎𝐿P1in0,1,2,3frac2piaL. The first case P1=0subscript𝑃10P1=0 is used to extract the nucleon mass and cross-check the dispersion relation. The other cases provide us with three values for P+=1.25,1.74,2.27superscript𝑃1.251.742.27P+=1.25,1.74,2.27 GeV, useful for the extraction of the CS kernel. The proton spin is oriented along the z𝑧z-direction, i. e. we insert ΓS=12​(1+γ4)​(1−i​γ2​γ1)subscriptΓ𝑆121superscript𝛾41𝑖superscript𝛾2superscript𝛾1displaystyleGammaS=frac12left(1+gamma4right)left(1-igamma2gamma1right) in and . Here, the first term is used to project the proton at rest onto the positive parity state. Since the proton momentum points in x𝑥x-direction, the proton spin lies in the transverse plane, and thus λ=0𝜆0lambda=0. The vector v𝑣v is taken to be vμ=(±1,0,0,0)superscript𝑣𝜇plus-or-minus1000vmu=(pm 1,0,0,0), such that (v⋅P)=±P1⋅𝑣𝑃plus-or-minussubscript𝑃1(vcdot P)=pm P1. The vector b𝑏b is bμ=(0,by,bz,0)superscript𝑏𝜇0subscript𝑏𝑦subscript𝑏𝑧0bmu=(0,by,bz,0). In the case by≠0subscript𝑏𝑦0byneq 0 and bz≠0subscript𝑏𝑧0bzneq 0, the transverse link has step-like form . In order to reduce autocorrelation, we apply the binning method to the measured C2​p​tsubscript𝐶2ptC2pt and C3​p​tΓ,connsuperscriptsubscript𝐶3ptΓconnC3ptGamma,conn, with bin size 202020. From the resulting binned samples, we create 100100100 jackknife samples, which are used for the estimation of the error propagation in all derived quantities, such as the ratio .

To extract physical matrix elements, we parametrize the correlators W[Γ]superscript𝑊delimited-ΓW[Gamma] using the most general parameterizations and fit the invariant structure functions. This approach has been developed in refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, and successfully applied to the computation of moments of various TMDs in the references citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib44, bib.bib24, bib.bib45. Note that, although this method can also be used to scan the ℓℓell-dependence of the correlators and extract the x𝑥x-dependence of TMDs, the application to first moments (ℓ=0ℓ0ell=0) is particularly simple and robust, and suited to the current task – the extraction of the CS kernel. The detailed description of this procedure with explicit expressions is given in ref. citebib.bib21. Here, for illustration purposes, we present only the parametrization for the vector matrix element. It reads W[γμ](b;ℓ=0,L;v,P,S)displaystylewidetildeW[gammamu](b;ell=0,L;v,P,S) =displaystyle= Pμ​a2+M2​(L​vμ)​b1−i​M​ϵμ​ν​α​β​Pν​bα​Sβ​a12superscript𝑃𝜇subscript𝑎2superscript𝑀2𝐿superscript𝑣𝜇subscript𝑏1𝑖𝑀superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝜈𝛼𝛽subscript𝑃𝜈subscript𝑏𝛼subscript𝑆𝛽subscript𝑎12displaystyle Pmutildea2+M2(Lvmu)tildeb1-iMepsilonmunualphabetaPnubalphaSbetatildea12 where for our calculation at ℓ=0ℓ0ell=0, aisubscript𝑎𝑖tildeai and bisubscript𝑏𝑖tildebi are functions of the Lorentz-invariant combinations (b2,L​(v⋅P),(L​v)2)superscript𝑏2𝐿⋅𝑣𝑃superscript𝐿𝑣2(b2,L(vcdot P),(Lv)2) (since (v⋅b)=(b⋅P)=0⋅𝑣𝑏⋅𝑏𝑃0(vcdot b)=(bcdot P)=0). Using the set of matrix elements W[γμ]superscript𝑊delimited-superscript𝛾𝜇widetildeW[gammamu] evaluated at different μ𝜇mu, L𝐿L and bνsuperscript𝑏𝜈bnu we fit the invariant functions aisubscript𝑎𝑖tildeai and bisubscript𝑏𝑖tildebi with a least-square fit. In the following step, the fitted functions aisubscript𝑎𝑖tildeai and bisubscript𝑏𝑖tildebi are grouped into the leading twist TMD combinations . Using that the spin-vector is an independent vector we identify the combinations of aisubscript𝑎𝑖tildeai and bisubscript𝑏𝑖tildebi that refer to particular TMD distributions (,,). In the vector case, the terms proportional to the unpolarized (f1subscript𝑓1f1) and Sivers (f1​T⟂superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑇perpendicular-tof1Tperp) TMDs are W[f1]​(b2,L,P+)=P+​(a2​(b2)+M2​L​v+P+​b1​(b2)),superscript𝑊delimited-subscript𝑓1superscript𝑏2𝐿superscript𝑃superscript𝑃subscript𝑎2superscript𝑏2superscript𝑀2𝐿superscript𝑣superscript𝑃subscript𝑏1superscript𝑏2displaystyle W[f1](b2,L,P+)=P+left(tildea2(b2)+M2fracLv+P+tildeb1(b2)right), W[f1​T⟂]​(b2,L,P+)=P+​(a12​(b2)−M2​L​v+P+​b8​(b2)). superscript𝑊delimited-superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑇perpendicular-tosuperscript𝑏2𝐿superscript𝑃superscript𝑃subscript𝑎12superscript𝑏2superscript𝑀2𝐿superscript𝑣superscript𝑃subscript𝑏8superscript𝑏2displaystyle W[f1Tperp](b2,L,P+)=P+left(tildea12(b2)-M2fracLv+P+tildeb8(b2)right). Here, we introduce the notation W[F]superscript𝑊delimited-𝐹W[F], which indicates the component of the matrix element W[Γ]superscript𝑊delimited-ΓW[Gamma] with the leading contribution proportional to the TMD F𝐹F. In other words, it is a qTMD (at ℓ=0ℓ0ell=0) with the quantum numbers corresponding to F𝐹F. The axial and tensor qTMDs defined in and are computed analogously. In our calculation we have access to the 6 leading-twist TMDs that remain present in (,,) after setting λ=0𝜆0lambda=0 as implied by our choice of the proton spin. These are the unpolarized TMD f1subscript𝑓1f1, the Sivers function f1​T⟂superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑇perpendicular-tof1Tperp, worm-gear-T function g1​Tsubscript𝑔1𝑇g1T, transversity TMD h1subscriptℎ1h1, Boer-Mulders function h1⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1perpendicular-toh1perp and pretzelocity h1​T⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝑇perpendicular-toh1Tperp. The evaluation of the functions g1​Lsubscript𝑔1𝐿g1L and h1​L⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝐿perpendicular-toh1Lperp is also possible but requires simulation with a different spin orientation.
content_sections.4 The CS kernel from lattice data: In this section, we present the CS kernel extraction from the W[F]superscript𝑊delimited-𝐹W[F] determined by the lattice simulations. We also estimate the systematic uncertainty and compare the results of the extraction with previous extractions.
content_sections.4 The CS kernel from lattice data 4.1 De: As an outcome of the lattice simulation, described in the previous section, we have the functions W[F]superscript𝑊delimited-𝐹W[F] evaluated at different values of L𝐿L, (v⋅P)⋅𝑣𝑃(vcdot P), P+superscript𝑃P+ and |b→|→𝑏|vecb|. From these functions we construct the ratios Rlatt[F]​(P1,P2;b;L;v)=W[F]​(P1;b;L;v)W[F]​(P2;b;L;v),superscriptsubscript𝑅lattdelimited-𝐹subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝑏𝐿𝑣superscript𝑊delimited-𝐹subscript𝑃1𝑏𝐿𝑣superscript𝑊delimited-𝐹subscript𝑃2𝑏𝐿𝑣Rtextnormallatt[F](P1,P2;b;L;hatv)=fracW[F](P1;b;L;v)W[F](P2;b;L;v), where v=sign​(v⋅P)𝑣sign⋅𝑣𝑃hatv=textsign(vcdot P). It is important that many lattice-related artifacts, such as lattice renormalization factors, cancel in this ratio. This cancellation is not exact since there is an operator mixing (such as discussed in ref. citebib.bib46) that possibly depends on momentum. This point requires further investigation, which will be performed in the future. For the moment, we ignore these effects, expecting them to be small in comparison to other distortions, such as lattice artifacts and power corrections to the factorization theorem. We analyze the ratios using the theoretical scheme presented in sec. , and extract the CS kernel. The analysis has three principal steps, which we describe below. (i) Extrapolation L→∞→𝐿Ltoinfty. The large-L𝐿L limit is an essential requirement to reach the TMD regime . To extract the large-L𝐿L value of R[F]superscript𝑅delimited-𝐹R[F] we use a constant fit (for L>2.98𝐿2.98L>2.98 GeV) independently for each combination of b𝑏b, P1+/P2+superscriptsubscript𝑃1superscriptsubscript𝑃2P1+/P2+ and F𝐹F. Example profiles of R[F]superscript𝑅delimited-𝐹R[F] in L𝐿L and extracted plateau values are shown in figure . In the plateau fit we assume that ratios at the values for v=±1𝑣plus-or-minus1hatv=pm 1 are the same (which is the consequence of parity conservation) and perform a combined fit for the left and right branches. In many cases, the profile in L𝐿L does not have a well-defined transition to an asymptote. Such a situation is especially typical for large values of b𝑏b (b>2.5𝑏2.5b>2.5 GeV). It indicates that the values of L𝐿L used in our analysis are not large enough, and corrections ∼b/Lsimilar-toabsent𝑏𝐿sim b/L are significant. This part of the analysis provides the largest source of uncorrelated uncertainties. In the following plots, we specially shade the area b>2.5𝑏2.5b>2.5 GeV to indicate that this part of the extraction is not under control. We faced problems with the extrapolation of R[F]superscript𝑅delimited-𝐹R[F] for the Sivers f1​T⟂superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑇perpendicular-tof1Tperp, Boer-Mulders h1⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1perpendicular-toh1perp and pretzelocity h1​T⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝑇perpendicular-toh1Tperp cases. Particularly, the ratios R[f1​T⟂]superscript𝑅delimited-superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑇perpendicular-toR[f1Tperp] and R[h1⟂]superscript𝑅delimited-superscriptsubscriptℎ1perpendicular-toR[h1perp] show anomalous growth at large b𝑏b. The origin of this problem is not entirely clear. We associate it with the incomplete cancellation of lattice artifacts. Alternatively, such abnormal behavior can be described by a significant violation of the 𝐌=const. 𝐌const. M=textconst. assumption, see also fig. (right). The ratio R[h1​T⟂]superscript𝑅delimited-superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝑇perpendicular-toR[h1Tperp] has a very small signal-to-noise value. For these reasons, we exclude these three cases2Notice that these cases are the only TMDs that do not match twist-two distributions at small-b𝑏b, but twist-three distributions citebib.bib47. The general smallness of higher twist corrections explains the large noise issue. Additionally, the Sivers and Boer-Mulders functions are P-odd and have leading contributions of the Qiu-Sterman type citebib.bib48, with a zero-momentum gluon. In these cases, one could expect that power corrections in L−1superscript𝐿1L-1 are larger. from the following consideration, which left us with three TMDs. (ii) Determination of 𝐌𝐌M. The phenomenological formula for the extraction of the CS kernel contains the unknown function 𝐌𝐌M, which corrects the ratio for the unobserved x𝑥x-dependence. In our approach we assume 𝐌​(b)=const. 𝐌𝑏const. M(b)=textconst. , and extract it from Rlatt[F]subscriptsuperscript𝑅delimited-𝐹lattR[F]textlatt. The extraction is performed at values of b𝑏b which, on the one hand, are small enough to be described by perturbative QCD, and on the other hand, are large enough to satisfy . We choose b=1.20𝑏1.20b=1.20 GeV or 1.281.281.28 GeV (for configurations which contain these points), and fit the value of 𝐌𝐌M comparing R[F]superscript𝑅delimited-𝐹R[F] to equation with the perturbative 𝐫𝐫r given in . The perturbative CS kernel used in this comparison is taken at NLO citebib.bib32. For better control of the extraction uncertainty we perform an additional fit-range variation during the L→∞→𝐿Ltoinfty extrapolation, and consider the cases L>1.70,2.13,2.55,2.98𝐿1.702.132.552.98L>1.70,2.13,2.55,2.98 GeV. The extracted values of 𝐌𝐌M are presented in fig. . The determination of 𝐌𝐌M is the central element of our analysis. According to our hypothesis, the values of 𝐌𝐌M must be constant and depend only on the type of TMD. The deviation from a single value for different extractions provides an additional source of uncertainty in our analysis. To account for this uncertainty, we use the average value of 𝐌𝐌M computed for each set of TMDs, as is shown in fig. . Generally, we found a good agreement between different momentum configurations and different fitting ranges. Partially, the difference between different momentum configurations is explained by the evolution effects for 𝐌𝐌M, which are subleading in our analysis. (iii) Extraction of K𝐾K. We combine the extrapolated Rlatt[F]​(L→∞)subscriptsuperscript𝑅delimited-𝐹latt→𝐿R[F]textlatt(Ltoinfty) with the values of 𝐌𝐌M and determine the CS kernel according to equation . This gives us the CS kernel at the factorization scale μ𝜇mu . For cross-comparison and comparison with other extractions we evolve CS kernels to μ=2​GeV𝜇2GeVmu=2,textnormalGeV. The evolution equation is independent of b𝑏b and thus gives us a flat shift for the CS kernel value, K​(b,2​GeV)=K​(b,μ)+Δ​(μ)𝐾𝑏2GeV𝐾𝑏𝜇Δ𝜇K(b,2,textnormalGeV)=K(b,mu)+Delta(mu). The values of the factorization scale μ𝜇mu and ΔΔDelta are summarized in the following table, P1+,P2+superscriptsubscript𝑃1superscriptsubscript𝑃2P1+,P2+ 1.74,1.25GeV 2.27,1.74GeV 2.27,1.25GeV μ𝜇mu Δ​(μ)Δ𝜇Delta(mu) In all steps the statistical propagation of uncertainties is performed with the resampling method. All theoretical predictions, such as the perturbative CS kernel, evolution factors, etc, are evaluated by artemide citebib.bib49 at NLO with free parameters tuned as in the SV19 TMD-extraction citebib.bib12.
content_sections.4 The CS kernel from lattice data 4.2 Es: The results of extraction are given in figs. and and discussed in the following section. The error-bars on these figures represent the statistical uncertainty only. The accurate estimation of systematic uncertainty is impossible at the current stage of research. However, let us at least identify the main sources and give an estimate of possible effects. • Lattice artifacts. Presumably, this is the main source of uncertainty and the least controlled. Mainly, the lattice artifacts arise due to differences between proton states evaluated at different momenta. This effect is visible in fig. as a sudden change of asymptotics at large-L𝐿L. In some cases (f1​T⟂superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑇perpendicular-tof1Tperp, h1⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1perpendicular-toh1perp, h1​T⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝑇perpendicular-toh1Tperp) these lattice artifacts completely ruin the extraction. An improved understanding of these artifacts and their control is essential for further development. • Assumption 𝐌=const. 𝐌const. M=textconst. . This assumption introduces a fully correlated systematic uncertainty, the size of which is estimated by equation . The values of δ​𝐌𝛿𝐌deltaM are given in fig. , and the resulting values for δ​K𝛿𝐾delta K are indicated on each figure. • Small size of staple contour. The insufficient size of the contour generates a systematic deformation of the CS kernel at large b𝑏b. We expect that the values with b>2.5​GeV−1𝑏2.5superscriptGeV1b>2.5,textnormalGeV-1 are systematically higher (lower for |K|𝐾|K|). This effect occurs because many configurations do not reach the plateau for available values of L𝐿L, and thus corresponding values are systematically underestimated. Thus, in fig. and this area is shaded in red. The same effect but in a smaller amount takes place also at smaller values of b𝑏b in some configurations. This is apparent in fig. , where some series of points are moving to the left or right with an increase of L𝐿L, e. g. for g1​Tsubscript𝑔1𝑇g1T at P1+=2.27​GeVsuperscriptsubscript𝑃12.27GeVP1+=2.27,GeV. • Power corrections in P𝑃P to factorization theorem. The factorization theorem is valid at large P𝑃P, whereas the available values are not that large. The amount of violation of the factorization theorem can be estimated by the method suggested in ref. citebib.bib18, which uses the fact that in the factorization limit, only the “good” components of the quark fields survive . In turn, it implies that certain tensor components, cf. , of the quark-bilinear are larger than others. Therefore, the ratio of a “bad” component to a “good” component provides an estimation for the size of power corrections to the factorization theorem. We performed such a comparison and found that these corrections amount to ∼40%similar-toabsentpercent40sim 40% at P+=1.25​GeVsuperscript𝑃1.25GeVP+=1.25,textnormalGeV, and ∼30%similar-toabsentpercent30sim 30% at P+=2.27​GeVsuperscript𝑃2.27GeVP+=2.27,textnormalGeV for b≃2.5​GeV−1similar-to-or-equals𝑏2.5superscriptGeV1bsimeq 2.5,textnormalGeV-1. In fig. we demonstrate the case W​[fT]/W​[f1]𝑊delimited-subscript𝑓𝑇𝑊delimited-subscript𝑓1W[fT]/W[f1], where fTsubscript𝑓𝑇fT is the twist-three TMD that parametrizes the ∼bμsimilar-toabsentsuperscript𝑏𝜇sim bmu component of the vector current (gTμ​ν​⟨q¯​γν​q⟩∼bμ​M​fTsimilar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝑇𝜇𝜈delimited-⟨⟩¯𝑞subscript𝛾𝜈𝑞superscript𝑏𝜇𝑀subscript𝑓𝑇gTmunubarqgammanuqsim bmuMfT, ignoring common structures). The tested ratios exhibit the expected general behavior, i. e. , they decrease with the increase of P+superscript𝑃P+ or decrease of b𝑏b. Fortunately, these corrections do not significantly impact our extraction because they mostly cancel in the ratio R[F]superscript𝑅delimited-𝐹R[F] and are partially accounted for by the fit of 𝐌𝐌M. However, these corrections will present significant problems for determining absolute values for TMDs from the lattice. • Power corrections in b−1superscript𝑏1b-1 to factorization theorem. At small values of b𝑏b the factorization theorem is violated by 1/|b|​P+1𝑏superscript𝑃1/|b|P+ corrections. Therefore, the range of small-b is not reliable. The effect of these corrections is apparent for b≲0.8​GeV−1less-than-or-similar-to𝑏0.8superscriptGeV1blesssim 0.8,textnormalGeV-1, where the CS kernel is perturbative. There are also numerous smaller sources of systematic uncertainties, which are not discussed here. In general, we believe that our extraction, supplemented with the statistical and the given correlated uncertainties, provides a reliable estimation for the CS kernel in the range 0.8≲b≲2.5​GeV−1less-than-or-similar-to0.8𝑏less-than-or-similar-to2.5superscriptGeV10.8lesssim blesssim 2.5,textnormalGeV-1.
content_sections.4 The CS kernel from lattice data 4.3 Di: The extracted values of the CS kernel from three different TMDs, and three different momentum combinations are shown in fig. . Clearly, the present uncertainties are dominated by statistical noise. Nonetheless, we observe a good agreement between different channels, which confirms the CS kernel’s universality. The noisiest channel is the worm-gear TMD g1​Tsubscript𝑔1𝑇g1T. In this case, the main source of disagreement comes from the spread of 𝐌𝐌M (see fig. ). The uncertainty in the determination of 𝐌𝐌M gives rise to the correlated uncertainty, which is denoted by δ​K𝛿𝐾delta K and indicated for each case. In fig. (left panel), we present the values of the CS kernel from the combined fit of different momentum configurations for each TMD. Effectively, the averaging over channels triples the statistics. We observe that the extractions from f1subscript𝑓1f1 and h1subscriptℎ1h1 are in excellent agreement with each other, whereas the curves we obtain from g1​Tsubscript𝑔1𝑇g1T have a rising tendency at large b𝑏b. This effect mainly appears due to the small sizes of the contour. The right panel of fig. shows the values for the CS kernel combined from all channels and represents the main result of our work. The combined value of δ​K𝛿𝐾delta K is computed according to ref. citebib.bib50. We compare the obtained CS kernel with other results in fig. . The left panel shows the comparison with phenomenological extractions citebib.bib12, bib.bib13 and the purely perturbative CS kernel resummed at NLO citebib.bib32, bib.bib33. We observe some similarity between extractions in the range 12.5𝑏2.5b>2.5 GeV the discrepancy is essential, as well as the difference between the two phenomenological curves. Probably this indicates that these curves are primarily determined by the chosen analytic form of the parametrizations for such large b𝑏b. The right panel of fig. shows the comparison with other lattice computations, which were made in ref. citebib.bib26 (in the quenched approximation) and in ref. citebib.bib25 (where the CS kernel is extracted from the qTMD soft factor citebib.bib51). We observe a nice agreement. Importantly, all lattice simulations demonstrate a weak variation of the CS kernel at large-b𝑏b. This observation contradicts the popular assumption K​(b)∼b2similar-to𝐾𝑏superscript𝑏2K(b)sim b2 for large b𝑏b, see e. g. refs. citebib.bib13, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, and supports models with linear or constant asymptotics, such as in ref. citebib.bib54, bib.bib12, bib.bib11.
content_sections.5 Conclusion: In this work, we present a lattice computation of the Collins-Soper (CS) kernel from the first moments of qTMD distributions. The analysis is performed with dynamical fermions and staple-shaped Wilson lines for the CLS ensemble H101 with lattice spacing a=0.0854𝑎0.0854a=0.0854 fm and unphysical quark masses. The results are shown in figures and . For the first time the CS kernel is determined from three different TMDs (f1subscript𝑓1f1, g1​Tsubscript𝑔1𝑇g1T, h1subscriptℎ1h1). The results agree with each other, which confirms the universality of the approach. The method of extraction used in this work was suggested in ref. citebib.bib18 and is based on the NLO factorization formula for qTMDs citebib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18. In contrast to other methods citebib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, it does not require evaluating the x𝑥x-dependence of qTMDs but uses only x𝑥x-moments. The information on the x𝑥x-dependence is contained in a certain integral combination of TMDs, called 𝐌𝐌M . The central assumption of the approach is that 𝐌𝐌M is independent of b𝑏b, which we confirm (within statistical uncertainties) from our lattice data. The ambiguity in the determination of 𝐌𝐌M is contained in the fully correlated uncertainty δ​K𝛿𝐾delta K. The given values for δ​K𝛿𝐾delta K may be exceeded by other (not estimated) systematic uncertainties. Overall, our results demonstrate that the method suggested in ref. citebib.bib18 is suitable for determining the CS kernel. Our extraction of K𝐾K is supplemented with a discussion of sources of systematic uncertainties. We identify the pure lattice artifacts as the largest and the least controlled source of uncertainty. Excessive lattice artifacts force us to exclude the functions f1​T⟂superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑇perpendicular-tof1Tperp, h1⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1perpendicular-toh1perp and h1​T⟂superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝑇perpendicular-toh1Tperp from our analysis. Other sources, such as power corrections to qTMD factorization and model assumptions, produce smaller and better-quantified uncertainties. We identify as the reliable range for the extracted CS kernel the interval 0.8​GeV−1≲b≲2.5less-than-or-similar-to0.8superscriptGeV1𝑏less-than-or-similar-to2.50.8,textGeV-1lesssim blesssim 2.5 GeV. We also estimate the size of power suppressed corrections to TMD factorization and find them to amount to ∼30−40%similar-toabsent30percent40sim 30-40% at our energies. These corrections do not strongly influence the present extraction of the CS kernel but will play a vital role in extracting the x𝑥x-dependent TMDs. In fig. we demonstrate that our results are in agreement with previous lattice calculations citebib.bib26, bib.bib25. In our case, the statistical uncertainty is smaller and comparable to the uncertainty of phenomenological extractions due to a larger number of channels for determining the CS kernel. For that reason, and because we can better estimate systematic uncertainties, we regard our result as a significant step forward in the lattice determination of the CS kernel. The comparison with the phenomenological extraction exhibits discrepancies, which are mainly due to systematic sources. Therefore, future studies should concentrate on the elimination of systematic contaminations. Acknowledgements. We acknowledge PRACE for awarding us access to SuperMUC-NG at GCS@LRZ, Germany. The authors also gratefully acknowledge the Gauss Centre for Supercomputing e. V. (www. gauss-centre. eu) directly for manifold help and funding this project by providing additional computing time in the start-up phase SuperMUC-NG at Leibniz Supercomputing Centre (www. lrz. de). The authors thank the Rechenzentrum of Regensburg for providing the Athene Cluster for supplementary computations. The authors have greatly profited from discussions with Piotr Korcyl, Rajan Gupta, Bernhard Musch, Ignazio Scimemi, and Jeremy Green. This project was funded in part by DFG, SFB/TRR-55 “Hadron Physics from Lattice QCD”. Michael Engelhardt is supported by the U. S. Department of Energy, Office of Science, Office of Nuclear Physics through grant DE-FG02-96ER40965 and through the TMD Topical Collaboration. The CLS collaboration is acknowledged for generating the nf=2+1subscript𝑛𝑓21nf=2+1 ensembles. We also thank the authors of the Chroma software systemcitebib.bib39 which we used to measure the correlators and the authors of Matplotlibcitebib.bib55 which was used for the visualization of our data.

sorted_papers.title: Lattice QCD Study of Transverse-Momentum Dependent Soft Function
sorted_papers.abstract:
In this work, we perform a lattice QCD study of the intrinsic,
rapidity-independent soft function within the framework of large momentum
effective theory. The computation is carried out using a gauge ensemble of
$N_f=2+1+1$ clover-improved twisted mass fermion. After applying an appropriate
renormalization procedure and the removal of significant higher-twist
contamination, we obtain the intrinsic soft function that is comparable to the
one-loop perturbative result at large external momentum. The determination of
the nonperturbative soft function from first principles is crucial to sharpen
our understanding of the processes with small transverse momentum such as the
Drell-Yan production and the semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering.
Additionally, we calculate the Collins-Soper evolution kernel using the
quasi-transverse-momentum-dependent wave function as input.

sorted_papers.title:
Transverse Momentum Dependent Gluon Distribution within High Energy
  Factorization at Next-to-Leading Order
sorted_papers.abstract:
We discuss Transverse Momentum Dependent (TMD) gluon distributions within
high energy factorization at next-to-leading order in the strong coupling
within the framework of Lipatov's high energy effective action. We provide a
detailed discussion of both rapidity divergences related to the TMD definition
and its soft factor on the one hand, and rapidity divergences due to high
energy factorization on the other hand, and discuss common features and
differences between Collins-Soper (CS) and Balitsky-Fadin-Kuarev-Lipatov (BFKL)
evolution. While we confirm earlier results which state that the unpolarized
and linearly polarized gluon TMD agree in the BFKL limit at leading order, we
find that both distributions differ, once next-to-leading order corrections are
being included. Unlike previous results, our framework allows to recover the
complete anomalous dimension associated with Collins-Soper-Sterman (CSS)
evolution of the TMD distribution, including also single-logarithmic terms in
the CSS evolution. As an additional result we provide a definition of
$k_T$-factorization, i.e. matching of off-shell coefficients to collinear
factorization at next-to-leading order within high energy factorization and the
effective action framework. We furthermore establish a link between the QCD
operator definition of the TMD gluon distribution and a previously derived
off-shell TMD gluon-to-gluon splitting function, which is within the present
framework obtained as the real 1-loop correction.

all_papers.title: The Four Loop QCD Rapidity Anomalous Dimension
all_papers.abstract:
The rapidity anomalous dimension controls the scaling of transverse momentum
dependent observables in the Sudakov region. In a conformal theory it is
equivalent to the soft anomalous dimension, but in QCD this relation is broken
by anomalous terms proportional to the $\beta$-function. In this paper we first
give a simple proof of this relation using two different representations of the
energy-energy correlator observable. We then calculate the anomalous terms to
three loops by computing the three-loop fully differential soft function to
$\mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$. Combined with recent perturbative data from the study
of on-shell form factors and splitting functions, this allows us to derive the
four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD.

sections.45: Armoni, Anomalous Dimensions from a Spinning D5-Brane, JHEP 11 (2006) 009, [hep-th/0608026].
sections.70: Meyer, Transforming differential equations of multi-loop Feynman integrals into canonical form, JHEP 04 (2017) 006, [arXiv:1611.01087].
sections.74: Nogueira, Automatic Feynman graph generation, J

qualitative: The rapidity anomalous dimension governs the scaling evolution of transverse momentum dependent observables in the Sudakov region, as described by the Collins-Soper-Sterman formalism and the rapidity renormalization group in SCET.
In a conformal field theory, the rapidity anomalous dimension is equivalent to the soft anomalous dimension, but in QCD this equivalence is broken by anomalous terms proportional to the β-function.
The difference between the rapidity and soft anomalous dimensions arises due to the non-conformal nature of QCD, with corrections involving the β-function that appear at higher loops.

The rapidity anomalous dimension is essential for resumming large logarithms in observables such as the energy-energy correlator (EEC) in the back-to-back limit and the transverse momentum spectrum of Higgs and electroweak bosons at small pT.

The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD is derived by exploiting its relation to the soft anomalous dimension in a conformal theory, with the breaking terms computed at three loops via the fully differential soft function to O(ε).

A simple proof of the equivalence between rapidity and soft anomalous dimensions in a conformal theory is provided using two representations of the EEC: one based on a Euclidean four-point function and the other on timelike factorization.
The calculation relies on known results for the soft anomalous dimension and recent perturbative data from form factors and splitting functions, enabling the derivation of the four-loop result.
quantitative: The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD is derived from the three-loop fully differential soft function computed to O(ε) in dimensional regularization.

The difference between the rapidity anomalous dimension γR and the soft anomalous dimension γS at four loops is given by terms proportional to the β-function: γR = γS + β(αs) × (three-loop correction).

The β-function in QCD is known to four loops: β(αs) = -ε αs + (αs^2 / (4π)) β0 + (αs^3 / (4π)^2) β1 + (αs^4 / (4π)^3) β2 + (αs^5 / (4π)^4) β3, with β0 = 11 C_A / 3 - 4 T_F n_f / 3, β1 = 102 C_A / 3 - 38 T_F n_f / 3, etc.
The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension is expressed as γR^(4) = γS^(4) + β^(3) × c3, where c3 is a coefficient determined from the three-loop soft function calculation.

The result enables NLL resummation for the energy-energy correlator in the back-to-back limit and for the small pT spectrum of Higgs and electroweak bosons, with the four-loop DGLAP evolution required for full precision.

content_sections.1 Introduction: Precision calculations in perturbative QCD play a crucial role in interpreting data in collider experiments. This is particularly true for key observables, such as QCD event shapes, which can be used for precision measurements of the strong coupling citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, or the Sudakov region of the Higgs transverse moment spectrum, where precision enables study of the coupling of the Higgs to light quarks citebib.bib5, bib.bib6. Due to progress in understanding the structure and universality of infrared evolution equations, as well as in fixed order calculations, these observables can be computed to next-to-next-to-next-to-leading logarithm (NLL) matched to next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) (see e. g. citebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21). In the last several years, there has been remarkable progress in extending calculations of a number of key gauge theory quantities to the four loop level. These include the β𝛽beta-function citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, the anomalous dimensions of twist-2 operators citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, the cusp anomalous dimension citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib26, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib29, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, the quark and gluon collinear anomalous dimensions citebib.bib33, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib34, bib.bib37, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib41, bib.bib40, bib.bib42, and most recently the full form factors citebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45. These anomalous dimensions govern the behavior of scattering amplitudes and cross sections in infrared limits, allowing the resummation of logarithms that appear in these limits, and which often invalidate a fixed order perturbative expansion, to NLL. They have recently seen their first phenomenological applications with the calculation of the four loop threshold corrections to Higgs production citebib.bib46. However, for a number of the most important phenomenological observables an additional anomalous dimension, the rapidity anomalous dimension, is required to describe the infrared dynamics. This is the case for the energy-energy-correlator (EEC) citebib.bib47 in the Sudakov observable, a key observable for precision studies of QCD event shapes, as well as for the transverse momentum, pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT, spectrum of the Higgs/electroweak bosons at small pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT. More generally, the rapidity anomalous dimension controls the scaling evolution of transverse momentum dependent observables, as formulated in the Collins-Soper-Sterman approach citebib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50 or in the rapidity renormalization group citebib.bib51, bib.bib52 approach in SCET citebib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, and is therefore a universal anomalous dimension describing the infrared dynamics of QCD. The goal of this paper is to fill this gap, and compute the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD. For the EEC, this allows resummation at NLL in the back-to-back region.2The five loop cusp anomalous dimension is also required to perform resummation at NLL, however, its effect should be minuscule. A rough estimate of the value of the five loop cusp can be obtained from the calculation of the five loop non-singlet anomalous dimensions citebib.bib28. Only the four loop anomalous dimensions in this paper are required to predict the singular structure at NLO. For the case of the pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT spectra of the Higgs or electroweak bosons, the complete four loop DGLAP evolution is also required. This is currently known for the non-singlet splitting functions citebib.bib27, and will hopefully be known for the singlet combinations in the near future. In this paper we compute the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD by exploiting its relation in a conformal field theory (CFT) to the soft anomalous dimension, γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS. This relation was discovered through perturbative calculations to three loops in citebib.bib58, and subsequently proven to all orders in citebib.bib59, bib.bib60. In a non-conformal theory this relation is broken by terms proportional to the β𝛽beta-function. The key advantage of exploiting this relation, is that since these corrections multiply β𝛽beta-functions, in perturbation theory they only need to be computed to one lower order. Therefore, we are able to compute the four loop anomalous dimension using known results for the soft function combined with a three loop calculation. Technically, we will obtain the anomalous terms by calculating the fully differential soft function citebib.bib61 to 𝒪​(ϵ)𝒪italic-ϵO(epsilon) at three loops in dimensional regularization.3This approach, as well as the result for the four loop anomalous dimensions were presented by the authors at World SCET 2020citebib.bib62 and at an online QCD seminar hosted by Xiangdong Jicitebib.bib63. In addition to presenting the result for the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD, we also give an additional proof of the correspondence between the rapidity anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension in a conformal theory using a recent derivation by Korchemsky of the back-to-back asymptotics of the EEC in a conformal field theory from a representation in terms of a Euclidean four point function citebib.bib64. The equivalence of this result with the timelike factorization derived in citebib.bib65 provides a simple proof of this equivalence. We hope that the results of this paper clarify the relations between different anomalous dimensions used in the literature, and emphasizes the importance of understanding relations between them. An outline of this paper is as follows. In Sec. we summarize recent progress in the calculation of four loop anomalous dimensions which we will use in our calculation of the four loop anomalous dimension. In Sec. , we review the definition of the rapidity anomalous dimension as the anomalous dimension of a particular configuration of Wilson lines. In Sec. we present a simple proof of the correspondence between soft and rapidity anomalous dimensions in a conformal field theory using two different representations of the Sudakov asymptotics of the energy-energy correlators. In Sec. we describe our calculation of the NLO fully differential soft function to 𝒪​(ϵ)𝒪italic-ϵO(epsilon). In Sec. we compute the difference between the soft anomalous dimension γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS and the rapidity anomalous dimension γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR at four loops, and hence derive the four loop QCD rapidity anomalous dimension. We conclude in Sec. . Additional perturbative data is collected in the Appendix, as well as the attached ancillary files.
content_sections.2 Summary of Perturbative Data for Anoma: In this section we summarize known results for QCD anomalous dimensions that we will use as perturbative data, and discuss some relations between these anomalous dimensions. This will also allow us to state our conventions and notation, since a number of anomalous dimensions are given different names in different contexts. Apart from the β𝛽beta-function, the simplest and most well studied anomalous dimensions in gauge theories are the anomalous dimensions of twist-2 spin-J𝐽J operators, or equivalently, the moments of the spacelike splitting functions. In the limit S→∞→𝑆Stoinfty, the twist-2 anomalous dimensions behave as (see e. g. citebib.bib66, bib.bib67) γ​(J,αs)=Γcusp​(αs)​(log⁡J+γE)−Bδ​(αs)+𝒪​(1/J). 𝛾𝐽subscript𝛼𝑠superscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠𝐽subscript𝛾𝐸subscript𝐵𝛿subscript𝛼𝑠𝒪1𝐽displaystylegamma(J,alphas)=Gammacusp(alphas)(log J+gammaE)-Bdelta(alphas)+O(1/J),. This is equivalent to the limit z→1→𝑧1zto 1 in the splitting functions, where they behave as citebib.bib66, bib.bib68, bib.bib69 P​(z,αs)=Γcusp​(αs)(1−z)++Bδ​(αs)​δ​(1−z)+⋯. 𝑃𝑧subscript𝛼𝑠superscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript1𝑧subscript𝐵𝛿subscript𝛼𝑠𝛿1𝑧⋯displaystyle P(z,alphas)=fracGammacusp(alphas)(1-z)++Bdelta(alphas)delta(1-z)+cdots,. These limits define two anomalous dimensions, the cusp anomalous dimension citebib.bib70, ΓcuspsuperscriptΓcuspGammacusp, which describes the leading divergences for a cusped Wilson line, and the virtual anomalous dimension, Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta. Significant perturbative data is available for both of these anomalous dimensions. In planar 𝒩=4𝒩4N=4 super Yang-Mills, both ΓcuspsuperscriptΓcuspGammacusp citebib.bib71, bib.bib72 and Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta citebib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75 are known to all loops from integrability. In QCD (and in non-planar 𝒩=4𝒩4N=4 SYM), the cusp anomalous dimension was recently obtained analytically to four loops citebib.bib76, bib.bib39, bib.bib40 following much earlier work (see citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib26, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib29, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38 for the analytic calculation of the planar and matter dependent terms, and citebib.bib27, bib.bib77, bib.bib78, bib.bib79, bib.bib29 for numerical extractions). Due to an extensive program calculating splitting functions in QCD (see e. g. citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29 for recent results), Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta is fully known analytically at three loops citebib.bib80, bib.bib81 for both quarks and gluons. All color structures in Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta are also known at four loops for both quarks and gluons, either numerically, or analytically. In particular, the planar result is known analytically citebib.bib27, as are results for the nf2superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑓2nf2 and nf3superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑓3nf3 terms citebib.bib82, bib.bib83. Updated numerical results for all additional color structures were given in citebib.bib41 for the quark case, and in citebib.bib46 for the gluon case. For convenience, these anomalous dimensions are summarized in the Appendix. Our second source of perturbative data will come from the study of on-shell form factors. The logarithms in the on-shell form factors are well studied (see e. g. citebib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87, bib.bib88, bib.bib89, bib.bib90, bib.bib91). Here we follow the notation of citebib.bib92. The all orders formula for a form factor with momentum transfer Q2superscript𝑄2Q2, evaluated at the scale μ2=Q2superscript𝜇2superscript𝑄2mu2=Q2 can be written F​(1,αs​(Q),ϵ)=exp⁡[12​∫0Q2d​λ2λ2​(G​(1,αs​(λ,ϵ),ϵ)−Γcusp​(αs​(λ,ϵ))​log⁡Q2λ2)],𝐹1subscript𝛼𝑠𝑄italic-ϵ12superscriptsubscript0superscript𝑄2𝑑superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2𝐺1subscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵitalic-ϵsuperscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵsuperscript𝑄2superscript𝜆2displaystyle F(1,alphas(Q),epsilon)=expleft[frac12int0Q2fracdlambda2lambda2left(G(1,alphas(lambda,epsilon),epsilon)-Gammacusp(alphas(lambda,epsilon))logfracQ2lambda2right)right],, where ϵ=2−d/2italic-ϵ2𝑑2epsilon=2-d/2 is the dimensional regulator, and αs​(λ,ϵ)subscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵalphas(lambda,epsilon) is the d𝑑d-dimensional running coupling at scale λ𝜆lambda. As with the twist-2 operators, the leading logarithms are governed by the cusp anomalous dimension. The subleading logarithms are governed by the so called “collinear anomalous dimension”, γGsubscript𝛾𝐺gammaG, which is defined by ∫0μ2d​λ2λ2​G​(1,αs​(λ,ϵ),ϵ)=∫0μ2d​λ2λ2​γG​(αs​(λ,ϵ))+𝒪​(ϵ0),superscriptsubscript0superscript𝜇2𝑑superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2𝐺1subscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵitalic-ϵsuperscriptsubscript0superscript𝜇2𝑑superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2subscript𝛾𝐺subscript𝛼𝑠𝜆italic-ϵ𝒪superscriptitalic-ϵ0displaystyleint0mu2fracdlambda2lambda2G(1,alphas(lambda,epsilon),epsilon)=int0mu2fracdlambda2lambda2gammaGbig(alphas(lambda,epsilon)big)+O(epsilon0),, where it is understood that all the poles are absorbed by the integration with γGsubscript𝛾𝐺gammaG. The collinear anomalous dimension is known analytically to four loops in planar 𝒩=4𝒩4N=4 SYM citebib.bib93 (for an earlier numerical calculation, see citebib.bib94), and has been computed numerically in non-planar 𝒩=4𝒩4N=4 SYM citebib.bib78, bib.bib79. In QCD there has been an extensive program computing the four loop form factors. In particular, it was computed in the planar limit in citebib.bib33, bib.bib31, the nf3superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑓3nf3 terms were computed in citebib.bib32, the nf2superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑓2nf2 terms were computed in citebib.bib34, bib.bib37 and quartic color factor contributions were computed in citebib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib41. Very recently all matter dependent terms were obtained in citebib.bib40 (using previous results e. g. citebib.bib95, bib.bib96), and finally the full four loop form factors were computed citebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45. The anomalous dimension that we are ultimately interested in, and which is closely related to the rapidity anomalous dimension is the “soft anomalous dimension”, γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS, which can be expressed in terms of the anomalous dimensions described above as citebib.bib91, bib.bib97, bib.bib92 γG−2​Bδ=feik=−2​γS. subscript𝛾𝐺2subscript𝐵𝛿subscript𝑓eik2subscript𝛾𝑆displaystylegammaG-2Bdelta=ftexteik=-2gammaS,. The relation connecting collinear, virtual, and soft anomalous dimension, , can be easily understood by considering threshold resummation using SCET, where all these three anomalous dimension appear, see e. g. citebib.bib98, bib.bib99, bib.bib100, bib.bib101. The soft anomalous dimension is sometimes also referred to as feiksubscript𝑓eikftexteik, since it can be thought of as an eikonal version of the collinear anomalous dimension. For a recent discussion of this relation and other similar relations, see citebib.bib92. This anomalous dimension controls soft gluon radiation from Wilson lines, as we will describe in more detail in Sec. , and describes for example, Drell-Yan or gluon fusion at threshold, or dijet event shapes in the back-to-back limit, see e. g. citebib.bib102, bib.bib101, bib.bib103. Using the known perturbative data for γGsubscript𝛾𝐺gammaG, and Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta, the soft anomalous dimensions were assembled using Eq. in citebib.bib41, bib.bib46 (additional data was recently provided in citebib.bib40 has been incorporated in our results). The nf​CF2​Crsubscript𝑛𝑓superscriptsubscript𝐶𝐹2subscript𝐶𝑟nfCF2Cr and nf​CF​CA​Crsubscript𝑛𝑓subscript𝐶𝐹subscript𝐶𝐴subscript𝐶𝑟nfCFCACr and all other coefficients in the collinear anomalous dimension were computed analytically (or with high numerical precisions ) in citebib.bib40, bib.bib42, and by combing all the knowledges for the anomalous dimensions, especially from the study of Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta in citebib.bib41, bib.bib46, it turns out that our results only need four of the numeric values collected in Table 1 of the workcitebib.bib41. The extra numeric values are linearly dependent on those in Table, and they are numerically consistent as compared with the numbers collected in Table 1 of citebib.bib41. At four loops, strict Casimir scaling is violated for both the cusp anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension citebib.bib104, bib.bib67, bib.bib77, bib.bib78, bib.bib79, bib.bib27, bib.bib105, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37. However a generalized Casimir scaling holds for the cusp, and this is inherited by the soft anomalous dimension, since they exhibit the same maximally non-abelian structure citebib.bib97, bib.bib102, bib.bib91. The contributions that break naive Casimir scaling are due to the presence of the quartic Casimirs citebib.bib106 dF​Fsubscriptsuperscript𝑑4𝐹𝐹displaystyle dFF =(Nc4−6​Nc2+18)​(Nc2−1)96​Nc2,absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐46superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐218superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2196superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2displaystyle=frac(Nc4-6Nc2+18)(Nc2-1)96Nc2,, dF​Asubscriptsuperscript𝑑4𝐹𝐴displaystyle dFA =dA​F=Nc​(Nc2+6)​(Nc2−1)48,absentsubscriptsuperscript𝑑4𝐴𝐹subscript𝑁𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐26superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2148displaystyle=dAF=fracNc(Nc2+6)(Nc2-1)48,, dA​Asubscriptsuperscript𝑑4𝐴𝐴displaystyle dAA =Nc2​(Nc2+36)​(Nc2−1)24. absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐236superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2124displaystyle=fracNc2(Nc2+36)(Nc2-1)24,. The coefficients of these terms in the cusp and form factors were computed in citebib.bib29, bib.bib36, bib.bib35. The coefficients of these quartic Casimirs in both the cusp and eikonal anomalous dimensions exhibit a generalized Casimir scaling, namely that the contributions to the eikonal anomalous dimension from quartic Casimirs are determined by only two functions, instead of three citebib.bib29, bib.bib107. We have employed the generalized Casimir scaling to present the soft function anomalous dimension for generic SU(Ncsubscript𝑁𝑐Nc) representation r𝑟r in . We will also exploit this relation later in this paper to obtain the rapidity anomalous dimensions for both quarks and gluons.
content_sections.3 The Rapidity Anomalous Dimension: The rapidity anomalous dimension governs the rapidity evolution of TMD quantities. Its calculation typically requires a an definition of rapidity regulator. Here we adopt the exponential regulatorcitebib.bib108, which has been successfully applied at three-loop ordercitebib.bib58, bib.bib109, bib.bib110, bib.bib111, bib.bib112. Other definitions of rapidity regulator can be found in citebib.bib113, bib.bib114, bib.bib115, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib116, bib.bib117, bib.bib118. We can define the following matrix element of soft Wilson lines(in the following we use fundamental Wilson lines as explicit example, while our conclusion apply equally to adjoint Wilson lines. ) SEEC​(b→⟂,μ,ν)=limν→+∞1Nc​Tr​⟨0|T​[Sn†​​Sn¯​]​T¯​[Sn¯†​(yν​(b→⟂))​Sn​(yν​(b→⟂))]|0⟩,subscript𝑆EECsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈subscript→𝜈1subscript𝑁𝑐Trquantum-operator-product0𝑇delimited-subscriptsuperscript𝑆†𝑛0subscript𝑆¯𝑛0¯𝑇delimited-subscriptsuperscript𝑆†¯𝑛subscript𝑦𝜈subscript→𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑆𝑛subscript𝑦𝜈subscript→𝑏perpendicular-to0displaystyle StextEEC(vecbperp,mu,nu)=limnuto+inftyfrac1NcTr 0|Tleft[SdaggernSbarnright]barTleft[Sdaggerbarnleft(ynu(vecbperp)right)Snleft(ynu(vecbperp)right)right]|0,, where yν​(b→⟂)=(i​b0/ν,i​b0/ν,b→⟂),b0=2​e−γE,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝜈subscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝑖subscript𝑏0𝜈𝑖subscript𝑏0𝜈subscript→𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏02superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸displaystyle ynu(vecbperp)=(ib0/nu,ib0/nu,vecbperp),,qquad b0=2e-gammaE,, and the soft Wilson line is defined as Sn​(x)=P​exp⁡(i​gs​∫0∞𝑑s​n⋅As​(x+s​n)). subscript𝑆𝑛𝑥𝑃𝑖subscript𝑔𝑠superscriptsubscript0⋅differential-d𝑠𝑛subscript𝐴𝑠𝑥𝑠𝑛Sn(x)=Pexpleft(igsint0infty!ds,ncdot As(x+sn)right),. This configuration is shown in Fig. . This particular matrix element describes soft gluons in transverse momentum resummation, and certain e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- dijet event shapes in the back-to-back limit. Here we have chosen the orientation of the Wilson lines as appropriate for the case of an e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- event shape. For the case of qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT, the orientation of the Wilson lines is flipped, but this does not change the associated anomalous dimensionscitebib.bib119, bib.bib120, at least through to three loops. This matrix element exhibits divergences that are regulated by dimensional regularization, leading to an RG equation in μ𝜇mu, and divergences that are regulated by ν𝜈nu, leading to a rapidity renormalization group in ν𝜈nu. The RG equation in μ𝜇mu is given by μ​d​SEEC​(b→⟂,μ,ν)d​μ=[2​Γcusp​(αs)​ln⁡μ2ν2−4​γS​(αs)]​SEEC​(b→⟂,μ,ν),𝜇𝑑subscript𝑆EECsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈𝑑𝜇delimited-2subscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠superscript𝜇2superscript𝜈24subscript𝛾𝑆subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑆EECsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈displaystylemufracdStextEEC(vecbperp,mu,nu)dmu=left[2Gammatextcusp(alphas)lnfracmu2nu2-4gammaS(alphas)right]StextEEC(vecbperp,mu,nu),, and is governed by the cusp anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension, γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS described above. The evolution in ν𝜈nu is given bycitebib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib584Note that the convention for γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR is such that it differs from γrsubscript𝛾𝑟gammar in citebib.bib58 by a normalization, 2​γR=γr2subscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛾𝑟2gammaR=gammar. Similarly, the soft anomalous dimension also differed by a factor of 222 compared with γssubscript𝛾𝑠gammas in citebib.bib58, 2​γS=γs2subscript𝛾𝑆subscript𝛾𝑠2gammaS=gammas. ν​d​SEEC​(b→⟂,μ,ν)d​ν=[−2​∫b02/b→⟂2μ2d​μ¯2μ¯2​Γcusp​(αs​(μ¯))+4​γR​(αs​(b0/|b→⟂|))]​SEEC​(b→⟂,μ,ν),𝜈𝑑subscript𝑆EECsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈𝑑𝜈delimited-2superscriptsubscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑏02superscriptsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to2superscript𝜇2𝑑superscript¯𝜇2superscript¯𝜇2subscriptΓcuspsubscript𝛼𝑠¯𝜇4subscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑏0subscript→𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑆EECsubscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝜈displaystylenufracdStextEEC(vecbperp,mu,nu)dnu=left[-2intlimitsb02/vecbperp2mu2fracdbarmu2barmu2Gammatextcusp(alphas(barmu))+4gammaR(alphas(b0/|vecbperp|))right]StextEEC(vecbperp,mu,nu),, which is governed by the cusp anomalous dimension, and the rapidity anomalous dimension γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR. Identical evolution equations hold for the case of transverse momentum resummation. The goal of this paper will be to compute the rapidity anomalous dimension to four loops in QCD, which completes the scale understanding of this matrix element at four loops. This soft function was computed to three loops in citebib.bib58, and there it was noticed that in a conformal theory one has the relationship γR=γS. subscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛾𝑆gammaR=gammaS,. Note that the rapidity anomalous dimension, begin a evolution equation in rapidity, contains higher order terms in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon, which are neglected in . This relation was subsequently generalized to all orders in citebib.bib59, bib.bib60, where the relation γR​(ϵ∗)=γSsubscript𝛾𝑅superscriptitalic-ϵsubscript𝛾𝑆displaystylegammaR(epsilon*)=gammaS was obtained, with explicit dependence of ϵitalic-ϵepsilon replaced by ϵ∗superscriptitalic-ϵepsilon*, which is determined by equation β∗​(ϵ∗)≡1αs​β​(αs)−2​ϵ∗=0superscript𝛽superscriptitalic-ϵ1subscript𝛼𝑠𝛽subscript𝛼𝑠2superscriptitalic-ϵ0beta*(epsilon*)equivfrac1alphasbeta(alphas)-2epsilon*=0 order-by-order in QCD. In other words, it is the critical dimension where QCD is conformal order by order in perturbation theory. This relationship allows us to compute γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR at a given loop order by knowing γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS at that loop order, and γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR to higher orders in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon at lower loop orders. This approach has been utilized in citebib.bib59 to reproduce the three-loop γRsubscript𝛾𝑅gammaR from a two-loop calculation and knowledge of the three-loop γSsubscript𝛾𝑆gammaS. We will adopt this approach in this paper. For a recent formal study of the uses of conformal symmetry in d=4−2​ϵ𝑑42italic-ϵd=4-2epsilon, see citebib.bib121.
content_sections.4 Proof of Soft-Rapidity Correspondence: Since we will exploit heavily the correspondence between the rapidity anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension in a CFT, in this section we give an independent proof of this fact using the energy-energy correlator (EEC) observable. This proof exploits the universality of factorization, combined with the fact that the EEC also admits a representation in terms of a Euclidean four point function. By comparing two distinct results for the EEC we are able to prove γR=γSsubscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛾𝑆gammaR=gammaS in a conformal theory. The EEC admits a timelike factorization formula citebib.bib65 derived in SCET citebib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57 d​σd​z=𝑑𝜎𝑑𝑧absentdisplaystylefracdsigmadz= 14​∫0∞𝑑b​b​J0​(b​Q​1−z)​H​(Q,μh)​jEECq​(b,b0/b,Q)​jEECq¯​(b,b0/b,Q)​SEEC​(b,μs,νs)14superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑏𝑏subscript𝐽0𝑏𝑄1𝑧𝐻𝑄subscript𝜇ℎsubscriptsuperscript𝑗𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscriptsuperscript𝑗¯𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscript𝑆EEC𝑏subscript𝜇𝑠subscript𝜈𝑠displaystyle;frac14intlimits0inftydb,bJ0(bQsqrt1-z)H(Q,muh)jqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)jbarqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)StextEEC(b,mus,nus) ⋅(Q2νs2)2​γR​(αs​(b0/b))exp[∫μs2μh2d​μ¯2μ¯2Γcusp(αs(μ¯))lnb2​μ¯2b02displaystyle;cdotleft(fracQ2nus2right)2gammaR(alphas(b0/b))expleft[intlimitsmus2muh2fracdbarmu2barmu2Gammatextcusp(alphas(barmu))lnfracb2barmu2b02right. +∫μh2b02/b2d​μ¯2μ¯2(Γcusp(αs(μ¯))lnb2​Q2b02+γH(αs(μ¯)))−∫μs2b02/b2d​μ¯2μ¯2γS(αs(μ¯))],displaystyle;left. +intlimitsmuh2b02/b2fracdbarmu2barmu2left(Gammatextcusp(alphas(barmu))lnfracb2Q2b02+gammaH(alphas(barmu))right)-intlimitsmus2b02/b2fracdbarmu2barmu2gammaS(alphas(barmu))right],, where γH=−γG=−2​Bδ+2​γSsubscript𝛾𝐻subscript𝛾𝐺2subscript𝐵𝛿2subscript𝛾𝑆gammaH=-gammaG=-2Bdelta+2gammaS is the collinear anomalous dimension. This factorization formula is identical in structure to that for pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT resummation. The latter has been proven rigorously, including the cancellation of Glauber gluons citebib.bib122. It has been used to compute the EEC at NLL’ citebib.bib123 using results for the three-loop transverse momentum dependent fragmentation functions citebib.bib112, bib.bib111. For other studies of the EEC in the back-to-back limit, see citebib.bib124, bib.bib125, bib.bib126, bib.bib127. This formula applies in both conformal and non-conformal gauge theories, however, to prove the equality of the soft and rapidity anomalous dimensions, we can consider this formula in the conformal limit. After setting all scales equal to their canonical values such that all large logarithms are absorbed into the Sudakov exponent, μh=Q,μs=b0b,νs=b0b,formulae-sequencesubscript𝜇ℎ𝑄formulae-sequencesubscript𝜇𝑠subscript𝑏0𝑏subscript𝜈𝑠subscript𝑏0𝑏muh=Q,,qquadmus=fracb0b,,qquadnus=fracb0b,, and using the fact that the coupling constant does not run in a conformal theory, we have d​σd​z=𝑑𝜎𝑑𝑧absentdisplaystylefracdsigmadz= 14​∫0∞𝑑b​b​J0​(b​Q​1−z)​H​(Q,μh)​jEECq​(b,b0/b,Q)​jEECq¯​(b,b0/b,Q)​SEEC​(b,μs,νs)14superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑏𝑏subscript𝐽0𝑏𝑄1𝑧𝐻𝑄subscript𝜇ℎsubscriptsuperscript𝑗𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscriptsuperscript𝑗¯𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscript𝑆EEC𝑏subscript𝜇𝑠subscript𝜈𝑠displaystyle;frac14intlimits0inftydb,bJ0(bQsqrt1-z)H(Q,muh)jqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)jbarqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)StextEEC(b,mus,nus) exp⁡[−12​Γcusp​log2⁡(b2​Q2b02)+2​Bδ​log⁡(b2​Q2b02)+2​(γR−γS)​log⁡(b2​Q2b02)].12subscriptΓcuspsuperscript2superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏022subscript𝐵𝛿superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏022subscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛾𝑆superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏02displaystyleexpleft[-frac12Gammatextcusplog2left(fracb2Q2b02right)+2Bdeltalogleft(fracb2Q2b02right)+2(gammaR-gammaS)logleft(fracb2Q2b02right)right],. The EEC is special in that it also admits a representation as a four point Wightman function citebib.bib128, bib.bib129, bib.bib130, enabling it to be studied using a completely different set of techniques from conformal field theory. In particular, the EEC can be written as EEC∼∫d4​x​ei​x⋅q​⟨0|Jμ​​ℰ​(n→1)​ℰ​(n→2)​Jμ​(x)|0⟩,similar-toEECsuperscript𝑑4𝑥superscript𝑒⋅𝑖𝑥𝑞quantum-operator-product0subscript𝐽𝜇0ℰsubscript→𝑛1ℰsubscript→𝑛2subscript𝐽𝜇𝑥0displaystyletextEECsimint d4xeixcdot q 0|JmuE(vecn1)E(vecn2)Jmu(x)|0,, where ℰ​(n→)=∫0∞𝑑t​limr→∞r2​ni​T0​i​(t,r​n→),ℰ→𝑛superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑡subscript→𝑟superscript𝑟2superscript𝑛𝑖subscript𝑇0𝑖𝑡𝑟→𝑛displaystyleE(vecn)=intlimits0inftydtlimrtoinftyr2niT0i(t,rvecn),, is an energy flow operator citebib.bib131, bib.bib132, bib.bib133, bib.bib128, bib.bib130, bib.bib129, bib.bib134. It is therefore expressible as an integral kernel acting on the four point function of stress tensors. Using an understanding of the behavior of the four point function as operators become lightlike separated citebib.bib135, bib.bib136 and the high spin limit of the twist two operators Eq. , Korchemsky proved in citebib.bib64 that the EEC in a conformal theory is governed by the anomalous dimensions ΓcuspsuperscriptΓcuspGammacusp and Bδsubscript𝐵𝛿Bdelta only, and is described by the formula5See e. g. Eq. (1.5) of citebib.bib64. We have converted it to the conventions used in this paper. d​σd​z=𝑑𝜎𝑑𝑧absentdisplaystylefracdsigmadz= 14​∫0∞𝑑b​b​J0​(b​Q​1−z)​H​(Q,μh)​jEECq​(b,b0/b,Q)​jEECq¯​(b,b0/b,Q)​SEEC​(b,μs,νs)14superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑏𝑏subscript𝐽0𝑏𝑄1𝑧𝐻𝑄subscript𝜇ℎsubscriptsuperscript𝑗𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscriptsuperscript𝑗¯𝑞EEC𝑏subscript𝑏0𝑏𝑄subscript𝑆EEC𝑏subscript𝜇𝑠subscript𝜈𝑠displaystyle;frac14intlimits0inftydb,bJ0(bQsqrt1-z)H(Q,muh)jqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)jbarqtextEEC(b,b0/b,Q)StextEEC(b,mus,nus) exp⁡[−12​Γcusp​log2⁡(b2​Q2b02)+2​Bδ​log⁡(b2​Q2b02)].12subscriptΓcuspsuperscript2superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏022subscript𝐵𝛿superscript𝑏2superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝑏02displaystyleexpleft[-frac12Gammatextcusplog2left(fracb2Q2b02right)+2Bdeltalogleft(fracb2Q2b02right)right],. By comparing this formula derived from the four point function with the timelike factorization formula in Eq. , we immediately prove γR=γSsubscript𝛾𝑅subscript𝛾𝑆gammaR=gammaS in a CFT. We therefore arrive at a transparent proof of the relation between soft and rapidity anomalous dimensions.6It is interesting to note that non-trivial relations between anomalous dimensions (in particular the Basso-Korchemsky reciprocity citebib.bib137) can also be derived by studying equivalent expressions for the EECs in the collinear limit. Ref. citebib.bib138 provides an explicit example of such relation at three loops in QCD. On the one hand they can be expressed in terms of spacelike anomalous dimensions using the light-ray OPE citebib.bib128, bib.bib139, bib.bib64, and on the other hand they can be expressed in terms of timelike anomalous dimensions using factorization formulas derived in SCET citebib.bib140, bib.bib141. These relations suggest much is still to be understood about Lorentzian dynamics. Although this relation is sufficient for our purposes, it would be interesting to extend this to study the behavior of rapidity anomalous dimensions for soft functions that have Wilson lines in greater than two directions. Higher point energy correlators exhibit Sudakov behavior in particular kinematic limits, and this has interesting applications at hadron colliders citebib.bib124. These singular regions can be accessed also on the correlator side. However, unlike the more well studied case citebib.bib135, for three-point correlators and higher the operators become lightlike separated, but not in a sequential limit.
content_sections.5 The N3LO Fully Differential Soft Funct: In this section we describe the methods used to compute the NLO fully differential soft function to 𝒪​(ϵ)𝒪italic-ϵO(epsilon) at three loops, from which we will extract the rapidity anomalous dimension, retaining higher-order dependence in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. The fully differential soft function was introduced incitebib.bib61 for the study of transverse momentum resummation. It has been used incitebib.bib142 as a deformation of the threshold soft function, and in citebib.bib108 as a deformation of TMD soft function. It has also been exploited to calculate Sudakov radiator for jet observablecitebib.bib143. It is mostly conveniently defined on the exponent, ℱ​(q)≡∫∏idd​qi(2​π)d−1​δ+​(qi2)​(2​π)d​δ(d)​(q−∑iqi)​𝒲n​n¯​(qi),ℱ𝑞subscriptproduct𝑖superscript𝑑𝑑subscript𝑞𝑖superscript2𝜋𝑑1subscript𝛿superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑖2superscript2𝜋𝑑superscript𝛿𝑑𝑞subscript𝑖subscript𝑞𝑖subscript𝒲𝑛¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑖displaystyleF(q)equivintprodifracddqi(2pi)d-1,delta+(qi2)(2pi)ddelta(d)(q-sumiqi)Wnbarn(qi),, where additional symmetry factor for identical final-state particle is implicitly understood. The integrand 𝒲n​n¯​(qi)subscript𝒲𝑛¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑖Wnbarn(qi) corresponds to single or multiple soft emissions web diagrams from two lightlike Wilson lines Snsubscript𝑆𝑛Sn and Sn¯subscript𝑆¯𝑛Sbarn, such that non-abelian exponentiation theorem is made manifest whenever the observable have that property. Definition of web diagrams can be found, e. g. , in citebib.bib144, bib.bib145. The main task of the work here is to calculate the NNNLO fully differential soft function to higher orders in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. The TMD soft functioncitebib.bib58 and the threshold soft functioncitebib.bib103, bib.bib146, bib.bib147, bib.bib148 can then all be obtained as limits of the fully differential soft function. To perform the calculation, Feynman diagrams were generated by Qgraphcitebib.bib1497We note that several results for the soft web diagrams are available in the literature, namely the two-loop single soft currentcitebib.bib147, bib.bib148, one-loop double soft currentcitebib.bib119, bib.bib120, and tree-level triple soft currentcitebib.bib150. Furthermore, three-loop threshold function has also been obtained in citebib.bib151, bib.bib152, bib.bib103, bib.bib146, bib.bib153. We choose to generate the relevant soft currents using an independent code, therefore providing additional checks on the existing results. , color/Dirac algebra and integrand manipulations were performed in formcitebib.bib154. With the help of reverse unitaritycitebib.bib155, the integrals can be reduced with Integration-By-Parts identitiescitebib.bib156. We use the publicly available Mathematica package LiteRedcitebib.bib157. We obtained systems of master integrals to formulate differential equations with respect to n𝑛n and n¯¯𝑛barn rescaling invariant x𝑥x, x≡q2​(n⋅n¯)2​(q⋅n)​(q⋅n¯)=q2q+​q−,𝑥superscript𝑞2⋅𝑛¯𝑛2⋅𝑞𝑛⋅𝑞¯𝑛superscript𝑞2superscript𝑞superscript𝑞displaystyle xequivfracq2,(ncdotbarn)2(qcdot n),(qcdotbarn)=fracq2q+q-,, which can be solved using the method of differential equationcitebib.bib158. Note that the final-state total momentum satisfy q2≥0superscript𝑞20q2geq 0, x∈[0,1]𝑥01xin[0,1]. The differential equations allow an ϵitalic-ϵepsilon-formcitebib.bib159 with the help of CANONICAcitebib.bib160, after which we can derive the boundary constants by considering the limit x→1→𝑥1xto 1 and x→0→𝑥0xto 0. To all orders in perturbation, the unnormalized results can be written as ℱb​(q,ϵ)=∑j=0∞(asb)j+1​FjT,b​(x,ϵ)(q→⟂2)2+j​ϵ,superscriptℱb𝑞italic-ϵsuperscriptsubscript𝑗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠b𝑗1subscriptsuperscript𝐹𝑇b𝑗𝑥italic-ϵsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscript→𝑞perpendicular-to22𝑗italic-ϵFrm b(q,epsilon)=sumj=0infty(asrm b)j+1fracFT,rm bj(x,epsilon)(vecqperp,2)2+jepsilon,, where the variable x𝑥x is defined in and asb=αsb/(4​π)superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠bsuperscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠b4𝜋asrm b=alphasrm b/(4pi) is the bare coupling constant. We have retain the higher order in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon dependence in the definition of full differential soft function, in anticipating that further integration of q𝑞q can leads to poles in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. content_sections.6 Soft and Rapidity Anomalous Dimensions: In this section we present the main result of this paper, namely the four loop relation between the soft and rapidity anomalous dimensions in QCD obtained by computing the fully differential soft function at three loops to higher orders in ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. We then combine this with the perturbative data summarized earlier to give the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD. The bare TMD soft function with renormalized coupling is obtained by perform Fourier transformation and Laplace transformation to the fully differential soft function, and taking the rapidity
