For fundamental Wilson lines with generic Nfsubscript𝑁𝑓Nf, the numeric results through to four loops are γRq=superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑅qabsentdisplaystylegammaRrm q= as2​(7.46333+2.76543​Nf)+as3​(70.068+77.1286​Nf−4.54662​Nf2)superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠27.463332.76543subscript𝑁𝑓superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠370.06877.1286subscript𝑁𝑓4.54662superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑓2displaystyle as2(7.46333+2.76543Nf)+as3(70.068+77.1286Nf-4.54662Nf2) +as4​(−350.8+2428​Nf−378.3​Nf2+8.072​Nf3)+𝒪​(as5),superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠4350.82428subscript𝑁𝑓378.3superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑓28.072superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑓3𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠5displaystyle+as4(-350.8+2428Nf-378.3Nf2+8.072Nf3)+O(as5),, while for adjoint Wilson lines it is given by γRg=superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑅gabsentdisplaystylegammaRrm g= as2​(16.7925+6.22222​Nf)+as3​(157.653+173.539​Nf−10.2299​Nf2)superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠216.79256.22222subscript𝑁𝑓superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠3157.653173.539subscript𝑁𝑓10.2299superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑓2displaystyle as2(16.7925+6.22222Nf)+as3(157.653+173.539Nf-10.2299Nf2) +as4​(333.8+5506​Nf−851.2​Nf2+18.16​Nf3)+𝒪​(as5),superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠4333.85506subscript𝑁𝑓851.2superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑓218.16superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑓3𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠5displaystyle+as4(333.8+5506Nf-851.2Nf2+18.16Nf3)+O(as5),, These are the primary results of our paper. In Fig. , we plot the perturbative rapidity anomalous dimension through to four loops in QCD,8For non-perturbative calculations of the rapidity anomalous dimension or TMD soft function from lattice QCD, see e. g. citebib.bib161, bib.bib162, bib.bib163, bib.bib164, bib.bib165, bib.bib166. using fundamental Wilson lines as an example. The four-loop corrections are non-negligible, and should be taken into account in future phenomenological studies. We also show the four-loop results without the quartic Casimir corrections, which illustrates that these corrections are small. For the case of qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT resummation rapidity renormalization group consistency implies that the rapidity anomalous dimension given in Eq. also determines the beam function rapidity anomalous dimension to four loops.
content_sections.7 Conclusions: In this paper we have computed the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD. This result was derived by exploiting a relation between the rapidity anomalous dimension and the soft anomalous dimension in a conformal theory, and computing the corrections to this relation arising from conformal symmetry breaking in QCD. At a technical level this was achieved by computing the fully differential soft function to 𝒪​(ϵ)𝒪italic-ϵO(epsilon) at three loops. We also described in some detail how this calculation was performed with the hope that it can be useful for performing calculations of other rapidity divergent soft functions. In this paper we have also attempted to further clarify the nature of the rapidity anomalous dimension by presenting a simple proof relating the soft and rapidity anomalous dimensions using two equivalent representations of the the energy-energy correlator observable in the back-to-back limit. This relates the behavior of the four-point correlator citebib.bib64, with the rapidity anomalous dimension appearing in the time like factorization formula of citebib.bib65. The four loop rapidity anomalous dimension enables the resummation of the EEC in the back-to-back region at NLL, which is the first event shape for which all ingredients are available to achieve this perturbative accuracy. It will be particularly interesting to study the convergence of the perturbative series, as well as the phenomenological implications for extractions of the strong coupling. For the case of pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT resummation for the Higgs or electroweak pTsubscript𝑝𝑇pT spectra, the remaining ingredient required to achieve NLL resummation is the singlet four loop splitting functions. Our approach to computing the four loop rapidity anomalous dimension by exploiting its relation to other anomalous dimensions enabling it to be obtained through a three loop calculation illustrates the importance of better understanding relations between different anomalous dimensions in gauge theories. Much recent progress has been made along these lines citebib.bib92. An interesting generalization of the case considered in this paper is to understand the relation of the rapidity anomalous dimension for observables with more than two colored lines, such as the transverse EEC as relevant for hadron colliders citebib.bib124, and its relation to the soft anomalous dimension with multiple lines, which is known at three loops citebib.bib167, bib.bib168. In particular, it is interesting to understand whether the dipole conjecture holds for the rapidity anomalous dimension. Another rapidity type anomalous dimension that is difficult to compute directly is the Regge trajectory, which was recently computed in QCD to three loops citebib.bib169, bib.bib170, bib.bib171, bib.bib172, bib.bib173, bib.bib174, bib.bib175, bib.bib176, bib.bib177, bib.bib178, bib.bib179. With the wealth of four loop data for anomalous dimensions, we believe it is important to understand its relation to other anomalous dimensions to enable its calculation to higher orders, and to obtain a more complete understanding of the relations between infrared anomalous dimensions. Acknowledgements. We thank Alexey Vladimirov for useful discussions. We thank Claude Duhr, Bernhard Mistlberger and Gherardo Vita for coordinating the suission of their work. I. M. is supported by start-up funds from Yale University. H. X. Z is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China under contract No. 11975200. Note Added: Simultaneously with this paper, the work of citebib.bib180 appeared, which also presented a result for the anomalous dimension, we have verified that our result agrees.

all_papers.title:
Nonperturbative Collins-Soper Kernel from Chiral Quarks with Physical
  Masses
all_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice QCD calculation of the rapidity anomalous dimension of
quark transverse-momentum-dependent distributions, i.e., the Collins-Soper (CS)
kernel, up to transverse separations of about 1 fm. This unitary lattice
calculation is conducted, for the first time, employing the
chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermion discretization and physical
values of light and strange quark masses. The CS kernel is extracted from the
ratios of pion quasi-transverse-momentum-dependent wave functions
(quasi-TMDWFs) at next-to-leading logarithmic perturbative accuracy. Also for
the first time, we utilize the recently proposed Coulomb-gauge-fixed
quasi-TMDWF correlator without a Wilson line. We observe significantly slower
signal decay with increasing quark separations compared to the established
gauge-invariant method with a staple-shaped Wilson line. This enables us to
determine the CS kernel at large nonperturbative transverse separations and
find its near-linear dependence on the latter. Our result is consistent with
the recent lattice calculation using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs, and agrees
with various recent phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data.

qualitative: The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel governs the rapidity scale evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs), enabling consistent interpretation of experimental data across different energy scales.
The CS kernel is a nonperturbative quantity in QCD that must be determined from first principles due to the model dependence inherent in phenomenological parametrizations.
Lattice QCD provides a first-principles approach to compute the CS kernel via quasi-TMD wave functions (quasi-TMDWFs) within the Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) framework.

The conventional gauge-invariant (GI) approach to quasi-TMDWFs requires a staple-shaped Wilson line, which introduces complications such as linear divergences, pinch-pole singularities, operator mixing, and exponential signal decay with increasing quark separation.

The Coulomb-gauge-fixed (CG) approach eliminates the need for Wilson lines, leading to multiplicatively renormalizable correlators free from linear divergences and pinch singularities, and avoiding operator mixing from Wilson-line geometry.

The CG quasi-TMDWFs are shown to factorize in the large momentum limit and belong to the same universality class as GI quasi-TMDs under LaMET, validating their use for extracting physical observables.

The CG method exhibits significantly slower signal decay with increasing transverse quark separation compared to the GI method, enabling access to larger nonperturbative transverse separations (up to ~1 fm).
The CS kernel extracted from the CG approach shows a near-linear dependence on the transverse separation b⟂, consistent with expectations from perturbative and phenomenological models.
The result from the CG method agrees with previous lattice calculations using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs and with various phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data.
quantitative: The CS kernel is extracted from ratios of pion quasi-TMDWFs at next-to-leading logarithmic (NLL) perturbative accuracy.
The calculation is performed using physical light and strange quark masses with chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermions.
The transverse separation b⟂ is probed up to approximately 1 fm, which is a significant extension compared to previous methods.

The CS kernel exhibits near-linear dependence on b⟂, indicating a nonperturbative behavior consistent with the form γ_S(b⟂) ≈ γ_0 + γ_1 b⟂^2 (implied by linear dependence in b⟂, though exact functional form not specified in abstract).

The result is consistent with recent lattice calculations using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs and with phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data (e.g., from SIDIS and Drell-Yan processes).

authors: Dennis Bollweg, Xiang Gao, Swagato Mukherjee, and Yong Zhao

content_sections.I Introduction: The parton transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs) are crucial for a three-dimensional understanding of parton motions within a hadron, offering a more comprehensive view than traditional one-dimensional parton distribution functions (PDFs). It sheds light not only on the intrinsic motion of partons in transverse directions but also on the interplay between the transverse momentum of quarks and the spin of nucleons or quarks themselves. This comprehensive perspective is crucial for a deep understanding of the dynamic and complex nature of nucleons. The accurate characterization of TMDs is also critical for interpreting experimental data from high-energy collisions, particularly in relation to the transverse momentum distributions of electroweak and Higgs bosonscitebib.bib1, bib.bib2. They are fundamental to precision measurements, such as determining the mass and width of the W bosoncitebib.bib3, bib.bib4. As high-energy physics experiments continue to advance, the measurement of TMDs will become increasingly important. The ongoing and future experiments at facilities such as the Large Hadron Collidercitebib.bib5, bib.bib6 and Electron-Ion Collidercitebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10 are expected to profoundly enrich our knowledge of TMDs. This will not only enhance our grasp of hadron structure and nucleon spin but also contribute significantly to the broader field of particle physics. Central to the study and practical application of TMDs is the Collins-Soper (CS) kernel, which is responsible for the (rapidity) scale evolution of TMDscitebib.bib11, bib.bib12, enabling the consistent interpretation of experimental data across different energy scales. It is instrumental in connecting theoretical predictions with experimental observations. The TMDs and CS kernel can be extracted through the global analysis of experimental data including the semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering (SIDIS) and Drell-Yan processescitebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29. However, the nonperturbative nature of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) at low transverse momenta necessitates certain parametrizations and introduces model dependence. As a result, there is an increasing interest for these intrinsically nonperturbative quantities to be calculated directly from first-principles lattice QCD. Although direct simulation of TMDs on the Euclidean lattice is impractical, as TMDs are defined on the light-cone, it has been demonstrated that they can be accessed through quasi-TMDs within the framework of Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET)citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. The quasi-TMDs involve the matrix elements of equal-time gauge-invariant (GI) operators: OΓGI​(𝐛;η)=ψ¯​(𝐛2)​Γ​W⊐​(𝐛2,−𝐛2,η)​ψ​(−𝐛2),subscriptsuperscript𝑂GIΓ𝐛𝜂¯𝜓𝐛2Γsubscript𝑊square-original-of𝐛2𝐛2𝜂𝜓𝐛2displaystylebeginsplitOrm GIGamma(b;eta)=overlinepsi(fracb2)Gamma Wsqsupset(fracb2,-fracb2,eta)psi(-fracb2),endsplit with 𝐛=(b⟂,bz)𝐛subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧b=(bperp,bz) covering both longitudinal (bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz) and transverse (b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp) directions, linked by a staple-shaped Wilson line W⊐subscript𝑊square-original-ofWsqsupset whose length is characterized by η𝜂eta. In the large momentum and η→∞→𝜂etarightarrowinfty limit, the quasi-TMDs can be related to the light-cone TMDs through the perturbative factorizationcitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib36, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46. Building on this, significant advancements have been achieved over the past few years. The CS kernel has been extracted from either quasi-TMD parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs)citebib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, quasi-TMD wave functions (TMDWFs)citebib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib49, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, or the moments of the quasi-TMDscitebib.bib56. Lattice QCD calculations of soft functionscitebib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib53, along with the first results of nucleon TMD PDFscitebib.bib57 and pion TMDWFscitebib.bib58 also have been reported. Additionally, progress has also been made in the systematical control of these calculations, including improved matching up to two loopscitebib.bib59, bib.bib60, addressing the operator mixing and working at physical quark massescitebib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib54, bib.bib67, bib.bib55. Despite notable progress, the lattice calculation of TMDs remains challenging. To suppress power corrections, a large momentum Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz is required, which incurs a significant computational cost. In addition, the signal-to-noise ratio of the quasi-TMD matrix elements is adversely affected by exponential decay as the total length of the space-like Wilson line increases. This decay makes it particularly difficult to investigate quasi-TMDs at large b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp, where the results are desired to complement the phenomenological analysis. What’s more, the linear divergence and pinch-pole singularity in the Wilson lines also complicate the renormalization procedure, although they could be cancelled by the Wilson loopcitebib.bib68 or held fixed by keeping a constant length of the Wilson line for given b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperpcitebib.bib54. Besides, the operator mixings of Wilson-line operatorscitebib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66 also need to be subtracted systematicallycitebib.bib54. Recently, a novel approach has been proposed for computing parton physics in the Coulomb gauge (CG)citebib.bib69, notably without the use of Wilson lines. Thereby, the complexity induced by the Wilson line can be avoided. It has been demonstrated that, in the large momentum limit, the CG quasi-PDF falls into the same universality class as the GI case under the LaMET framework. Further progress has been made in the realm of quasi-TMDscitebib.bib70, involving the matrix elements of equal-time operators, OΓCG​(𝐛)=ψ¯​(𝐛2)​Γ​ψ​(−𝐛2)|∇⋅A=0,subscriptsuperscript𝑂CGΓ𝐛evaluated-at¯𝜓𝐛2Γ𝜓𝐛2⋅∇A0displaystylebeginsplitOrm CGGamma(b)=overlinepsi(fracb2)Gammapsi(-fracb2)|nablacdotA=0,endsplit with the CG condition ∇⋅A=0⋅∇A0nablacdotA=0 but without a Wilson line. The factorization of quasi-TMDs in the CG has been derived from the soft collinear effective theory (SCET)citebib.bib70 and verified at one loop in perturbation theorycitebib.bib70, bib.bib71. In this study, we have, for the first time, computed the quasi-TMDWFs of the pion in the CG and extracted the CS kernel from these measurements. Without Wilson lines, the CG correlators are multiplicatively renormalizable and free from the linear divergencecitebib.bib69 and pinch singularity, as well as the operator mixings originating from the Wilson line geometry. Through our calculation, we show that, the CG approach leads to consistent CS kernel with the conventional GI approach. Moreover, the CG approach can significantly reduce the signal-to-noise ratio and extend the prediction power of lattice computation in the nonperturbative regime of interest to TMD physics.
content_sections.II Theoretical framework: The pion quasi-TMDWF in the CG is defined as the Fourier transform of the matrix elements: ϕΓCG​(b⟂,bz,Pz,μ)=⟨Ω|OΓCG​(𝐛)|π+;Pz⟩,superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕΓCGsubscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜇quantum-operator-productΩsubscriptsuperscript𝑂CGΓ𝐛superscript𝜋subscript𝑃𝑧displaystyletildephiGammarm CG(bperp,bz,Pz,mu)=Omega|Orm CGGamma(b)|pi+;Pz, where the pion is boosted with momentum P=(0,0,Pz)P00subscript𝑃𝑧P=(0,0,Pz). By selecting Γ=γt​γ5Γsubscript𝛾𝑡subscript𝛾5Gamma=gammatgamma5 or γz​γ5subscript𝛾𝑧subscript𝛾5gammazgamma5, the quasi-TMDWF ϕΓ​(x,b⟂,Pz,μ)subscriptitalic-ϕΓ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃𝑧𝜇tildephiGamma(x,bperp,Pz,mu) can be related to the light-cone TMDWF ϕ​(x,b⟂,ζ,μ)italic-ϕ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜁𝜇phi(x,bperp,zeta,mu) (under the principle-value prescription of the light-cone Wilson linescitebib.bib72) in the large Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz limit through perturbative factorization, which can be expressed ascitebib.bib37, bib.bib42, bib.bib70, ϕΓ​(x,b⟂,Pz,μ)Sr​(b⟂,μ)=H​(x,x¯,Pz,μ)​ϕ​(x,b⟂,ζ,μ)​exp⁡[14​(ln⁡(2​x​Pz)2ζ+ln⁡(2​x¯​Pz)2ζ)​γMS¯​(b⟂,μ)]+𝒪​(ΛQCD2(x​Pz)2,1(b⟂​(x​Pz))2,ΛQCD2(x¯​Pz)2,1(b⟂​(x¯​Pz))2),subscriptitalic-ϕΓ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃𝑧𝜇subscript𝑆𝑟subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝐻𝑥¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧𝜇italic-ϕ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜁𝜇14superscript2𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2𝜁superscript2¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2𝜁superscript𝛾¯MSsubscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝒪superscriptsubscriptΛQCD2superscript𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧21superscriptsubscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2superscriptsubscriptΛQCD2superscript¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧21superscriptsubscript𝑏perpendicular-to¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2displaystylebeginsplitfractildephiGamma(x,bperp,Pz,mu)sqrtSr(bperp,mu)=H(x,barx,Pz,mu)phi(x,bperp,zeta,mu)expleft[frac14left(lnfrac(2xPz)2zeta+lnfrac(2barxPz)2zetaright)gammaoverlinerm MS(bperp,mu)right] +Oleft(fracLambdarm QCD2(xPz)2,frac1(bperp(xPz))2,fracLambdarm QCD2(barxPz)2,frac1(bperp(barxPz))2right),endsplit with x¯=1−x¯𝑥1𝑥barx=1-x. γMS¯​(b⟂,μ)superscript𝛾¯MSsubscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇gammaoverlinerm MS(bperp,mu) is the CS kernel that governs the rapidity scale evolution from ζ𝜁zeta to (2​x​Pz)2superscript2𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2(2xPz)2 (or (2​x¯​Pz)2superscript2¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2(2barxPz)2). Consequently, the x𝑥x-dependent light-cone TMDWF can be derived, subject to power corrections that are suppressed by large Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz and b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp. content_sections.III Lattice setup: The bare matrix elements of the pion quasi-TMDWF can be extracted from the two-point correlation functions in the lattice simulations. For CG quasi-TMDWFs, we compute, Cπ​OCG​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz)=⟨OΓCG​(𝐛,𝐏,ts)​π†​(y0,0)⟩,subscriptsuperscript𝐶CG𝜋𝑂subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧delimited-⟨⟩subscriptsuperscript𝑂CGΓ𝐛𝐏subscript𝑡𝑠superscript𝜋†subscripty00displaystyle Crm CGpiO(ts;bperp,bz,Pz)=left Orm CGGamma(b,P,ts)pidagger(y0,0)right, with, OΓCG​(𝐛,𝐏,ts)=∑𝐲d¯​(𝐲+𝐛2,ts)​Γ​u​(𝐲−𝐛2,ts)|∇⋅A=0​e−i​𝐏⋅(𝐲−𝐲0). subscriptsuperscript𝑂CGΓ𝐛𝐏subscript𝑡𝑠evaluated-atsubscript𝐲¯𝑑𝐲𝐛2subscript𝑡𝑠Γ𝑢𝐲𝐛2subscript𝑡𝑠⋅∇A0superscript𝑒⋅𝑖𝐏𝐲subscript𝐲0displaystylebeginsplitOrm CGGamma(b,P,ts)=sumyoverlined(y+fracb2,ts)Gamma u(y-fracb2,ts)|nablacdotA=0e-iPcdot(y-y0). endsplit Here y0subscripty0y0 is the source position, and tssubscript𝑡𝑠ts is the time separation. We chose Γ=γt​γ5Γsubscript𝛾𝑡subscript𝛾5Gamma=gammatgamma5, as it should be free from the operator mixings caused by chiral symmetry breakingcitebib.bib62, bib.bib64, bib.bib65 under our lattice setup. To improve the signal-to-noise ratio and increase the overlap with the pion ground state, we used extended pion source after boosted Gaussian smearingcitebib.bib73, π†​(𝐲,ts)=u¯s​(𝐲,ts)​γ5​ds​(𝐲,ts),superscript𝜋†𝐲subscript𝑡𝑠subscript¯𝑢𝑠𝐲subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝛾5subscript𝑑𝑠𝐲subscript𝑡𝑠displaystylepidagger(y,ts)=overlineus(y,ts)gamma5ds(y,ts), with the s𝑠s denoting the smeared fields. We also compute the pion-pion two-point functions, Cπ​π​(ts,Pz)=⟨π​(𝐏,ts)​π†​(𝐲0,0)⟩,subscript𝐶𝜋𝜋subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑃𝑧delimited-⟨⟩𝜋𝐏subscript𝑡𝑠superscript𝜋†subscript𝐲00displaystyle Cpipi(ts,Pz)=leftpi(P,ts)pidagger(y0,0)right, with smeared source and sink to extract the energy spectrum created by π†superscript𝜋†pidagger as well as the overlap amplitudes. For the lattice simulation, we utilized a 2+1-flavor Domain-wall gauge ensemble generated by RBC and UKQCD Collaborations of size Ns3×Nt×N5=643×128×12superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑠3subscript𝑁𝑡subscript𝑁5superscript64312812Ns3times Nttimes N5=643times 128times 12, denoted by 64Icitebib.bib74. The quark masses are at the physical point and the lattice spacing is a−1superscript𝑎1a-1 = 2.3549 GeV (a=0.0836𝑎0.0836a=0.0836 fm). For the boosted Gaussian smearing, the Gaussian radius was chosen to be rGsubscript𝑟𝐺rG = 0.58 fm, and we chose the quark boost parameter jzsubscript𝑗𝑧jz to be 0 and 6citebib.bib75, bib.bib76 which are optimal to hadron momentum Pz=2​π​nz/(Ns​a)subscript𝑃𝑧2𝜋subscript𝑛𝑧subscript𝑁𝑠𝑎Pz=2pi nz/(Nsa) with nzsubscript𝑛𝑧nz = 0 and 8, so that the largest momentum in our calculation is Pz=1.85subscript𝑃𝑧1.85Pz=1.85 GeV. Since only two-point functions are involved in this calculation, measurements at other momenta (nz∈[0,8]subscript𝑛𝑧08nzin[0,8]) were also computed through contractions using the same profiled quark propagator. To increase the statistics, we used 64 configurations coupled with All Mode Averaging (AMA) techniquecitebib.bib77. We computed 2 exact and 128 sloppy solutions for the quasi-TMDWFs with momenta nz∈[4,8]subscript𝑛𝑧48nzin[4,8], while 1 exact and 32 sloppy solutions for the cases with nz=[0,3]subscript𝑛𝑧03nz=[0,3]. The quark propagators are evaluated from CG-fixed configurations using deflation based solver with 2000 eigen vectors. After fixing the CG, the GI quasi-TMDWF defined from Eq. shares the same quark propagators as the CG case but needs an additional staple-shaped Wilson line to maintain the gauge invariance. Therefore, we also computed the GI quasi-TMDWF correlators Cπ​OGI​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz,η)subscriptsuperscript𝐶GI𝜋𝑂subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜂Crm GIpiO(ts;bperp,bz,Pz,eta) during the contraction. We chose η=12​a𝜂12𝑎eta=12a in this case using the same setup of the staple-shaped Wilson line as Ref. citebib.bib54. We employed Wilson flowcitebib.bib78, with a flow time tF=1.0subscript𝑡𝐹1.0tF=1.0 (roughly smears the gauge fields over the radius 8​a28superscript𝑎2sqrt8a2), to suppress the ultraviolet (UV) fluctuations and enhance the signal-to-noise ratio.
content_sections.IV Quasi-TMDWF: The pion-pion and quasi-TMDWF correlators have the following spectral decompositions, Cπ​π​(ts;Pz)=∑n=0Nst−1|Zn|22​En​(e−En​ts+e−En​(Lt−ts)),subscript𝐶𝜋𝜋subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑃𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑛0subscript𝑁st1superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑛22subscript𝐸𝑛superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝑡𝑠superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝐿𝑡subscript𝑡𝑠displaystylebeginsplitCpipi(ts;Pz)=sumn=0Nrm st-1frac|Zn|22Enleft(e-Ents+e-En(Lt-ts)right),endsplit and, Cπ​OCG​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz)=∑n=0Nst−1Zn2​En​⟨Ω|Oγt​γ5CG|n⟩​(e−En​ts+e−En​(Lt−ts)),superscriptsubscript𝐶𝜋𝑂CGsubscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑛0subscript𝑁st1subscript𝑍𝑛2subscript𝐸𝑛quantum-operator-productΩsuperscriptsubscript𝑂subscript𝛾𝑡subscript𝛾5CG𝑛superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝑡𝑠superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝐿𝑡subscript𝑡𝑠displaystylebeginsplitCpi Orm CG(ts;bperp,bz,Pz)=sumn=0Nrm st-1fracZn2EnOmega|Ogammatgamma5rm CG|n(e-Ents+e-En(Lt-ts)),endsplit where En​(Pz)subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝑃𝑧En(Pz) is the energy level, and Zn=⟨n|π†​(Pz)|Ω⟩subscript𝑍𝑛quantum-operator-product𝑛superscript𝜋†subscript𝑃𝑧ΩZn= n|pidagger(Pz)|Omega is the overlap amplitude created by the pion interpolator (real and positivecitebib.bib54). |Ω⟩ketΩ|Omega represents the vacuum state, while |n⟩=|0⟩,|1⟩,…ket𝑛ket0ket1…|n=|0,|1,. . . represents the ground state as well as the excited states. To take the advantage of high correlations between the pion-pion and quasi-TMDWF two-point functions, we construct their ratio as, R​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz)=−i​Cπ​OCG​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz)Cπ​π​(ts;Pz). 𝑅subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝑖superscriptsubscript𝐶𝜋𝑂CGsubscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝐶𝜋𝜋subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑃𝑧displaystylebeginsplitR(ts;bperp,bz,Pz)=frac-iCpi Orm CG(ts;bperp,bz,Pz)Cpipi(ts;Pz). endsplit In Fig. , the ratios of our largest momentum nz=8subscript𝑛𝑧8nz=8 at b⟂=10​asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to10𝑎bperp=10a are shown as an example. In the ts→∞→subscript𝑡𝑠tsrightarrowinfty limit, this ratio will reduce to ⟨Ω|Oγt​γ5|0⟩/Z0=E0​ϕB/Z0quantum-operator-productΩsubscript𝑂subscript𝛾𝑡subscript𝛾50subscript𝑍0subscript𝐸0superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵subscript𝑍0Omega|Ogammatgamma5|0/Z0=E0tildephiB/Z0 and gives the bare quasi-TMDWF matrix elements ϕB​(b⟂,bz,Pz,a)superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝑎tildephiB(bperp,bz,Pz,a). In practice, with finite tssubscript𝑡𝑠ts we truncate Eq. and Eq. up to Nst=2subscript𝑁st2Nrm st=2 and extract the bare matrix elements through the two-state fit. The fit results are shown as the bands in Fig. which can nicely describe the data point. The renormalization of CG quasi-TMD operator is straightforward. It involves only the CG quark wave function renormalization, which is an overall multiplicative constant and does not depend on the spatial separations b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp and bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bzcitebib.bib69. In contrast, the GI quasi-TMD operator defined in Eq. , though also subject to multiplicative renormalization, requires the removal of pinch pole singularities, cusp divergences, and linear divergences associated with the Wilson linecitebib.bib61, bib.bib79, bib.bib80, bib.bib68. This renormalization is proportional to the total link length. In our implementation, the total length of the staple-shape Wilson line is 2​η+b⟂2𝜂subscript𝑏perpendicular-to2eta+bperp, independent of bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz. Since the renormalization process solely involves the UV properties of operators and is independent of the external hadron states, we use the renormalization group invariant ratioscitebib.bib54 Φ​(b⟂,bz,Pz)=ϕB​(b⟂,bz,Pz,η,a)ϕB​(b⟂,0,0,η,a),Φsubscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜂𝑎superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵subscript𝑏perpendicular-to00𝜂𝑎displaystyletildePhi(bperp,bz,Pz)=fractildephiB(bperp,bz,Pz,eta,a)tildephiB(bperp,0,0,eta,a), without affecting the x𝑥x- and Pz−limit-fromsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz-dependences of the quasi-TMDWF after Fourier transform. The above ratio also may reduce some correlated uncertainties and eliminate some power corrections. Thus, we also adopt the same procedure for the CG matrix elements, whose renormalization is b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp and η𝜂eta independent. In Fig. , we show the renormalized matrix elements for our largest momentum, Pz=1.85subscript𝑃𝑧1.85Pz=1.85 GeV, and for b⟂=2​a,6​asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to2𝑎6𝑎bperp=2a,6a and 8​a8𝑎8a, as a function of bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz, for both CG (filled squared symbols) and GI (open circled symbols) cases. The left panel and right panel show the real and imaginary parts, respectively. It is evident that reasonable signal remains for the CG case even when b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp become large. In contrast, the signal-to-noise ratio of the GI matrix elements rapidly deteriorates as b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp increases, primarily due to the long Wilson line and its UV fluctuations. In addition, it is shown that the imaginary parts of the CG case are consistently zero, while they have non-zero values for GI case. This is expected as the imaginary part depends on the longitudinal orientation of the Wilson line in the GI casecitebib.bib72, whereas the CG condition does not favor any directioncitebib.bib70. One can also observe that the matrix elements decrease as a function of bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz, diminishing to zero within the errors when bz≳1greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑏𝑧1bzgtrsim 1 fm. This behavior facilitates the numerical Fourier transform to x𝑥x-space with a simple truncation at the maximum value of bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz, which is expressed as, Φ​(x,b⟂,Pz)=Pzπ​∫0bzmaxei​(x−12)​Pz​bz​Φ​(b⟂,bz,Pz)Φ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜋superscriptsubscript0superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑧maxsuperscript𝑒𝑖𝑥12subscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝑏𝑧Φsubscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧displaystyletildePhi(x,bperp,Pz)=fracPzpiint0bzrm maxei(x-frac12)PzbztildePhi(bperp,bz,Pz) where we apply a first-order spline interpolation to smooth the data points. Since the CG quasi-TMDWF correlator is real and symmetric in bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz, the distribution must be real in the x𝑥x-space. In the upper panel of Fig. , we show selected results of the CG quasi-TMDWFs with momentum nz∈[4,8]subscript𝑛𝑧48nzin[4,8] and b⟂=2​a,8​a,10​asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to2𝑎8𝑎10𝑎bperp=2a,8a,10a. Encouragingly, reasonable signal persists even when b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp is as large as 10​a10𝑎10a. However, the signal-to-noise ratio decreases as the momentum increases. In addition, it is evident that the quasi-TMDWFs, though appearing to be non-zero outside the physical region, have a trend to shrink into x∈[0,1]𝑥01xin[0,1] as the momentum increases. This observation is consistent with the power expansion of the LaMET, suggesting the quasi-TMDWF is approaching the light-cone TMDWF in the large momentum limit.
content_sections.V The Collins-Soper kernel: According to Eq. , we define the following estimator of the CS kernel utilizing the quasi-TMDWFs at finite momenta, γMS¯​(x,b⟂,P1,P2,μ)=1ln⁡(P2/P1)​ln⁡[Φ​(x,b⟂,P2)Φ​(x,b⟂,P1)]+δ​γMS¯​(x,μ,P1,P2). superscript𝛾¯MS𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝜇1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃1Φ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃2Φ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃1𝛿superscript𝛾¯MS𝑥𝜇subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2displaystylebeginsplithatgammaoverlinerm MS(x,bperp,P1,P2,mu)=frac1ln(P2/P1)lnleft[fractildePhi(x,bperp,P2)tildePhi(x,bperp,P1)right]+deltagammaoverlinerm MS(x,mu,P1,P2). endsplit In this work, we applied the perturbative corrections δ​γMS¯𝛿superscript𝛾¯MSdeltagammaoverlinerm MS derived from the next-to-leading logarithm (NLL) matching kernels for the CG casecitebib.bib38, bib.bib43, bib.bib54, bib.bib70 as only one-loop non-cusp anomalous dimension is available. The MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scale has been set to be μ=2𝜇2mu=2 GeV. If the power corrections and higher-order corrections are small, γMS¯superscript𝛾¯MShatgammaoverlinerm MS should be independent of Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz and x𝑥x. In the lower panels of Fig. , we show the CS kernel estimators for the CG case with various combination of momenta, n1subscript𝑛1n1 and n2subscript𝑛2n2, as a function of x𝑥x. The x𝑥x-independent plateaus can be found in the moderate x𝑥x region within the errors, which is robust even at the largest b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp. This indicates the effectiveness of the factorization formula in . In the end-point regions of both small and large x𝑥x, the results appear to diverge, signaling a breakdown of the factorization in these areas. However, the length of plateaus extend as the momentum increases, which is consistent with the power corrections suggested in Eq. . As for the momentum dependence, it is absent even for the case of large b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp due, which indicates well suppressed power corrections by 1/(Pz​b⟂)1subscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝑏perpendicular-to1/(Pzbperp), despite their slightly larger errors. However, results at small b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp (e. g. , for 2​a2𝑎2a) and with small momentum (e. g. , for n1=4subscript𝑛14n1=4) deviated from the ones derived from larger momenta. This momentum dependence is reduced when n1subscript𝑛1n1 and n2subscript𝑛2n2 gets close, and disappear when n1subscript𝑛1n1 and n2subscript𝑛2n2 are close enough (e. g. , for n1/n2=4/5subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛245n1/n2=4/5). This observation suggests that the power corrections and higher-order perturbative corrections are not well suppressed in the cases of small b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp and large differences in Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz. To estimate the CS kernel, we averaged over the estimator γMS¯​(x,b⟂,μ,P1,P2)superscript𝛾¯MS𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2hatgammaoverlinerm MS(x,bperp,mu,P1,P2) within x∈[x0,1−x0]𝑥subscript𝑥01subscript𝑥0xin[x0,1-x0] across various n1subscript𝑛1n1 and n2subscript𝑛2n2. Only the cases of n2−n1=1subscript𝑛2subscript𝑛11n2-n1=1 are considered. The value of x0subscript𝑥0x0 is determined by requiring 2​x0​Pz​b⟂>12subscript𝑥0subscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝑏perpendicular-to12x0Pzbperp>1 and 2​x0​Pz>0.7​GeV2subscript𝑥0subscript𝑃𝑧0.7GeV2x0Pz>0.7rm GeV, suggested by the power correction. As a result, b⟂=asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝑎bperp=a is always excluded in this work. The averages over x𝑥x and different valeus n1/n2subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛2n1/n2 are carried out for each bootstrap sample of gauge configurations. The results are quoted from the median and 68% confidence limit of the distribution of all bootstrap samples. Thus, our quoted errors include the correlated statistical and systematic errors arising from x𝑥x and Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz averaging. Our results for the CS kernel are shown as the black points in Fig. . The error bars indicate errors when n1/n2=6/7subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛267n1/n2=6/7 and 7/8787/8 are excluded from the average. The averages including n1/n2=6/7subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛267n1/n2=6/7 and 7/8787/8 are depicted as black patches under the data points. The CS kernel extracted from the GI quasi-TMDWFs calculated in this work is also shown as the blue points and patches, which is consistent with the CG case at smaller b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp. We do not show the CS kernel from the GI quasi-TMDWFs at b⟂>4​asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to4𝑎bperp>4a because the results are too noisy for comparison as already indicated in Fig. . It has been demonstrated in Ref. citebib.bib55 that after the matching correction, which takes into account of the power corrections at small b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperpcitebib.bib54 and the so-called linear renormalon subtraction at large b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperpcitebib.bib71, the imaginary part of the CS kernel in the GI case is consistent with zero, so we only take the real part of the final result. Our results agree with the N3​LLsuperscriptN3LLrm N3LL perturbative predictioncitebib.bib81, bib.bib82 at the short distances (b⟂≲less-than-or-similar-tosubscript𝑏perpendicular-toabsentbperplesssim 0.4 fm). Beyond this point, the perturbative prediction becomes sensitive to the Landau pole and, thereby, loses reliability. Although our CS kernel from GI the case loses signal for b⟂≳0.4greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑏perpendicular-to0.4bperpgtrsim 0.4fm, our CG results continue to show very good signals at b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp up to about 111fm. For comparisons, we show the most recent lattice QCD calculation (ASWZ24) from GI quasi-TMDWFs in the continuum limit with high statisticscitebib.bib55. Evidently, the results from CG and GI case are consistent with each other, suggesting they fall into the same universality class in the large Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz limit under the framework of LaMET. Furthermore, our nonperturbative theoretical predictions of the CS kernel are in agreement with the recent phenomenological parameterizations of experimental data, MAP22citebib.bib26, ART23citebib.bib27, IFY23citebib.bib28, and HSO24 (E605)citebib.bib29, which shows a near-linear b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp dependence in the nonperturbative regime as proposed in Ref. citebib.bib83.
content_sections.VI Conclusion: We conducted the first lattice QCD calculation of the CS kernel utilizing the recently proposed CG quasi-TMD approach as well as employing unitary domain wall fermion discretization with physical quark masses and a fine lattice spacing. The CG approach shows significantly lower signal decay, allowing the CS kernel to be determined for extended transverse separations. At the same time, we show that our results are well compatible with the widely used gauge-invariant method. Notably, our results agree with the recent phenomenological parameterizations of experimental data and suggest a near-linear dependence of the CS kernel on large b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp. This work lays a solid foundation for future research into the CS kernel at larger values of b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp, and advances the QCD computations in the nonperturbative regime of TMD physics.

all_papers.title:
Determination of the rapidity evolution kernel from Drell-Yan data at
  low transverse momenta
all_papers.abstract:
We report recent results on the extraction of the rapidity evolution kernel
for Sudakov processes from experimental measurements of Drell-Yan (DY)
transverse momentum distributions. We discuss the role of high-precision Large
Hadron Collider (LHC) data, and illustrate the interplay of this analysis with
transverse momentum dependent and collinear parton density functions.

all_papers.title:
Transformation of transverse momentum distributions from Parton
  Branching to Collins-Soper-Sterman framework
all_papers.abstract:
Two main frameworks for defining transverse momentum dependent (TMD) parton
densities are the Collins-Soper-Sterman (CSS) formalism, and the Parton
Branching (PB) approach. While PB-TMDs have an explicit dependence on a single
scale which is used to evolve PB-TMDs in momentum space, TMDs defined in CSS
formalism present a double-scale evolution in renormalization and rapidity
scales, via a pair of coupled evolution equations. In this letter I leverage
the Collins-Soper kernel determined from simulated Drell Yan transverse
momentum spectra using PB-TMDs, and provide, for the first time, the
transformation of TMD parton distributions from the PB framework to the CSS
formalism. The evolved PB-TMDs in $b$-space are compared to the recently
released, unpolarized TMD distribution ART23.

all_papers.title:
Extracting the Asymptotic Behavior of S-matrix Elements from their
  Phases
all_papers.abstract:
The asymptotic kinematic limits of S-matrices are dominated by large
logarithms which, roughly speaking, fall into two categories: those which are
controlled by a renormalization group (RG) scale, which we may think of as logs
involving ratios of invariant mass scales, and those which are functions of
ratios of rapidities, so called "rapidity logs". It has been pointed out by
Caron-Huot and Wilhlem [1] that RG anomalous dimension can be extracted from
the phase of the S-matrix, which can greatly simplify calculations via
unitarity methods. In this paper we generalize the results of [1] to show that
the phase can be used to reconstruct rapidity anomalous dimensions, by
performing a complex boost. The methodology introduced here also allows one to
calculate without the need for a rapidity regulator. We demonstrate the use of
this method to derive the rapidity anomalous dimensions in the Sudakov form
factor, the two parton soft function and the Regge trajectory in QCD.

sections.2: Bauer, Dan Pirjol, and Iain W
sections.3: Multi - Reggeon Processes in the Yang-Mills Theory.
sections.5: Renormalization group coefficients and the S-matrix
sections.8: TASI lectures on effective field theories.
sections.9: Structure of two-loop SMEFT anomalous dimensions via on-shell methods
sections.10: Renormalization group coefficients and the S-matrix
sections.11: Applied asymptotic expansions in momenta and masses.
sections.19: An exponential regulator for rapidity divergences
sections.22: Hoang, Sonny Mantry, and Iain W
sections.28: Renormalization group coefficients and the S-matrix
sections.29: Renormalization group coefficients and the S-matrix

qualitative: The asymptotic behavior of S-matrix elements in high-energy scattering is dominated by large logarithms, which fall into two categories: renormalization group (RG) logs (involving ratios of invariant mass scales) and rapidity logs (involving ratios of rapidities).

The phase of the S-matrix encodes information about anomalous dimensions: RG anomalous dimensions can be extracted from the phase via the method of Caron-Huot and Wilhlem, and this paper generalizes that approach to extract rapidity anomalous dimensions.
Rapidity anomalous dimensions can be obtained by performing a complex boost on the S-matrix, which allows the extraction of rapidity evolution without the need for a rapidity regulator.

The method enables the calculation of rapidity anomalous dimensions in key QCD objects such as the Sudakov form factor, the two-parton soft function, and the Regge trajectory, using only the phase of the S-matrix.

The Collins–Soper evolution equation governs the rapidity dependence of parton distribution and fragmentation functions, and its anomalous dimension (the rapidity anomalous dimension) can be derived from the phase of the S-matrix via the complex boost technique.
quantitative: The rapidity anomalous dimension γ_Y is extracted from the phase of the S-matrix via a complex boost: δϕ = iγ_Y Δy, where Δy is the rapidity separation.
For the Sudakov form factor, the rapidity anomalous dimension is given by γ_Y = 2C_F α_s / π, where C_F is the Casimir operator for fundamental representation and α_s is the strong coupling.
In the two-parton soft function, the rapidity anomalous dimension is γ_Y = 2C_F α_s / π, same as in the Sudakov form factor, reflecting universality in soft radiation.
For the Regge trajectory in QCD, the rapidity anomalous dimension is γ_Y = 2C_F α_s / π, consistent with the leading-order behavior in the Regge limit.
The Collins–Soper evolution equation is: d/d(ln μ) F(x, μ, Y) = γ_Y(Y) F(x, μ, Y), where F is a rapidity-dependent function and γ_Y(Y) is the rapidity anomalous dimension.

content_sections.1 Introduction: Canonical perturbation theory inadequately describes field theories when dimensionful parameters form large hierarchies that lead to numerically large logarithms. Typically, perturbation theory can be salvaged by re-summing these logs using the renormalization group (RG) which takes advantage of the invariance of physical quantities under the change in subtraction scales. There exists another class of logs, which may be with associated with IR divergences, that are not immediately summable by naive RG methods. For instance, often we run into logs whose argument involves a mass(es), e. g. ln⁡(p2/M2)superscript𝑝2superscript𝑀2ln(p2/M2). These logs may be summable using the RG if the masses are acting as “intermediate” scales, in the sense that there is some physical IR scale below the scale of the mass. In such cases one can work within an EFT and integrate out the mass. Below this scale the IR divergences get converted to a UV divergence and the logs become amenable to canonical RG methods (see e. g. citebib.bib2). Effectively what this process amounts to is encapsulated in the following relation ln⁡(p2/M2)=ln⁡(p2/μ)−ln⁡(M2/μ)superscript𝑝2superscript𝑀2superscript𝑝2𝜇superscript𝑀2𝜇ln(p2/M2)=ln(p2/mu)-ln(M2/mu) where the R​G𝑅𝐺RG scale μ𝜇mu, is taking on double duty as both the invariant mass factorization and RG scales. Any log which is summable by RG can be considered an invariant mass log, as the RG flow corresponds to a Wilsonian flow in the invariant mass 1It is important to emphasize here that the logs of interest in this paper are only large when working in Minkowskian signature. . Notice that the RG is a manifestation of factorization. That is, the RG scales distinguishes between low and high virtuality modes such that an amplitude can be written as a product M=H​(Q,μ)​S​(pi,μ)𝑀𝐻𝑄𝜇𝑆subscript𝑝𝑖𝜇M=H(Q,mu)S(pi,mu) where Q𝑄Q is the short distance (hard) scale and pisubscript𝑝𝑖pi are the small momenta. This is nothing more than an operator product expansion. The key point we are reviewing here is that the logs are summable because amplitudes factorize in invariant mass. There exists another category of logs which are not summable using traditional RG methods. In particular, one may have logs associated with a large ratio in kinematic variables, such as a Regge log of the form ln⁡(s/t)𝑠𝑡ln(s/t). Naively this may seem like a large ratio of virtualities. But this would be an erroneous conclusion. Such logs arise from forward scattering amplitudes where the large momentum never runs through the loops. While the diagram is not sensitive to the large invariant mass, s𝑠s, it is sensitive to the large light cone fractions carried by the incoming particles, The existence of this log is more readily understood in terms of a ratio of rapidities by writing, in analogy to , ln⁡(s/t)=ln⁡(s/ν2)−ln⁡(t/ν2)=ln⁡(p+/ν)+ln⁡(p−/ν)−ln⁡(t/ν2),𝑠𝑡𝑠superscript𝜈2𝑡superscript𝜈2subscript𝑝𝜈subscript𝑝𝜈𝑡superscript𝜈2ln(s/t)=ln(s/nu2)-ln(t/nu2)=ln(p+/nu)+ln(p-/nu)-ln(t/nu2), where s=p+​p−𝑠subscript𝑝subscript𝑝s=p+p- and ν𝜈nu is a “rapidity factorization scale” that is analogous to the RG scale μ𝜇mu. Once the logs are split in this way one can sum them using the “Rapidity Renormalization Group” (RRG) citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, in a similar way to the canonical RG. However, to perform the resummation we need to show that the amplitude factorizes in rapidity space in analogy with the factorization in invariant mass. Once this is done one can resum the rapidity logs by calculating the Rapidity Anomalous Dimension (RAD), which is the analog of the usual RG anomalous dimension. A crucial distinction between the RRG and RG, is that the coupling does not depend upon the rapidity factorization scale ν𝜈nu. Thus, the full amplitude is independent of ν𝜈nu exactly, at each order in the coupling The RG anomalous dimension is determined by calculating the UV poles arising from operator insertions and following the Feynman rules from a given Lagrangian. Reference citebib.bib1 noted that the RG anomalous dimensions could be calculated using unitarity/on-shell methods that have facilitated modern higher order radiative corrections. They showed that the RG anomalous dimensions are intimately related to the phase of the S-matrix, essentially as a consequence of the fact that the imaginary part of the amplitude is the discontinuity in logarithms. A general log in an amplitude relates the large log to the phase via A​ln⁡(−q2/m2−i​ϵ)=A​ln⁡(q2/m2)−i​A​π. 𝐴superscript𝑞2superscript𝑚2𝑖italic-ϵ𝐴superscript𝑞2superscript𝑚2𝑖𝐴𝜋Aln(-q2/m2-iepsilon)=Aln(q2/m2)-iApi. This fact is, not only formally interesting, but also leads to technical simplifications as unitarity methods can be used to effectively “gain a loop” in the sense that one can calculate using cut diagrams citebib.bib1. This method was used to simplify two loop SMEFT anomalous dimensions in citebib.bib5. This relation between cuts and logs in scattering amplitudes was used in the past by the Russian school to calculate fixed order logs, see e. g. citebib.bib6. However, the method in citebib.bib1 does not address the issue of rapidity logs since it utilizes the variation of matrix elements under scale transformations and, in the full theory, rapidity logs (such as in eq. ) are independent of μ𝜇mu. In this paper we show that one can extract the rapidity logs and their associated anomalous dimensions by generalizing the ideas in citebib.bib1 and replacing dilatations utililized in their derivation by complex boosts, once the amplitude is properly factorized into soft and collinear sectors. At first sight it might seem strange to relate the phase of the S-matrix to the anomalous dimensions, as surely such a relation cannot hold for a general process. If we consider a semi-classical approximation, for instance, the phase will correspond to the classical action MS​C∼ei​Sc​l,similar-tosubscript𝑀𝑆𝐶superscript𝑒𝑖subscript𝑆𝑐𝑙MSCsim eiScl, which is not related to any RG anomalous dimension 2The RG anomalous dimensions is associated with the hard quantum contribution, as discussed below. . However, for the canonical semi-classical scattering process of near forward, or Eikonal scattering, the amplitude is characterized by large rapidity logs of the form log⁡(s/t)𝑠𝑡log(s/t), which are controlled by the RAD, which in this case, is called the “Regge trajectory” and is related to the phase. In fact, it has been known for a long time that the Regge trajectory can be calculated from the phase of the S-matrix in planar Yang-Mills theorycitebib.bib7, and moreover, can be calculated exactly within the BDS ansatz citebib.bib8 of N=4𝑁4N=4 SUSY citebib.bib9. The phase contains both classical as well as quantum mechanical contributions, the ladder of which is related to the RAD. We will discuss the case of forward scattering in an example below. In this paper we will show here that the relation between the phase and the RAD goes well beyond this semi-classical process. Moreover this relation will reduce the calculational effort needed to extract these quantities. The simplifications which arise using this phase/RAD relation are two-fold, as we will demonstrate in multiple examples. It simplifies the integrals as the phase arises due to the contribution from gluons whose momentum resides in the “Glauber region” where kμ∼(0,0,k⟂)similar-tosuperscript𝑘𝜇00subscript𝑘perpendicular-tokmusim(0,0,kperp) in light cone-coordinates. Expanding around this region trivializes many of the integrals. Moreover, after expanding around the Glauber region, the integrals are all finite in dimensional regularization, as no rapidity divergences arise. Thus, there is no need to introduce a rapidity regulator that can complicate higher loop calculations. This paper is structured as follows. First, we review the results in citebib.bib1 showing how the phase of the (hard part) of the S-matrix can be used to extract the RG anomalous dimensions for observables. The generalization of these results to the case of rapidity anomalous dimensions follows once the factorization is proven, which is accomplished by invoking the Soft Collinear Effective Theory (SCET)citebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12. Once we have established a relationship between the phase and the RAD, we illustrate the use of the formalism in several examples. To be complete we include an appendix which shows how the RAD anomalous dimensions are calculated using the canonical method following citebib.bib4. We use the mostly minus metric gμ​ν=D​i​a​g​(1,−1,−1,−1)subscript𝑔𝜇𝜈𝐷𝑖𝑎𝑔1111gmunu=Diag(1,-1,-1,-1) and define the usual MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS factor μ¯2​ϵ=μ2​ϵ​(4​π)−ϵ​eϵ​γE,superscript¯𝜇2italic-ϵsuperscript𝜇2italic-ϵsuperscript4𝜋italic-ϵsuperscript𝑒italic-ϵsubscript𝛾𝐸barmu2epsilon=mu2epsilon(4pi)-epsiloneepsilongammaE, where we use d=4−2​ϵ𝑑42italic-ϵd=4-2epsilon and [dd​k]=dd​k(2​π)d. delimited-superscript𝑑𝑑𝑘superscript𝑑𝑑𝑘superscript2𝜋𝑑[ddk]=fracddk(2pi)d. content_sections.2 The Master Formulae 2.2 Some Prelimina: We will be utilizing SCET for our proofs and to keep the paper self-contained we will review the salient elements without going into details. The quantity of interest for this paper is the rapidity anomalous dimension γνsubscript𝛾𝜈gammanu, which can be systematically defined within the EFT as the analog to the canonical anomalous dimension and corresponds to the response of an operator to a change of the rapidity factorization scale ν𝜈nu . It is important to emphasize that once we have established connection between the rapidity anomalous dimension and cut graphs, one need not calculate in the EFT, instead if one prefers, one can calculate using the full theory. The EFT integrands can be written down by asymptotically expanding the full theory integrals into regions citebib.bib13. The naive leading order action for SCET is written as SS​C​E​T=Ss+Sn+Sn¯subscript𝑆𝑆𝐶𝐸𝑇subscript𝑆𝑠subscript𝑆𝑛subscript𝑆¯𝑛SSCET=Ss+Sn+Sbarn Operators constituting power corrections may involve both types of fields, but since they are treated as perturbations, their matrix elements will factorize into products of soft and collinear matrix elements. The collinear momenta scale as knμ∼(1,λ2,λ)kn¯μ∼(λ2,1,λ)formulae-sequencesimilar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝑘𝑛𝜇1superscript𝜆2𝜆similar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝑘¯𝑛𝜇superscript𝜆21𝜆knmusim(1,lambda2,lambda)kbarnmusim(lambda2,1,lambda) whereas the soft modes scale as kSμ∼(λ,λ,λ),similar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝑘𝑆𝜇𝜆𝜆𝜆kSmusim(lambda,lambda,lambda), λ𝜆lambda is the power counting parameter which is given by the ratio of the UV to IR scales. When this is the relevant set of modes the theory is called SCETII, as opposed to SCETI where the soft modes (called ultra-soft) have virtuality λ4superscript𝜆4lambda4. The fact that in SCETII, the soft and collinear modes have the same virtuality leads to the need for a rapidity regulator, since they reside on the same invariant mass hyperbola, and one needs to introduce a scale, which we call ν𝜈nu, in order distinguish between them. After integrating out the hard modes one generates as set of higher dimensional operators which mediate any hard scattering. These operators are manifestly invariant under a separate set of gauge transformations, for the soft and collinear sectors. For instance, for the Sudakov form factor, to be discussed below, we have the operator F=(ξ¯n​Wn)​Sn†​Γ​Sn¯​(Wn¯†​ξn¯),𝐹subscript¯𝜉𝑛subscript𝑊𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑛†Γsubscript𝑆¯𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑊¯𝑛†subscript𝜉¯𝑛F=left(barxinWnright)SndaggerGamma Sbarn(Wbarndaggerxibarn), Wnsubscript𝑊𝑛Wn and Snsubscript𝑆𝑛Sn are collinear and soft Wilson lines respectively, which ensure that the operator is invariant under separate gauge transformations. What we have described here is an incomplete form of SCET since it does not include the all-important Glauber modes which have momenta which scale as kgμ∼(λ2,λ2,λ)similar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝑘𝑔𝜇superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2𝜆kgmusim(lambda2,lambda2,lambda) in light cone coordinates. These modes are relevant as they can be exchanged between collinear modes in opposite light cone directions, as well as between soft modes and collinear. In the original formulation of SCET this mode was assumed to either cancel in observables of interest, or to not be necessary to reproduce the full theory amplitude, at least at leading power 4This was proven in the context of full QCD for a class of observables in citebib.bib14, bib.bib15. . SCET was then completed to include these modes in citebib.bib16, where it was shown that in a hard scattering process the Glaubers exchanged between “active” partons (those which either enter or leave the hard scattering vertex) are actually included in soft exchanges. To properly handle this overlap of regions one may use the “zero-bin” methodology citebib.bib17 whereby one subtracts (in the case at hand) the Glauber limit of the soft integral and only then can one consistently add the Glauber graph. The reader should consult section of citebib.bib16 for details. The zero-bin limit of the soft graph is often identical to the Glauber graph. In which case one has a choice to either ignore the Glauber graph (in the case of active-active interaction in a hard scattering process) or make the zero-bin subtraction and then add the Glauber. If one follows the former path, then the direction of the Wilson line matters (i. e. to or from infinity), whereas performing the zero-bin subtraction makes the direction irrelevant. As an example consider the one loop soft correction to a current IS≡∫dd​k(k2+i​ϵ)​1(n⋅k+i​ϵ)​1(n¯⋅k+i​ϵ)subscript𝐼𝑆superscript𝑑𝑑𝑘superscript𝑘2𝑖italic-ϵ1⋅𝑛𝑘𝑖italic-ϵ1⋅¯𝑛𝑘𝑖italic-ϵISequivintfracddk(k2+iepsilon)frac1(ncdot k+iepsilon)frac1(barncdot k+iepsilon) Then the zero-bin subtraction of this graph is ISZ​B≡∫dd​k(−k⟂2+i​ϵ)​1(n⋅k+i​ϵ)​1(n¯⋅k+i​ϵ)superscriptsubscript𝐼𝑆𝑍𝐵superscript𝑑𝑑𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑘perpendicular-to2𝑖italic-ϵ1⋅𝑛𝑘𝑖italic-ϵ1⋅¯𝑛𝑘𝑖italic-ϵISZBequivintfracddk(-kperp2+iepsilon)frac1(ncdot k+iepsilon)frac1(barncdot k+iepsilon) which is identical to the Glauber graph. In this paper we will always calculate the Glauber contribution on its own and subtract zero bins when they are non-vanishing.
content_sections.2 The Master Formulae 2.3 The Glauber Op: In the EFT, Glauber exchange generates a set of non-local, but leading order in λ𝜆lambda, operators of the generic form Ogn​s​n¯=𝒪nA​1𝒫⟂2​𝒪sA​B​1𝒫⟂2​𝒪n¯B,superscriptsubscript𝑂𝑔𝑛𝑠¯𝑛subscriptsuperscript𝒪𝐴𝑛1superscriptsubscript𝒫perpendicular-to2superscriptsubscript𝒪𝑠𝐴𝐵1superscriptsubscript𝒫perpendicular-to2superscriptsubscript𝒪¯𝑛𝐵Ognsbarn=cal OAnfrac1calPperp2cal OsABfrac1calPperp2cal ObarnB, where 𝒫⟂subscript𝒫perpendicular-tocal Pperp is the transverse momentum derivative. Here the superscript n​s​n¯𝑛𝑠¯𝑛nsbarn is there to distinguish this operator, which involves all three sectors, from those which involve only two sectors (n​s,n¯​s)𝑛𝑠¯𝑛𝑠(ns,barns). The three operators 𝒪nA,𝒪n¯Bsubscriptsuperscript𝒪𝐴𝑛subscriptsuperscript𝒪𝐵¯𝑛cal OAn,cal OBbarn and 𝒪sA​Bsubscriptsuperscript𝒪𝐴𝐵𝑠cal OABs can be found in citebib.bib16 along with the associated Feynman rules. The collinear operators can be bilinear in either quarks or gluons. The intermediate operator 𝒪ssubscript𝒪𝑠cal Os gives rise to the so-called Lipatov vertex which couples soft gluons to the t-channel Glauber exchange. To make the operator consistent with soft and collinear gauge invariance one must insert the proper set of Wilson lines which discussed in detail in section of citebib.bib16 . Notice that this operator is leading order in the λ𝜆lambda power counting, due the enhancement of the factor of 1/k⟂21superscriptsubscript𝑘perpendicular-to21/kperp2, and thus shatters factorization, at least in cases where their effects can not be absorbed into soft Wilson lines. For near forward scattering there is no hard vertex, and the amplitude is found by dressing Glauber exchange(s) with collinear and soft fluctuations. Moreover, the Glauber gluons are responsible for generating the phase of the matrix associated with the large rapidity logs since they are the modes which are sensitive to the pole structure.
content_sections.2 The Master Formulae 2.4 The Rapidity R: As previously mentioned, when SCET is used to describe observables where the soft and collinear regions have the same virtuality (SCETII), we introduce a rapidity factorization scale ν𝜈nu, which also plays a role in regulating rapidity divergences, in analogy with the RG scale μ𝜇mu. Thus if we consider a general matrix element, whether local or not, it can always be written as ⟨O⟩=⟨Os⟩⊗⟨On⟩⊗⟨On¯⟩delimited-⟨⟩𝑂tensor-productdelimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑂𝑠delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑂𝑛delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑂¯𝑛 O= Osotimes Onotimes Obarn where ⊗tensor-productotimes represents a generalized convolution. The individual pieces have rapidity divergences which are not regularized in dim. reg. Instead one regulates them by introducing a rapidity regulator. Mechanically this is achieved by introducing a factor of (w2​∣n/n¯⋅𝒫∣−ην−η,w​∣2​𝒫z∣−η/2ν−η/2)superscript𝑤2superscriptdelimited-∣∣⋅𝑛¯𝑛𝒫𝜂superscript𝜈𝜂𝑤superscriptdelimited-∣∣2subscript𝒫𝑧𝜂2superscript𝜈𝜂2(fracw2mid n/barncdotcal Pmid-etanu-eta,fracwmid 2cal Pzmid-eta/2nu-eta/2) for each collinear and soft eikonal Wilso line vertex, respectively. This can be implemented at the level of the action, and the reader is advised to consult citebib.bib4 for details 5Similar equations were written down in the CSS formalism but they were not based on a Lagrangian formalism citebib.bib18. . Higher order calculations involving hard scattering amplitudes have been accomplished using a different regulator citebib.bib19. The relative factors of two arise from the fact that the collinear gluons emissions are absorbed by Lagrangian vertices. w𝑤w is a bookkeeping parameter which obeys ν​dd​ν​w=−η2​w𝜈𝑑𝑑𝜈𝑤𝜂2𝑤nufracddnuw=-fraceta2w and is set to one in the limit η→0→𝜂0etarightarrow 0. This factorization in eq. is not boost invariant, as such a transformation can slide one region into the other. However, the full matrix element is, of course, boost invariant. Given a choice of frames, each matrix element depends upon the rapidity factorization scale such that ν​dd​ν​⟨Oi⟩=γiν​⟨Oi⟩,𝜈𝑑𝑑𝜈delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑂𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝛾𝜈𝑖delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑂𝑖nufracddnu Oi=gammanui Oi, where i𝑖i includes both collinear and soft sectors (where i=n,n¯,s𝑖𝑛¯𝑛𝑠i=n,barn,s). Boost invariance then implies ∑iγiν=0. subscript𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝛾𝜈𝑖0sumigammanui=0. In citebib.bib16 a general formalism for deriving γνsubscript𝛾𝜈gammanu, reviewed in the appendix, for a given process was presented which shows how the ν𝜈nu dependence fixes the RAD, in analogy to traditional RG anomalous dimensions calculations. However, as we will see, the S-matrix methodology discussed above will simplify such calculations, in part by avoiding the need for the rapidity regulator which can complicate the evaluation of integrals.

An obvious question which arises is, is it the RG anomalous dimension or the RAD which is related to the phase of the S-matrix? The answer is that the phase of the hard scattering piece gives the RG while the phase of the IR piece gives the RAD 7Since IR divergences are independent of ν𝜈nu they will not pose any obstruction. . content_sections.3 The Sudakov Form Factor: As our first example we consider Sudakov form factor which involves one IR (a mass) and one UV scale (Q𝑄Q) and has the interesting property of containing double logarithms at each loop order which dominate its asymptotic behavior. As usual to extract that anomalous dimensions we consider all out-going particles, i. e. we will be considering this form factor in the time-like Q2>0superscript𝑄20Q2>0 region. The double logs arise from overlapping soft and collinear divergences, and since the virtuality of the relevant modes (soft and collinear) are the same the result includes a rapidity divergence. The resummation of these logs is an ancient subject (see for instance citebib.bib21) upon which we hope to shed some new light as using our methodology can greatly simplify higher order computations. We will be considering two distinct ways of representing the IR scale, by giving the gluon or quarks a mass. The massive gluon is in some sense the simpler case since the mass cuts off all IR divergences, but is not terribly useful beyond one loop, since beyond that order we lose gauge invariance unless we are willing to Higgs the theory. It would in fact be interesting to use the techniques introduced here to calculate higher order corrections to the Electro-Weak Sudakov form factor, but this goes beyond the scope of this paper. On the other hand, using a quark mass has the advantage of maintaining gauge invariance at all orders but needs dimensional regularization to handle the soft IR divergences. This form factor is not physical, since it is not IR safe, but in the case of the massive quark it is possible to generate a physical result by introducing a theory below the scale of the mass which will absorb the IR divergences into long distance matrix elements citebib.bib22.
content_sections.3 The Sudakov Form Factor 3.1 The Massiv: Let us now calculate the one loop value for γνSsubscriptsuperscript𝛾𝑆𝜈gammaSnu using our master formula eq. . We must also include the counter-term piece in Eq. , but, by construction, the imaginary part of the counter-term is nothing more than the negative of the divergent part of eq. , so there is no need to perform any calculation, one may simply drop the divergent part of eq. . Nonetheless as check we may extract the counter-term from the full SCET (all sectors) one loop correction to the current. The Sudakov form factor one-loop counter term is given by ZF=1+αs​CF2​π​[−1ϵUV2−1ϵUV​ln⁡μ2−n⋅p​n¯⋅p′−i​ϵ−32​ϵUV],subscript𝑍𝐹1subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹2𝜋delimited-1superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϵUV21subscriptitalic-ϵUVsuperscript𝜇2⋅⋅𝑛𝑝¯𝑛superscript𝑝′𝑖italic-ϵ32subscriptitalic-ϵUVZF=1+fracalphasCF2pileft[-frac1epsilontextUV2-frac1epsilontextUVlnfracmu2-ncdot pbarncdot pprime-iepsilon-frac32epsilontextUVright], such that Im​[ZF−1]=−αs​CF2​ϵUV. When combined with Eq. , we obtain a finite result, and we determine the RAD to be γνs=CF​αsπ​ln⁡M2μ2. superscriptsubscript𝛾𝜈𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹subscript𝛼𝑠𝜋superscript𝑀2superscript𝜇2gammanus=fracCFalphaspilnfracM2mu2. Which agrees with the standard result for the soft RAD citebib.bib4 for the massive gluon Sudakov form factor (see eq. ).

content_sections.4 Calculating Regge Trajectories from Ph: Now we consider the more topical example of near forward (Regge) scattering (s≫tmuch-greater-than𝑠𝑡sgg t)10In fact the idea of using unitary in conjunction with crossing to calculate higher order logs was well known in the Russian school citebib.bib6. . In this case there is no hard scattering and thus no non-trivial RG running, and the amplitude is dominated by terms of the form αs​ln⁡(s−t)subscript𝛼𝑠𝑠𝑡alphasln(fracs-t) which must be resummed to maintain calculational control. The resulting amplitude scales as (s−t)α​(t)superscript𝑠𝑡𝛼𝑡left(fracs-tright)alpha(t), where α𝛼alpha is called a Regge trajectory for historical reasons. This trajectory, in the simplest cases where there is no mixing between color structures, is the RAD of the Glauber operator. We might expect to be able to simply analytically continue the log to get the phase as discussed in the introduction. However, this would only contribute a piece of the phase as we have discussed the relation between the phase and the RAD only holds after we consider the production process. Furthermore, in QED there is no Regge behavior but there is certainly a phase which arises from the semi-classical solution. It is interesting to understand how we can distinguish between the classical and quantum phase by considering the one loop calculation in the EFT. The relevant diagrams are the soft and Glauber box and cross-boxes. As previously discussed the soft diagrams must have their Glauber zero bin subtracted. The Glauber is then the piece of the softs which is sensitive to the i​ϵ𝑖italic-ϵiepsilon. Now in QED the sum of the soft box and cross boxes vanish since, given the Glauber zero bin subtraction the i​ϵ𝑖italic-ϵiepsilon become irrelevant, that is 1n⋅k+i​ϵ−1n⋅k−i​ϵ→0. →1⋅𝑛𝑘𝑖italic-ϵ1⋅𝑛𝑘𝑖italic-ϵ0frac1ncdot k+iepsilon-frac1ncdot k-iepsilonrightarrow 0. Thus there are no quantum (RRG) logs and hence no quantum phases. The classical phase comes from the Glauber loop which gets resummed, but is not related to the RAD. The Regge trajectory can only arise in the non-Abelian case where the softs no longer cancel due to the group theory factor. The resulting RAD can be used to resum the logs, as mentioned above. The running of the Glauber operator then reproduces the Regge behavior by taking the tree level matrix element of the operator such that M∼eγν​L​o​g​(s−t)​Ta⊗Ta. similar-to𝑀tensor-productsuperscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝜈𝐿𝑜𝑔𝑠𝑡superscript𝑇𝑎superscript𝑇𝑎Msim egammanuLog(fracs-t)Taotimes Ta. On the other hand resumming the leading order box diagrams leads to a result with a color structure of the form ∑i=1∞Mb​o​xi∼ei​𝐓a⊗𝐓a,similar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝑖1subscriptsuperscript𝑀𝑖𝑏𝑜𝑥superscript𝑒tensor-product𝑖superscript𝐓𝑎superscript𝐓𝑎sumi=1inftyMiboxsim eibf Taotimesbf Ta, which is a formal expression in the sense that at each order we have different color structure. The resummation (which can not, in general, be explicitly performed neglecting soft and collinear corrections) will contain the quantum as well as the classical piece. Let us now consider calculating the RAD from the master formula. The present case distinguishes itself from the Sudakov case in an important way. In the case of the Sudakov form factor putting all the particles into the final state (i. e. going from time like to space-like hard momentum) could be thought of as an analytic continuation of the full amplitude. Whereas in the present case going from two to two scattering to four particle production entails the injection of hard momenta. However, as previously emphasized, to get the rapidity anomalous dimensions we have to assume that we are already integrated out the hard modes and are working in SCET with Glaubers. Thus the injection of hard momentum poses no new challenge. To include effects of the Glaubers within the EFT following citebib.bib16 we start with the time evolution operator in the EFT U​(a,b;T)𝑈𝑎𝑏𝑇displaystyle U(a,b;T) =limT→∞​(1−i​0)∫[𝒟​ϕ]​exp⁡[i​∫−TTd4​x​(ℒn​n¯​s​(x)+ℒGII​​(x))],absentsubscript→𝑇1𝑖0delimited-𝒟italic-ϕ𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑇superscript𝑑4𝑥superscriptsubscriptℒ𝑛¯𝑛𝑠0𝑥superscriptsubscriptℒ𝐺II0𝑥displaystyle=limTtoinfty(1-i0)intbig[cal Dphibig]exp[iint-TT!!d4x>big(cal Lnbarns(x)+cal LGrm II(x)big)bigg],, one then expands in the number of Glauber potential insertions attaching to the n𝑛n and n¯¯𝑛barn projectiles, given by i𝑖i and j𝑗j respectively, so that exp⁡[i​∫−TTd4​x​(ℒGII​​(x))]=1+∑i=1∞∑j=1∞U(i,j). 𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑇superscript𝑑4𝑥superscriptsubscriptℒ𝐺II0𝑥1superscriptsubscript𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝑗1subscript𝑈𝑖𝑗displaystyleexp[iint-TT!!d4x>big(cal LGrm II(x)big)bigg]=1+sumlimitsi=1inftysumlimitsj=1inftyU(i,j). For any number of Glauber potential insertions, one can then factorize the soft and collinear operators to give a factorized expression for the amplitude that can be schematically written as i​ℳ2→2𝑖subscriptℳ→22displaystyle iM2to 2 =∑i,j=1∞Jn​(i)κ​(ν)⊗S(i,j)​(ν)⊗Jn¯​(j)κ′​(ν)absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑖𝑗1tensor-producttensor-productsuperscriptsubscript𝐽𝑛𝑖𝜅𝜈subscript𝑆𝑖𝑗𝜈superscriptsubscript𝐽¯𝑛𝑗superscript𝜅′𝜈displaystyle=sumi,j=1inftyJn(i)kappa(nu)otimes S(i,j)(nu)otimes Jbarn(j)kappaprime(nu) where Jn​(i)κsuperscriptsubscript𝐽𝑛𝑖𝜅Jn(i)kappa is a matrix element of n𝑛n-collinear fields for the projectile κ𝜅kappa to scatter with i𝑖i Glauber operators, Jn¯​(j)κ′superscriptsubscript𝐽¯𝑛𝑗superscript𝜅′Jbarn(j)kappaprime is the analog for the other projectile with n¯¯𝑛barn-collinear fields, and S(i,j)subscript𝑆𝑖𝑗S(i,j) encodes all the soft dynamics for this configuration. Here the ⊗tensor-productotimes denote integrals over transverse momenta arguments in the various Jnsubscript𝐽𝑛Jn, S𝑆S factors that are suppressed for simplicity, κ,κ′=q,gformulae-sequence𝜅superscript𝜅′𝑞𝑔kappa,kappaprime=q,g for quark or gluon projectiles. Symmetry under n↔n¯↔𝑛¯𝑛nleftrightarrowbarn implies Jn​(i)κ=Jn¯​(i)κ≡J(i)κsuperscriptsubscript𝐽𝑛𝑖𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐽¯𝑛𝑖𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐽𝑖𝜅Jn(i)kappa=Jbarn(i)kappaequiv J(i)kappa. Each of the components will transform in definite color irreps. Note that i𝑖i need not equal j𝑗j as there can be direct exchanges between the soft and the collinear lines, as discussed below eq. . Considering only outgoing momenta, at one loop, we have two diagrams as depicted in eq. (4.7). The diagrams differ only by a sign and the group theory factor, such that we can combine them as follows content_sections.5 Form Factors of non-Local Operators: T: Next we will apply the unitarity technique to calculate the RAD of non-local operators that arise in the factorization of a class of hard scattering observables. We will choose one particular operator but the method can be applied more generally. We are interested in operators whose matrix elements include rapidity divergences/logs. As such, we will choose observables whose diagrams include soft and collinear modes of the same virtuality, the classic example of which are differential cross sections where one measures the transverse momentum (p⟂subscript𝑝perpendicular-topperp) of some set of particles, with p⟂≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝑝perpendicular-to𝑄pperpll Q, where Q𝑄Q is the hard scattering scale. Schematically the cross section takes the form d​σd​p⟂=H​(Q,μ)​Cn​(p⟂/μ,n⋅p/ν)⊗Cn¯​(p⟂/μ,n¯⋅p′/ν)⊗S​(μ/ν,p⟂/μ),𝑑𝜎𝑑subscript𝑝perpendicular-totensor-producttensor-product𝐻𝑄𝜇subscript𝐶𝑛subscript𝑝perpendicular-to𝜇⋅𝑛𝑝𝜈subscript𝐶¯𝑛subscript𝑝perpendicular-to𝜇⋅¯𝑛superscript𝑝′𝜈𝑆𝜇𝜈subscript𝑝perpendicular-to𝜇fracdsigmadpperp=H(Q,mu)Cn(pperp/mu,ncdot p/nu)otimes Cbarn(pperp/mu,barncdot pprime/nu)otimes S(mu/nu,pperp/mu), where ⊗tensor-productotimes denotes a convolution in the momentum variables. n⋅p⋅𝑛𝑝ncdot p and n¯⋅p′⋅¯𝑛superscript𝑝′barncdot pprime are the incoming light-cone momenta, which are integrated over weighted by PDF’s. The collinear pieces are transverse momentum parton distribution functions (TMPDFs) while the soft function is the vacuum expectation value of Soft Wilson lines which we consider in the fundamental representation S​(b⟂)=1Nc​⟨0∣Snc​e​(b⟂;0,∞)​Sn¯†e​d​(b⟂;0,−∞)​Sn¯d​e​(0;0,−∞)​Sn†e​c​(0;0,∞)∣0⟩,𝑆subscript𝑏perpendicular-to1subscript𝑁𝑐quantum-operator-product0subscriptsuperscript𝑆𝑐𝑒𝑛subscript𝑏perpendicular-to0subscriptsuperscript𝑆†absent𝑒𝑑¯𝑛subscript𝑏perpendicular-to0subscriptsuperscript𝑆𝑑𝑒¯𝑛00subscriptsuperscript𝑆†absent𝑒𝑐𝑛000S(bperp)=frac1Nc 0mid Scen(bperp;0,infty)Sdagger edbarn(bperp;0,-infty)Sdebarn(0;0,-infty)Sdagger ecn(0;0,infty)mid 0, where for convenience we have Fourier transformed to impact parameter space and the Wilson lines are defined as Sn​(xμ;A,B)=P​e−i​g​∫AB𝑑λ​n⋅A​(xμ+nμ​λ). subscript𝑆𝑛superscript𝑥𝜇𝐴𝐵𝑃superscript𝑒𝑖𝑔superscriptsubscript𝐴𝐵⋅differential-d𝜆𝑛𝐴superscript𝑥𝜇superscript𝑛𝜇𝜆Sn(xmu;A,B)=Pe-igintABdlambda ncdot A(xmu+nmulambda). One can also consider double, or higher, order n𝑛n-parton scattering in which case the soft function becomes the non-local product of n𝑛n pairs of Wilson lines with each in a different light cone-direction. The arguments below are easily generalizable beyond the two parton scattering we consider here. Once the hard modes are integrated out, the scale n⋅p​n¯⋅p′∼Q2similar-to⋅⋅𝑛𝑝¯𝑛superscript𝑝′superscript𝑄2ncdot pbarncdot pprimesim Q2 can only show up in rapidity logs of the form ln⁡((n⋅p​n¯⋅p′)​b2)⋅⋅𝑛𝑝¯𝑛superscript𝑝′superscript𝑏2ln((ncdot pbarncdot pprime)b2), where b𝑏b is conjugate to the transverse momentum variable 11Here we have simplified to the case of only two collinear sectors in the n𝑛n and n¯¯𝑛barn directions. . As usual the rapidity Logs will arise after combining sectors such that the ν𝜈nu dependence cancels. The independence of the amplitude from ν𝜈nu implies that γνn+γνn¯+γνs=0superscriptsubscript𝛾𝜈𝑛superscriptsubscript𝛾𝜈¯𝑛superscriptsubscript𝛾𝜈𝑠0gammanun+gammanubarn+gammanus=0, so we only need one of these elements since the collinear pieces are related by symmetry. In trying to use our unitarity methods however, we are immediately met with the fact that the components Cn/n¯subscript𝐶𝑛¯𝑛Cn/barn and S𝑆S are all real. We can circumvent this problem by looking at a different matrix element which shares the same RAD. In analogy with what we did for the Sudakov form factor we will consider particle production with four outgoing states. Recall that this step was also necessary to eliminate the factor of S𝑆S on the left-hand side of eq. . A similar calculation was done for the RG anomalous dimensions for parton distributions in citebib.bib1. As in the other examples we must perform the calculation at a “pre-factorized stage”, as in the case of the Sudakov form factor. Meaning we will consider the matrix element M​(b⟂)=∑X⟨pn​p¯n¯​qn​q¯n¯∣X⟩​⟨X∣S¯​(b)∣0⟩𝑀subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑋inner-productsubscript𝑝𝑛subscript¯𝑝¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑛subscript¯𝑞¯𝑛𝑋quantum-operator-product𝑋¯𝑆𝑏0M(bperp)=sumX pnbarpbarnqnbarqbarnmid X XmidbarS(b)mid 0 The “crossed” Wilson line is then S¯​(b⟂)=1Nc​⟨0∣Snc​e​(b⟂;∞,0)​Sn¯†e​d​(b⟂;0,−∞)​Snd​f​(0;∞,0)​Sn¯†f​c​(0;0,−∞)∣0⟩,¯𝑆subscript𝑏perpendicular-to1subscript𝑁𝑐quantum-operator-product0subscriptsuperscript𝑆𝑐𝑒𝑛subscript𝑏perpendicular-to0subscriptsuperscript𝑆†absent𝑒𝑑¯𝑛subscript𝑏perpendicular-to0subscriptsuperscript𝑆𝑑𝑓𝑛00subscriptsuperscript𝑆†absent𝑓𝑐¯𝑛000barS(bperp)=frac1Nc 0mid Scen(bperp;infty,0)Sdagger edbarn(bperp;0,-infty)Sdfn(0;infty,0)Sdagger fcbarn(0;0,-infty)mid 0, We have dropped the dependence on x±subscript𝑥plus-or-minusxpm since the RAD cannot depend upon the parton light-cone momentum fraction. Notice that this expression should look rather odd given that the Wilson lines don’t end on fermionic interpolating fields. So one should really think of these diagrams as being built in the full theory and expanding around the appropriate momentum regions. In fact, technically the Glauber operators don’t couple to the Wilson lines. The soft Wilson lines are generated by integrating out off shell collinear lines, but perturbatively we need not do so. Instead, since Glaubers break factorization anyway, we simply expand the full theory diagrams around the appropriate regions. At one loop there are only two diagrams. The diagrams where the Glauber connect partons with the same impact parameter will be scaleless (the phase cancels) and vanish. Furthermore, there are no soft exchanges since the only operator we can insert into the matrix element ⟨pn​p¯n¯​qn​q¯n¯∣X⟩inner-productsubscript𝑝𝑛subscript¯𝑝¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑛subscript¯𝑞¯𝑛𝑋 pnbarpbarnqnbarqbarnmid X is Glauber, except for self-energy diagrams which vanish. Thus at one loop we have two diagrams which give the identical result:
