all_papers.title:
Joint Track Functions: Expanding the Space of Calculable Correlations at
  Colliders
all_papers.abstract:
The theoretical description of observables at collider experiments relies on
factorization theorems separating perturbative dynamics from universal
non-perturbative matrix elements. Despite significant recent progress in
extending these factorization theorems to increasingly differential jet
substructure observables, the focus has been primarily on infrared safe
observables sensitive only to correlations in the energy of final state
hadrons. However, significant information about the dynamics of the underlying
collision is encoded in how energy is correlated between hadrons of different
quantum numbers. In this paper we extend the class of calculable correlations
by deriving factorization theorems for a broad class of correlations, $\langle
\mathcal{E}_{R_1}(n_1) \cdots \mathcal{E}_{R_k}(n_k) \rangle$, between the
energy flux carried by hadrons specified by quantum numbers, $R_1, \cdots,
R_k$. We show that these factorization theorems involve moments of a new class
of universal non-perturbative functions, the "joint track functions", which
extend the track function formalism to describe the fraction of energy carried
by hadrons of multiple quantum numbers arising from the fragmentation of quarks
or gluons. We study the general properties of these functions, and then apply
this to the specific case of joint track functions for positive and negative
electromagnetic charges. We extract these from parton shower Monte Carlo
programs and use them to calculate correlations in electromagnetically charged
energy flux. We additionally propose and study a C-odd
$\mathcal{E}_{\mathcal{Q}}$ detector, which results in a qualitatively distinct
scaling behavior compared to the standard energy correlators. Our formalism
significantly extends the class of observables that can be computed at hadron
colliders, with a wide range of applications from particle to nuclear physics.

all_papers.title: Analyzing N-point Energy Correlators Inside Jets with CMS Open Data
all_papers.abstract:
Jets of hadrons produced at high-energy colliders provide experimental access
to the dynamics of asymptotically free quarks and gluons and their confinement
into hadrons. In this paper, we show that the high energies of the Large Hadron
Collider (LHC), together with the exceptional resolution of its detectors,
allow multipoint correlation functions of energy flow operators to be directly
measured within jets for the first time. Using Open Data from the CMS
experiment, we show that reformulating jet substructure in terms of these
correlators provides new ways of probing the dynamics of QCD jets, which
enables direct imaging of the confining transition to free hadrons as well as
precision measurements of the scaling properties and interactions of quarks and
gluons. This opens a new era in our understanding of jet substructure and
illustrates the immense unexploited potential of high-quality LHC data sets for
elucidating the dynamics of QCD.

all_papers.title:
Unbinned Deep Learning Jet Substructure Measurement in High $Q^2$ ep
  collisions at HERA
all_papers.abstract:
The radiation pattern within high energy quark- and gluon-initiated jets (jet
substructure) is used extensively as a precision probe of the strong force as
well as an environment for optimizing event generators with numerous
applications in high energy particle and nuclear physics. Looking at
electron-proton collisions is of particular interest as many of the
complications present at hadron colliders are absent. A detailed study of
modern jet substructure observables, jet angularities, in electron-proton
collisions is presented using data recorded using the H1 detector at HERA. The
measurement is unbinned and multi-dimensional, using machine learning to
correct for detector effects. All of the available reconstructed object
information of the respective jets is interpreted by a graph neural network,
achieving superior precision on a selected set of jet angularities. Training
these networks was enabled by the use of a large number of GPUs in the
Perlmutter supercomputer at Berkeley Lab. The particle jets are reconstructed
in the laboratory frame, using the $k_{\mathrm{T}}$ jet clustering algorithm.
Results are reported at high transverse momentum transfer $Q^2>150$ GeV${}^2$,
and inelasticity $0.2 < y < 0.7$. The analysis is also performed in sub-regions
of $Q^2$, thus probing scale dependencies of the substructure variables. The
data are compared with a variety of predictions and point towards possible
improvements of such models.

all_papers.title:
Towards an Interpretation of the First Measurements of Energy
  Correlators in the Quark-Gluon Plasma
all_papers.abstract:
Energy correlators have recently been proposed as a class of jet substructure
observables that directly link experimental measurements of the asymptotic
energy flux with the field theoretic description of the underlying microscopic
dynamics. This link holds particular promise in heavy-ion physics, where both
experimental measurements and theoretical interpretations are inherently
complex. With recent measurements of energy correlators in proton-proton
collisions, the first measurement of these observables on inclusive jets in
heavy-ion collisions underscores the importance of a theoretical understanding
of their behavior in this complex environment. In this manuscript, we extend
our previous calculations to account for several effects necessary for a
qualitative understanding of the behavior of energy correlators on inclusive
jets in heavy-ion collisions. Through a semi-analytic approach implemented in a
hydrodynamically expanding quark-gluon plasma (QGP), we account for
medium-induced radiation with leading broadening effects, selection biases
arising from energy loss, and a description of the confinement transition. Our
results represent a crucial first step towards interpreting the measurements of
energy correlators on inclusive jets in heavy-ion collisions, which marks a
significant milestone in connecting heavy-ion experiment and fundamental
quantum field theory, in the quest to disentangle the microscopic dynamics of
the QGP.

all_papers.title:
Production of three isolated photons in the parton Reggeization approach
  at high energies
all_papers.abstract:
We study a large-$p_T$ three-photon production in proton-proton collisions at
the LHC. We use the leading order (LO) approximation of the parton Reggeization
approach consistently merged with the next-to-leading order corrections
originated from the emission of additional jet. For numerical calculations we
use the parton-level generator KaTie and modified KMR-type unintegrated parton
distribution functions which satisfy exact normalization conditions for
arbitrary $x$. We compare our prediction with data from ATLAS collaboration at
the center-of-mass energy $\sqrt{s}=8$ TeV. We find that the inclusion of the
real next-to-leading-order corrections leads to a good agreement between our
predictions and data with the same accuracy as for the next-to-next-to-leading
calculations based on the collinear parton model of QCD. At higher energies
($\sqrt{s}=13$ and 27 TeV) parton Reggeization approach predicts larger cross
sections, up to $\sim 15$ \% and $\sim 30$ \%, respectively.

all_papers.title: Energy-Energy Correlators for Precision QCD
all_papers.abstract:
In this contribution to the Proceedings of the US Community Study on the
Future of Particle Physics (Snowmass 2021) we review recent progress in the
evaluation and application of the Energy-Energy Correlator (EEC) event shape
observable in $e^+e^-$ annihilation, hadronic collisions, and deep inelastic
scattering. The importance of EEC as a precision probe of the perturbative and
non perturbative aspects of QCD dynamics is emphasized. It can be used to
extract the strong coupling constant and to constrain TMD distribution
functions. Closely related energy-correlation shape variables have also been
used to tag boosted objects produced in high energy collisions. The
opportunities to study EEC at the future Electron-Ion Collider are also
highlighted.
all_papers.reason:
论文聚焦能量-能量关联（EEC）在QCD中的应用，未涉及柯林斯-索珀演化或快速度反常维数。核心主题为事件形状观测与强耦合常数提取，与查询的理论演化机制无关。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究柯林斯-索珀演化 — 不满足。证据：“Energy-Energy Correlator (EEC)” 对比 “Collins–Soper evolution”  
- C2：研究快速度 anomalous dimension — 不满足。证据：全文未提“anomalous dimension”或“rapidity evolution”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：

- 未触及快速度反常维数的计算或物理意义
authors: Duff Neill, Gherardo Vita, Ivan Vitev, Hua Xing Zhu

all_papers.title:
Not all that is $\beta_0$ is $\beta$-function: the DGLAP resummation and
  the running coupling in NLO JIMWLK
all_papers.abstract:
We reanalyze the origin of the large transverse logarithms associated with
the QCD one loop beta-function coefficient in the NLO JIMWLK Hamiltonian. We
show that some of these terms are not associated with the running of the QCD
coupling constant but rather with the DGLAP evolution. The DGLAP-like
resummation of these logarithms is mandatory within the JIMWLK Hamiltonian, as
long as the color correlation length in the projectile is larger than that in
the target. This regime in fact covers the whole range of rapidities at which
JIMWLK evolution is supposed to be applicable. We derive the RG equation that
resums these logarithms to all orders in alpha_s in the JIMWLK Hamiltonian.
This is a nonlinear equation for the eikonal scattering matrix S(x). We solve
this equation and perform the DGLAP resummation in two simple cases: the dilute
limit, where both the projectile and the target are far from saturation, and
the saturated regime, where the target correlation length also determines its
saturation momentum.
all_papers.reason:
论文聚焦于NLO JIMWLK中DGLAP类对数重求和与耦合常数运行的关系，未涉及Collins–Soper演化或快速度反常维数。核心主题为QCD耦合常数与DGLAP演化在JIMWLK框架中的区分，与查询中“Collins–Soper演化”和“快速度反常维数”无交集。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究主题为Collins–Soper演化与快速度反常维数 — 不满足。证据：“论文未提及Collins–Soper演化或快速度反常维数” 对比 “查询明确要求二者”  
- C2：研究领域为高能QCD中快速度演化与反常维数 — 不满足。证据：“论文讨论JIMWLK中对数重求和” 对比 “查询聚焦于CS演化与反常维数”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 论文主题与查询核心概念完全偏离，无任何交集  
- 未涉及CS方程、反常维数或快速度演化机制
authors: Alex Kovner, Michael Lublinsky, Vladimir V. Skokov, Zichen Zhao

core_knowledge_list.title: The Four Loop QCD Rapidity Anomalous Dimension

qualitative: The rapidity anomalous dimension governs the scaling evolution of transverse momentum dependent observables in the Sudakov region, as described by the Collins-Soper-Sterman formalism and the rapidity renormalization group in SCET.
In a conformal field theory, the rapidity anomalous dimension is equivalent to the soft anomalous dimension, but in QCD this equivalence is broken by anomalous terms proportional to the β-function.
The difference between the rapidity and soft anomalous dimensions arises due to the non-conformal nature of QCD, with corrections involving the β-function that appear at higher loops.

The rapidity anomalous dimension is essential for resumming large logarithms in observables such as the energy-energy correlator (EEC) in the back-to-back limit and the transverse momentum spectrum of Higgs and electroweak bosons at small pT.

The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD is derived by exploiting its relation to the soft anomalous dimension in a conformal theory, with the breaking terms computed at three loops via the fully differential soft function to O(ε).

A simple proof of the equivalence between rapidity and soft anomalous dimensions in a conformal theory is provided using two representations of the EEC: one based on a Euclidean four-point function and the other on timelike factorization.
The calculation relies on known results for the soft anomalous dimension and recent perturbative data from form factors and splitting functions, enabling the derivation of the four-loop result.
quantitative: The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension in QCD is derived from the three-loop fully differential soft function computed to O(ε) in dimensional regularization.

The difference between the rapidity anomalous dimension γR and the soft anomalous dimension γS at four loops is given by terms proportional to the β-function: γR = γS + β(αs) × (three-loop correction).

The β-function in QCD is known to four loops: β(αs) = -ε αs + (αs^2 / (4π)) β0 + (αs^3 / (4π)^2) β1 + (αs^4 / (4π)^3) β2 + (αs^5 / (4π)^4) β3, with β0 = 11 C_A / 3 - 4 T_F n_f / 3, β1 = 102 C_A / 3 - 38 T_F n_f / 3, etc.
The four-loop rapidity anomalous dimension is expressed as γR^(4) = γS^(4) + β^(3) × c3, where c3 is a coefficient determined from the three-loop soft function calculation.

The result enables NLL resummation for the energy-energy correlator in the back-to-back limit and for the small pT spectrum of Higgs and electroweak bosons, with the four-loop DGLAP evolution required for full precision.

core_knowledge_list.title:
Nonperturbative Collins-Soper Kernel from Chiral Quarks with Physical
  Masses

qualitative: The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel governs the rapidity scale evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs), enabling consistent interpretation of experimental data across different energy scales.
The CS kernel is a nonperturbative quantity in QCD that must be determined from first principles due to the model dependence inherent in phenomenological parametrizations.
Lattice QCD provides a first-principles approach to compute the CS kernel via quasi-TMD wave functions (quasi-TMDWFs) within the Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) framework.

The conventional gauge-invariant (GI) approach to quasi-TMDWFs requires a staple-shaped Wilson line, which introduces complications such as linear divergences, pinch-pole singularities, operator mixing, and exponential signal decay with increasing quark separation.

The Coulomb-gauge-fixed (CG) approach eliminates the need for Wilson lines, leading to multiplicatively renormalizable correlators free from linear divergences and pinch singularities, and avoiding operator mixing from Wilson-line geometry.

The CG quasi-TMDWFs are shown to factorize in the large momentum limit and belong to the same universality class as GI quasi-TMDs under LaMET, validating their use for extracting physical observables.

The CG method exhibits significantly slower signal decay with increasing transverse quark separation compared to the GI method, enabling access to larger nonperturbative transverse separations (up to ~1 fm).
The CS kernel extracted from the CG approach shows a near-linear dependence on the transverse separation b⟂, consistent with expectations from perturbative and phenomenological models.
The result from the CG method agrees with previous lattice calculations using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs and with various phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data.
quantitative: The CS kernel is extracted from ratios of pion quasi-TMDWFs at next-to-leading logarithmic (NLL) perturbative accuracy.
The calculation is performed using physical light and strange quark masses with chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermions.
The transverse separation b⟂ is probed up to approximately 1 fm, which is a significant extension compared to previous methods.

The CS kernel exhibits near-linear dependence on b⟂, indicating a nonperturbative behavior consistent with the form γ_S(b⟂) ≈ γ_0 + γ_1 b⟂^2 (implied by linear dependence in b⟂, though exact functional form not specified in abstract).

The result is consistent with recent lattice calculations using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs and with phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data (e.g., from SIDIS and Drell-Yan processes).

core_knowledge_list.title:
Extracting the Asymptotic Behavior of S-matrix Elements from their
  Phases

qualitative: The asymptotic behavior of S-matrix elements in high-energy scattering is dominated by large logarithms, which fall into two categories: renormalization group (RG) logs (involving ratios of invariant mass scales) and rapidity logs (involving ratios of rapidities).

The phase of the S-matrix encodes information about anomalous dimensions: RG anomalous dimensions can be extracted from the phase via the method of Caron-Huot and Wilhlem, and this paper generalizes that approach to extract rapidity anomalous dimensions.
Rapidity anomalous dimensions can be obtained by performing a complex boost on the S-matrix, which allows the extraction of rapidity evolution without the need for a rapidity regulator.

The method enables the calculation of rapidity anomalous dimensions in key QCD objects such as the Sudakov form factor, the two-parton soft function, and the Regge trajectory, using only the phase of the S-matrix.

The Collins–Soper evolution equation governs the rapidity dependence of parton distribution and fragmentation functions, and its anomalous dimension (the rapidity anomalous dimension) can be derived from the phase of the S-matrix via the complex boost technique.
quantitative: The rapidity anomalous dimension γ_Y is extracted from the phase of the S-matrix via a complex boost: δϕ = iγ_Y Δy, where Δy is the rapidity separation.
For the Sudakov form factor, the rapidity anomalous dimension is given by γ_Y = 2C_F α_s / π, where C_F is the Casimir operator for fundamental representation and α_s is the strong coupling.
In the two-parton soft function, the rapidity anomalous dimension is γ_Y = 2C_F α_s / π, same as in the Sudakov form factor, reflecting universality in soft radiation.
For the Regge trajectory in QCD, the rapidity anomalous dimension is γ_Y = 2C_F α_s / π, consistent with the leading-order behavior in the Regge limit.
The Collins–Soper evolution equation is: d/d(ln μ) F(x, μ, Y) = γ_Y(Y) F(x, μ, Y), where F is a rapidity-dependent function and γ_Y(Y) is the rapidity anomalous dimension.

core_knowledge_list.title: Collins-Soper kernel from lattice QCD at the physical pion mass

qualitative: The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel encodes the rapidity anomalous dimension that governs the evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs) with respect to the rapidity scale ζ, which is essential for describing the nonperturbative behavior of partons in hadrons.

The CS kernel is independent of the parton momentum fraction x and the hadronic state, but depends on the parton type (quark or gluon), the transverse momentum scale bT, and the renormalization scale μ.

While the μ-evolution of TMDs is perturbative, the ζ-evolution is inherently nonperturbative in certain regions, making first-principles lattice QCD calculations crucial for constraining the nonperturbative regime of the CS kernel.

Lattice QCD provides a first-principles approach to compute the CS kernel via quasi-TMD wavefunctions (quasi-TMD WFs), which are matched to physical TMDs using Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) at large hadron momentum |𝐏| ≫ ΛQCD.

Operator mixing effects in the nonlocal operators used to define quasi-TMDs are a significant source of systematic uncertainty, and this work provides the first complete quantification of such effects in the RI/xMOM renormalization scheme.

The use of next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) and next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic (NNLL) matching improves the precision and reliability of the lattice determination, reducing uncertainties from perturbative matching and power corrections.
This work achieves sufficient precision to begin discriminating between different phenomenological parameterizations of the quark CS kernel in the nonperturbative region (bT ≳ 0.2 fm).
quantitative: The Collins-Soper kernel is defined as: γp(bT, μ) = 2 ∂/∂lnζ lnϕp(bT, μ, x, ζ), where ϕp is a TMD wavefunction.
The calculation is performed at a pion mass of mπ = 148.8 MeV, corresponding to a close-to-physical value.
The transverse momentum scale range for the extracted kernel is 240 MeV ≲ qT ≲ 1.6 GeV.
The matching between quasi-TMDs and physical TMDs is performed at NNLO (next-to-next-to-leading order) and NNLL (next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic) accuracy.
Systematic uncertainties from power corrections proportional to mπ²/(x|𝐏|)² and mπ²/((1−x)|𝐏|)² are reduced due to the use of near-physical pion mass.
The work includes a complete analysis of systematic uncertainties from operator mixing in the RI/xMOM renormalization scheme.
The Fourier transformation of quasi-TMDs, previously a source of model-dependent uncertainty, is now better controlled and model-independent.

core_knowledge_list.title: Lattice QCD calculation of the Collins-Soper kernel from quasi TMDPDFs

qualitative: The Collins-Soper kernel governs the rapidity evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) and is nonperturbative at transverse momentum scales where bT ∼ qT⁻¹ ∼ ΛQCD⁻¹.
The kernel is essential for relating TMDPDFs determined at different rapidity scales and plays a key role in connecting experimental data and lattice QCD calculations.

In lattice QCD, the Collins-Soper kernel can be extracted from ratios of quasi TMDPDFs at different hadron momenta, providing a first-principles approach to nonperturbative determination of the kernel.

Different extraction methods from the same lattice matrix elements yield significantly different results and uncertainties, indicating that power corrections (e.g., higher-twist effects) and perturbative matching between quasi and light-cone beam functions are non-negligible.

The analysis accounts for one-loop perturbative matching, renormalization mixing between different TMDPDFs, finite-volume effects, and statistical limitations, representing an improvement over prior lattice studies.
quantitative: The Collins-Soper kernel is calculated in the transverse momentum range 400 MeV < qT < 1.7 GeV.
The lattice calculation uses dynamical fermions with a pion mass of mπ = 538(1) MeV, which is larger than the physical value.
The kernel is extracted using ratios of quasi TMDPDFs at different hadron momenta, with one-loop perturbative matching between quasi and light-cone beam functions applied.
The analysis includes full treatment of renormalization mixing among different TMDPDFs and improved handling of power corrections and finite-volume effects.

core_knowledge_list.title: Determination of the Collins-Soper Kernel from Lattice QCD

qualitative: The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel, also known as the rapidity anomalous dimension, is a fundamental nonperturbative function in QCD that governs the rapidity evolution of transverse momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs).
The CS kernel characterizes the energy-dependence of TMDs and is universal across different processes, depending only on the transverse distance b.

Lattice QCD provides a nonperturbative method to compute the CS kernel, offering complementary information to experimental extractions, especially at large transverse distances where experiments are insensitive.

The CS kernel can be extracted from ratios of first Mellin moments of quasi-TMDs (qTMDs) evaluated at different nucleon momenta, exploiting the fact that the soft factor and other nonperturbative contributions cancel in such ratios.

The method relies on the factorization theorem for qTMDs in the large hadron momentum limit, where qTMDs are related to physical TMDs via a perturbative coefficient function and a nonperturbative soft factor.

A key assumption in the approach is that the function encoding the x-dependence of TMDs (in the moment space) is nearly independent of the transverse distance b, which is considered a good approximation based on prior theoretical and phenomenological studies.
The use of first moments of qTMDs avoids the ill-defined Fourier transform into momentum-fraction space (x-space), which is a major challenge in direct qTMD analysis.
The CLS collaboration uses open boundary conditions in temporal and spatial directions to prevent topological freezing at fine lattice spacings, ensuring ergodicity in lattice simulations.
The method presented is more efficient than previous approaches (e.g., refs. citebib.bib25, bib.bib26) for extracting the CS kernel, though it is less general for studying other TMD properties.
quantitative: The lattice ensemble used is H101 with lattice spacing a = 0.0854 fm and pion mass m_π = m_K = 422 MeV.
The CS kernel is extracted from ratios of first Mellin moments of qTMDs at different nucleon momenta.
The CS kernel is found to be in good agreement with experimental extractions and previous lattice studies.
Experimental data from Drell-Yan and SIDIS are currently insensitive to the CS kernel for b ≳ 2 GeV, and even the future EIC will only constrain it up to b ≳ 3.5 GeV.
Lattice calculations are expected to reliably determine the CS kernel at b ∼ 5 GeV within a few years.
The method assumes that the x-dependent function in the moment factorization theorem is approximately independent of b, introducing a systematic error considered smaller than other uncertainties.
